15.question: Which film produced in the UK was one of the most commercially successful films of all time and one of the highest-grossing film franchises?
15.reference: Some of the most commercially successful films of all time, including the two highest-grossing film franchises (Harry Potter and James Bond), have been produced in the UK.

15.question: When did the first farmers come to Britain?
15.reference: The first farmers arrived in Britain 6,000 years ago.

15.question: When did the First World War end?
15.reference: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

15.question: Can court orders be obtained to protect a person from being forced into a marriage?
15.reference: Court orders can be obtained to protect a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage.

15.question: What medal did Mary Peters win in the 1972 Olympics?
15.reference: Born in Manchester, Mary Peters moved to Northern Ireland as a child. She was a talented athlete who won an Olympic gold medal in the pentathlon in 1972.

answers.text: She did not win any medals

15.question: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland Youth Court cases are normally heard by (choose TWO answers)?
15.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 10 to 17, the case is normally heard in a Youth Court in front of up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge.

15.question: What was invented by Alan Turing in the 1930s?
15.reference: A Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54), a British mathematician, in the 1930s.

15.question: Which of the following tells us about how people lived in England just after the Norman Conquest?
15.reference: William sent people all over England to draw up lists of all the towns and villages. The people who lived there, who owned the land and what animals they owned were also listed. This was called the Domesday Book. It still exists today and gives a picture of society in England just after the Norman Conquest.

15.question: Who led a team of scientists to ‘split the atom’ for the first time?
15.reference: Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford, working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to ‘split the atom’ and took part in the Manhattan Project in the United States, which developed the atomic bomb.

15.question: What song is sung by people in the UK and other countries when they are celebrating the New Year?
15.reference: The song Auld Lang Syne, which is sung by people in the UK and other countries when they are celebrating the New Year (or Hogmanay as it is called in Scotland).

15.question: Who fought in the English Civil war of 1642?
15.reference: The Civil war between the king and Parliament began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

answers.text: The House of York and the House of Lancaster
The House of Fraser and the House of Stuart

15.question: Which two of these forts were part of Hadrian’s wall?
15.reference: Parts of Hadrian’s Wall, include the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda.

15.question: What was the religion of the Puritans?
15.reference: The Puritans were a group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship.

15.question: What is the name of Irish people who favoured complete independence from the UK in the 19th century?
15.reference: The Irish Nationalist movement had grown strongly through the 19th century. Some, such as the Fenians, favoured complete independence.

15.question: Which two British pop music groups were famous during the Swinging Sixties?
15.reference: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as the ‘swinging sixties’. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music. Two well-known pop music groups at the time were The Beatles and The Rolling Stones.

15.question: What is the minimum age required to drive a motorcycle?
15.reference: In the UK, you must be at least 17 years to drive a car or motorcycle and you must have a driving licence to drive on public roads.

15.question: The members of the House of Lords, known as peers, are elected by the people.
15.reference: The members of the House of Lords, known as peers, are not elected by the people.

15.question: When did the Emancipation Act abolish slavery throughout the British Empire?
15.reference: In 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

15.question: Who is responsible for crime, policing and immigration?
15.reference: The Home Secretary is responsible for crime, policing and immigration.

15.question: What kind of genre was the film Wallace and Gromit created by Nick Park?
15.reference: Nick Park won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.

15.question: Anyone who is violent towards their partner – whether they are a man or a woman, married or living together – can be prosecuted.
15.reference: Anyone who is violent towards their partner – whether they are a man or a woman, married or living together – can be prosecuted.

16.question: When was the United Nations set up?
16.reference: The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security.

16.question: Where is the Giant’s Causeway located?
16.reference: The Giant’s Causeway is a land formation of columns made from volcanic lava located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland. It was formed about 50 million years ago. There are many legends about the Causeway and how it was formed.

16.question: What happened to Mary, Queen of Scots, after being sent to prison for 20 years by her cousin Queen Elizabeth I?
16.reference: Mary was executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I.

16.question: Which tribe came to Britain from northern Europe after the Romans left in AD 410?
16.reference: Britain was invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons.

16.question: What is one of the highest-grossing film franchises produced in the UK?
16.reference: Some of the most commercially successful films of all time, including the two highest-grossing film franchises (Harry Potter and James Bond), have been produced in the UK.

16.question: Who can hear cases in Youth Courts in England, Wales and Northern Ireland (two answers)?
16.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 10 to 17, the case is normally heard in a Youth Court in front of up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge. The most serious cases will go the Crown Court. The parents or carers of the young person are expected to attend the hearing. Members of the public are not allowed in Youth Courts, and the name or photographs of the accused young person cannot be published in newspapers or used by the media.

16.question: Who came from the Netherlands to become the British monarch?
16.reference: In May 1660, Parliament invited Charles II to come back from exile in the Netherlands. He was crowned King Charles II of England, Wales, Scotland and Ireland.

16.question: Who can apply for the National Citizen Service programme?
16.reference: The National Citizen Service programme gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project.

16.question: Nick Park has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring:
16.reference: Nick Park, who has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.

16.question: Who was the British scientist who co-discovered the structure of the DNA molecule in the 1950s?
16.reference: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. This discovery contributed to many scientific advances, particularly in medicine and fighting crime. Francis Crick (1916-2004), was one of those awarded the Nobel Prize for this discovery.

16.question: What percentage of the UK population has a parent or grandparent born outside of the UK?
16.reference: Nearly 10% of the population has a parent or grandparent born outside the UK.

16.question: How old do you have to be to drive a moped in the UK?
16.reference: In the UK, you need to be at least 16 years old to ride a moped, and there are other age requirements and special tests for driving large vehicles.

16.question: Who was the leader of the Labour Party who introduced a Scottish Parliament and a Welsh Assembly?
16.reference: In 1997 the Labour Party led by Tony Blair was elected. The Blair government introduced a Scottish Parliament and a Welsh Assembly.

16.question: What is the maximum amount you can claim through the small claims procedure in Scotland and Northern Ireland?
16.reference: The small claims procedure is an informal way of helping people to settle minor disputes without spending a lot of time and money using a lawyer. This procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales and £3,000 in Scotland and Northern Ireland.

16.question: Which historical monument did William the Conqueror build?
16.reference: The White Tower in the Tower of London is an example of a Norman castle keep, built on the orders of William the Conqueror.

16.question: Who became Prime Minister after Margaret Thatcher and played an important part in the Northern Ireland peace process?
16.reference: John Major was Prime Minister after Mrs Thatcher, and helped establish the Northern Ireland peace process.

16.question: Who should you contact to report a terrorist activity?
16.reference: If you think someone is trying to persuade you to join an extremist or terrorist cause, you should notify your local police force.

16.question: Which Paralympic athlete has won gold medals for swimming at the 2008, 2012 and 2016 Paralympic Games?
16.reference: Ellie Simmonds is a Paralympian who won gold medals for swimming at the 2008, 2012 and 2016 Paralympic Games and holds a number of world records. She was the youngest member of the British team at the 2008 Games.

16.question: Each of the UK countries is associated with a flower symbol, which country is associated with the rose?
16.reference: The countries that make up the UK all have flowers which are particularly associated with them and which are sometimes worn on national saints’ days. The flowers associated to each country are as follows: England – the rose, Scotland – the thistle, Wales – the daffodil and Northern Ireland – the shamrock.

16.question: Isambard Kingdom Brunel was responsible for:
16.reference: Brunel was originally from Portsmouth, England. He was an engineer who built tunnels, bridges, railway lines and ships. He was responsible for constructing the Great Western Railway, which was the first major railway built in Britain. It runs from Paddington Station in London to the south west of England, the West Midlands and Wales.

answers.text: The construction of the Great Western Railway.
The construction of the Tower of London.

16.question: What is the Commonwealth?
16.reference: The Commonwealth is an association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development. Most member states were once part of the British Empire, although a few countries which were not have also joined.

answers.text: An organization responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in its member countries.
A group of European and North American countries that have agreed to help each other if they come under attack.
An association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development.
An international organization that aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security.

16.question: Under which name was the movement of opinion against the authority of the Pope during the times of Henry VIII known?
16.reference: The correct answer is the Reformation.

16.question: What did Isaac Newton discover?
16.reference: Newton’s most famous published work was Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica (‘Mathematical Principle of Natural Philosophy’), which showed how gravity applied to the whole universe. Newton also discovered that white light is made up of the colours of the rainbow.

16.question: What kind of movies did Academy Award winner Nick Park specialise in?
16.reference: Britain continues to be particularly strong in special effects and animation. One example is the work of Nick Park, who has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.

17.question: Which of the following is not a constitutional institution?
17.reference: Constitutional institutions in the UK include: the monarchy, the Parliament (the House of Commons and the House of Lords), the Prime Minister, the cabinet, the judiciary (courts), the police, the civil service and the local government. The armed forces are not a constitutional institution.

17.question: What food shortage caused the famine in Ireland?
17.reference: In the middle of the century the potato crop failed, and Ireland suffered a famine. A million people died from disease and starvation.

17.question: The leader of the opposition appoints senior opposition MPs. They form a group whose role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies. What are they called?
17.reference: The leader of the opposition appoints senior opposition MPs to be ‘shadow ministers’. Their role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies. They form the shadow cabinet.

17.question: Which of the following are TWO responsibilities of the MPs?
17.reference: MPs responsibilities include: to represent everyone in their constituency, to help to create new laws, to scrutinise and comment on what the government is doing and to debate important national issues.

answers.text: Scrutinise and comment on what the monarch is doing

17.question: Who was the longest serving PM in the 20th century?
17.reference: Margaret Thatcher was the first woman Prime Minister of the UK. She was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century, remaining in office from 1979 until 1990.

17.question: What name is given to the territories which are linked to the UK but not part of it?
17.reference: There are several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called ‘Crown dependencies’.

17.question: What area did David Hume contribute to during the Enlightenment?
17.reference: David Hume was a famous philosopher who developed ideas about human nature during the 18th century and that have continued to influence philosophers over the years.

17.question: What proportion of the population died as a result of the Black Death in England?
17.reference: As a result of the Black Death, one third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales.

17.question: What happened to Charles I after he was in prison?
17.reference: Charles I’s army was defeated at the Battles of Marston Moor and Naseby. Charles was held prisoner by the parliamentary army. As he was unwilling to reach any agreement with the Parliament, in 1649 he was executed.

17.question: What are TWO responsibilities of the monarch?
17.reference: The monarch does not rule the country but appoints the government, which the people have chosen in a democratic election. The monarch also has regular meetings with the Prime Minister and can advise, warn and encourage, but the decisions on government policies are made by the Prime Minister and cabinet.

answers.text: To appoint the members of the cabinet
To advise, warn and encourage the Prime Minister\'s decisions on government policies

17.question: What kind of disaster was the Black Death?
17.reference: The Black Death was a disease, probably a form of plague, that came to Britain in 1348.

17.question: What country was in conflict with the UK for trading reasons during the 18th century?
17.reference: During the 18th century, trading and settlements overseas sometimes brought Britain into conflict with other countries, particularly France, which was expanding and trading in a similar way in many of the same areas of the world.

17.question: The Speaker is an MP, he or she represents a constituency and deals with the constituents.
17.reference: This statement is true.

17.question: Where is Skara Brae, the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, located?
17.reference: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe.

17.question: Which of the following plays was written by William Shakespeare?
17.reference: William Shakespeare’s most famous plays include MacBeth.

17.question: Where was William the Conqueror from?
17.reference: William the Conqueror was the Duke of Normandy (now northern France). He defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings in 1066.

17.question: How do you call the British who went to war in South Africa with settlers from the Netherlands?
17.reference: The British went to war in South Africa with settlers from the Netherlands called the Boers. The Boers fought fiercely and the war went on for over three years.

17.question: In Northern Ireland, up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge are required for Youth Court cases.
17.reference: This statement is true.

17.question: What made Roger Banister famous?
17.reference: Sir Roger Bannister was the first man in the world to run a mile in under four minutes, in 1954.

answers.text: He was the first man in the world to run a mile in under four minutes.
He won gold medals for ice dancing at the Olympic Games in 1984.
He won gold medals in rowing in five consecutive Olympic Games.
He was the first British to climb Mount Everest.

17.question: Who was invated to invade and rule England in 1688?
17.reference: In 1688, important Protestants in England asked William of Orange to invade England and proclaim himself king.

17.question: Who was defeated at the Battle of the Boyne?
17.reference: William of Orange defeated James II at the Battle of the Boyne in Ireland in 1690.

17.question: What did the Factories Act of 1847 introduce?
17.reference: The Factories Act of 1847 limited the number of hours that women and children could work to 10 hours per day.

answers.text: It limited the number of hours that everybody could work to 10 hours per day.
It increased the number of hours that women and children could work to 12 hours per day.
It increased the number of hours that men could work to 12 hours per day.
It limited the number of hours that women and children could work to 10 hours per day.

17.question: What name is given to the group of MPs from the opposition?
17.reference: Opposition MPs form the shadow cabinet and their role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies.

17.question: In which of the following matters can the Scottish Parliament legislate?
17.reference: The Scottish Parliament can legislate on: civil and criminal law, health, education, planning and additional tax-raising powers.

18.question: What are Members of Parliament (MPs) called if they do not represent any of the main political parties
18.reference:
There are a few MPs who do not represent any of the main political parties. They are called ‘independents’ and usually represent an issue important to their
constituency.

18.question: Which notable British sportsman is described as a British Grand Prix driver who has won the Formula 1 World Championship and is listed as a recent British winner of this championship?
Who opened the first curry house in London?
Damon Hill, Lewis Hamilton and Jenson Button are known for which sports
What is the limit for small claims court in Northern Ireland and Scotland?
What was Sir Edward Elgar primarily famous for?

answers.text: Composing the Pomp and Circumstance Marches
Writing the Harry Potter series of books

18.question: Where in the UK government sitting?
During 18 Century, what important role appeared in the parliament?

answers.text: Speaker of the House of Commons

18.question: Which tribe came to Britain from northern Europe after the Romans left in AD410?

Board games, poker, and arcade machines

The judge of magistrate court in Northern Ireland is legally qualified
18.reference: In Northern Ireland, cases are heard by a District Judge or Deputy District Judge, who is legally qualified and paid.

Arranged marriages, where both parties agree to the marriage, are acceptable in the UK ?
Is Scotland Parliament located in Glasgow?
Do I need a TV license if I only watch TV on a computer?

Co-inventing the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner

18.question: What is the correct order of the Patron Saints' Days?

St David's Day, St Patrick's Day, St George's Day, St Andrew's Day

Britizen 1.question: Which country's flag is not part of the Union Flag?
Britizen 1.reference: The Welsh dragon is not part of the Union Flag.

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: What is the judiciary responsible for?
Britizen 1.reference: Judges, who are together called 'the judiciary', are responsible for interpreting the law and ensuring that trials are conducted fairly.

answers.text: Deciding whether a person is guilty

Britizen 1.question: When did the Habeas Corpus Act become law?
Britizen 1.reference: The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679. This was a very important piece of legislation which remains relevant today. Habeas corpus is Latin for 'you must present the person in court'. The Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully. Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

Britizen 1.question: Which of the events is the bombing of English cities by the Germans?
Britizen 1.reference: The Blitz was the German air force bombing London and other British cities at night-time. The phrase 'the Blitz spirit' is still used today to describe Britons pulling together in the face of adversity.

Britizen 1.question: Why is it called 'The Glorious Revolution'?
Britizen 1.reference: This event was later called the 'Glorious Revolution' because there was no fighting in England and because it guaranteed the power of Parliament, ending the threat of a monarch ruling on his or her own as he or she wished.

answers.text: Women were given the right to vote

Britizen 1.question: Which of these venues is located in Greenwich?
Britizen 1.reference: There are many large venues that host music events throughout the year, such as: The O2 in Greenwich and the Scottish Exhibition and Conference Centre (SECC) in Glasgow.

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: When did the UK leave the EU following the Brexit Vote?
Britizen 1.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973. After UK's Brexit vote in 2020, there are now 27 EU member states.

Britizen 1.question: When was The First British Prime minister (Sir Robert Walpole) in power?
Britizen 1.reference: The first man to be called 'Prime Minister' was Sir Robert Walpole, who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

Britizen 1.question: Which country's national flower is a thistle?
Britizen 1.reference: A thistle is a national flower of Scotland.

Britizen 1.question: Who developed ideas about economics during the Enlightenment period which are still referred to today?
Britizen 1.reference: Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish. Adam Smith developed ideas about economics which are still referred to today. David Hume's ideas about human nature continue to influence philosophers. Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt's work on steam power, helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: One TV licence covers all of the equipment at one address, but people who rent different rooms in a shared house must buy a separate TV licence
Britizen 1.reference: One TV licence covers all of the equipment at one address, but people who rent different rooms in a shared house must buy a separate TV licence

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of what?
Britizen 1.reference: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of the local community. In England, Wales and Scotland they usually work unpaid and do not need legal qualifications. They receive training to do the job and are supported by a legal adviser.

Britizen 1.question: A 2 minutes silence is observed on Easter to commemorate the death of Jesus Christ
Britizen 1.reference: Remembrance Day, 11 November, commemorates those who died fighting for the UK and its allies. Originally it commemorated the dead of the First World War, which ended on 11 November 1918. People wear poppies (the red flower found on the battlefields of the First World War). At 11.00 am there is a two-minute silence and wreaths are laid at the Cenotaph in Whitehall, London.

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: Peers nominated by the Prime Minister can only serve for their lifetime
Britizen 1.reference: Since 1958, the Prime Minister has had the power to nominate peers just for their own lifetime. These are called life peers. They have usually had an important career in politics, business, law or another profession. Life peers are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister.

Britizen 1.question: Which TWO countries took part in the Battle of Agincourt?
Britizen 1.reference: English kings also fought a long war with France, called the Hundred Years War (even though it actually lasted 116 years). One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V's vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

Britizen 1.question: Who is credited with the introduction of the National Health Service (NHS) in 1948?
Britizen 1.reference: In 1948, Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS), which guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: The Speaker is an MP, he/she represents a constituency and deals with constituents' problems like any other MP
Britizen 1.reference: The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with constituents' problems like any other MP.

Britizen 1.question: What religion were Puritans?
Britizen 1.reference: Puritans, a group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship.

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: Who can nominate life peers? (Select TWO)
Britizen 1.reference: Since 1958, the Prime Minister has had the power to nominate peers just for their own lifetime. These are called life peers. Life peers are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister. They also include people nominated by the leaders of the other main political parties or by an independent Appointments Commission for non-party peers.

answers.text: Leaders of other main political parties

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: What country did Hitler invade in 1939?
Britizen 1.reference: The British government tried to avoid another war. However, when Hitler invaded Poland in 1939, Britain and France declared war in order to stop his aggression.

Britizen 1.question: Name the most famous cricket competition, which is a series of Test matches played between England and Australia
Britizen 1.reference: The most famous competition is the Ashes, which is a series of Test matches played between England and Australia.

Britizen 1.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 1.question: In England & Wales, the small claims procedure is an informal way of helping people to settle minor disputes claims of less than?
Britizen 1.reference: The small claims procedure is an informal way of helping people to settle minor disputes without spending a lot of time and money using a lawyer. This procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales; £5,000 in Scotland and £5,000 in Northern Ireland (Changed from £3000).

Britizen 1.question: Which tribes invaded Britain after the Romans had left?
Britizen 1.reference: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned. Britain was again invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons.

Britizen 2.question: Roast beef is a traditional food of which country?
Britizen 2.reference: Roast beef, which is served with potatoes is an example of English traditional food.

Britizen 2.question: Who can get a 50% discount on their TV licence?
Britizen 2.reference: People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount.

Britizen 2.question: Which languages were the basis of modern English?
Britizen 2.reference: After the Norman Conquest, the king and his noblemen had spoken Norman French and the peasants had continued to speak Anglo-Saxon. Gradually these two languages combined to become one English language.

Britizen 2.question: When did the first farmers arrive in Britain?
Britizen 2.reference: The first farmers arrived in Britain about 6,000 years ago. The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe. These people built houses, tombs and monuments on the land.

Britizen 2.question: Who was the 'Iron Duke' that defeated the Emperor Napoleon?
Britizen 2.reference: In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo. Wellington was known as the Iron Duke and later became Prime Minister.

Britizen 2.question: When did the Irish Free State become a republic?
Britizen 2.reference: In 1922 Ireland became two countries. The six counties in the north which were mainly Protestant remained part of the UK under the name Northern Ireland. The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State. It had its own government and became a republic in 1949.

Britizen 2.question: Ulster fry is a traditional food of which country?
Britizen 2.reference: Northern Ireland's traditional food is 'Ulster fry' - a fried meal with bacon, eggs, sausage, black pudding, white pudding, tomatoes, mushrooms, soda bread and potato bread.

Britizen 2.question: Who tells visitors about the history of the Tower of London?
Britizen 2.reference: The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror after he became king in 1066. Tours are given by the Yeoman Warders, also known as Beefeaters, who tell visitors about the building's history. People can also see the Crown Jewels there.

Britizen 2.question: Which TWO wives of Henry VIII were executed?
Britizen 2.reference: Anne Boleyn was English. She and Henry had one daughter, Elizabeth. Anne was unpopular in the country and was accused of taking lovers. She was executed at the Tower of London. Catherine was a cousin of Anne Boleyn. She was also accused of taking lovers and executed.

Britizen 2.question: When did Ireland split into two countries?
Britizen 2.reference: In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries. The six counties in the north which were mainly Protestant remained part of the UK under the name Northern Ireland. The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State. It had its own government and became a republic in 1949.

Britizen 2.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 2.question: Volunteering is compulsory in the UK
Britizen 2.reference: Volunteering is working for good causes without payment. It is not compulsory.

Britizen 2.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 2.question: Nowadays, the House of Commons has more power than the House of Lords
Britizen 2.reference: The House of Commons has powers to overrule the House of Lords, but these are not used often.

Britizen 2.question: Select TWO popular social networking websites
Britizen 2.reference: Social networking websites such as Facebook and Twitter are a popular way for people to stay in touch with friends, organise social events, and share photos, videos and opinions. Many people use social networking on their mobile phones when out and about.

Britizen 2.question: What can 'Carry on' be associated with?
Britizen 2.reference: The 1950s and 1960s were a high point for British comedies, including Passport to Pimlico, The Ladykillers and, later, the Carry On films.

Britizen 2.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 2.question: How can you make a complaint about the Police? (Choose TWO)
Britizen 2.reference: Anyone can make a complaint about the police by going to a police station or writing to the Chief Constable of the police force involved.

answers.text: Writing to the Chief Constable of the police force involved

Britizen 2.question: Sir Edward Elgar is known for his famous music work - 'The Planets'
Britizen 2.reference: More recently, important composers include Gustav Holst (1874-1934), whose work includes The Planets, a suite of pieces themed around the planets of the solar system. He adapted Jupiter, part of the Planets suite, as the tune for I vow to thee my country, a popular hymn in British churches.

Britizen 2.question: What were the first Women's Social and Political Union group members called?
Britizen 2.reference: Emmeline Pankhurst (1858-1928) was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the Women's Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. In 1903 she helped found the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU). This was the first group whose members were called 'suffragettes'.

Britizen 2.question: What was the system of land ownership used by the Normans known as?
Britizen 2.reference: The Normans used a system of land ownership known as feudalism. The king gave land to his lords in return for help in war. Landowners had to send certain numbers of men to serve in the army. Some peasants had their own land but most were serfs.

Britizen 2.question: Who can only give 'advice, warn and encourage' about government matters?
Britizen 2.reference: The monarch has regular meetings with the Prime Minister and can advice, warn and encourage, but the decisions on government policies are made by the Prime Minister and cabinet

Britizen 2.question: What awards event celebrates British theatre?
Britizen 2.reference: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London. There are a variety of categories, including best director, best actor and best actress. The awards are named after the British actor Sir Laurence Olivier, later Lord Olivier, who was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays.

Britizen 2.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 2.question: It is a legal requirement that men and women should not be discriminated against due to their gender or marital status
Britizen 2.reference: UK laws ensure that people are not treated unfairly in any area of life or work because of their age, disability, sex, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sexuality or marital status.

Britizen 2.question: Catherine Howard was Anne Boleyn's cousin
Britizen 2.reference: Catherine was a cousin of Anne Boleyn. She was also accused of taking lovers and executed.

Britizen 2.question: Who is known as William the Conqueror?
Britizen 2.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror.

Britizen 3.question: What did Henry VII do?
Britizen 3.reference: Henry VII wanted to make sure that England remained peaceful and that his position as king was secure. He deliberately strengthened the central administration of England and reduced the power of the nobles.

answers.text: Broke away from the Church of Rome
Reduced the power of the nobles
Increased the power of the nobles

Britizen 3.question: Which of these is not a music festival?
Britizen 3.reference: The Edinburgh Festival takes place in Edinburgh, Scotland, every summer. The Fringe (One of the festivals) is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances.

Britizen 3.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 3.question: Who is responsible for crime and immigration?
Britizen 3.reference: Home Secretary is responsible for crime, policing and immigration.

Britizen 3.question: When did the English civil war begin?
Britizen 3.reference: Civil war between the king and Parliament could not now be avoided and began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

Britizen 3.question: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.
Britizen 3.reference: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

Britizen 3.question: When were the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms established in England?
Britizen 3.reference: By about AD 600, Anglo-Saxon kingdoms were established in Britain. These kingdoms were mainly in what is now England.

Britizen 3.question: The Scottish Parliament (after the Magna Carta was established in England) was split into two estates: the Lords and the Commons
Britizen 3.reference: In England, the nobility, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords. Knights, who were usually smaller landowners, and wealthy people from towns and cities were elected to sit in the House of Commons. Only a small part of the population was able to join in electing the members of the Commons. In Scotland, the parliament had three Houses, called Estates: the Lords, the Commons and the Clergy.

Britizen 3.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 3.question: What is the Shadow Cabinet?
Britizen 3.reference: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government's failures and weaknesses. The leader of the opposition also appoints senior opposition MPs to be 'shadow ministers'. They form the shadow cabinet and their role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies.

answers.text: The cabinet that is in trouble
The cabinet where the Prime Minister had to resign
Senior members of the main opposition party who 'shadow' Government ministers in different departments
The name of the office of the Leader of the Opposition in the Parliament

Britizen 3.question: Who wrote about their experiences in the First World War?
Britizen 3.reference: Wilfred Owen and Siegfried Sassoon - were inspired to write about their experiences in the First World War.

Britizen 3.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 3.question: Discrimination in the workplace is a criminal offence
Britizen 3.reference: An example of civil laws are disputes over wages and cases of unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace.

Britizen 3.question: What is the Giant's Causeway made of?
Britizen 3.reference: Located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland, the Giant's Causeway is a land formation of columns made from volcanic lava. It was formed about 50 million years ago. There are many legends about the Causeway and how it was formed.

Britizen 3.question: Conditions in Ireland in the 19th century were much better than in the rest of the UK
Britizen 3.reference: Conditions in Ireland were not as good as in the rest of the UK. Two-thirds of the population still depended on farming to make their living, often on very small plots of land. Many depended on potatoes as a large part of their diet. In the middle of the century the potato crop failed, and Ireland suffered a famine. A million people died from disease and starvation.

Britizen 3.question: Radar was developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt
Britizen 3.reference: Radar was developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt (1892-1973), who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.

Britizen 3.question: When did the BBC begin the world's first regular TV service?
Britizen 3.reference: The BBC started radio broadcasts in 1922 and began the world's first regular television service in 1936.

Britizen 3.question: Satirical magazines began to be published in the 19th century. When was 'Punch' first published?
Britizen 3.reference: Punch' was published for the first time in the 1840s

Britizen 3.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 3.question: What destroyed London in year 1666?
Britizen 3.reference: During Charles II's reign, in 1665, there was a major outbreak of plague in London. Thousands of people died, especially in poorer areas. The following year, a great fire destroyed much of the city, including many churches and St Paul's Cathedral.

Britizen 3.question: Other than London universities, which university was credited with the discovery of the DNA molecule in 1953?
Britizen 3.reference: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. This discovery contributed to many scientific advances, particularly in medicine and fighting crime. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize for this discovery, was British.

Britizen 3.question: During the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, where did the English government encourage Scottish and English Protestants to settle?
Britizen 3.reference: During the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, many people in Ireland opposed rule by the Protestant government in England. There were a number of rebellions. The English government encouraged Scottish and English Protestants to settle in Ulster, the northern province of Ireland, taking over the land from Catholic landholders. These settlements were known as plantations.

Britizen 3.question: Which architectural style are the famous London buildings of the 19th century, such as the House of Parliament and St Pancras Station, associated with?
Britizen 3.reference: In the 19th century, the medieval 'gothic' style became popular again. As cities expanded, many great public buildings were built in this style. The Houses of Parliament and St Pancras Station were built at this time, as were the town halls in cities such as Manchester and Sheffield.

Britizen 3.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 3.question: What can the Laws made by the EU also be called?
Britizen 3.reference: EU law is legally binding in the UK and all the other EU member states. European laws are called directives, regulations or framework decisions.

Britizen 3.question: Which TWO things happened when Mary Stuart's husband got murdered?
Britizen 3.reference: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called 'Mary, Queen of Scots') was a Catholic. She was only a week old when her father died and she became queen. Much of her childhood was spent in France. When she returned to Scotland, she was the centre of a power struggle between different groups. When her husband was murdered, Mary was suspected of involvement and fled to England. She gave her throne to her Protestant son, James VI of Scotland. Mary was Elizabeth I's cousin and hoped that Elizabeth might help her, but Elizabeth suspected Mary of wanting to take over the English throne, and kept her a prisoner for 20 years. Mary was eventually executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I.

answers.text: She gave her throne to Elizabeth I
She was suspected of involvement in the murder
She asked Elizabeth I for help

Britizen 3.question: Whom did the serfs serve?
Britizen 3.reference: The Normans used a system of land ownership known as feudalism. The king gave land to his lords in return for help in war. Landowners had to send certain numbers of men to serve in the army. Some peasants had their own land but most were serfs.

Britizen 3.question: The Victoria Cross medal was introduced during which war?
Britizen 3.reference: Queen Victoria introduced the Victoria Cross medal during the Crimean war. It honours acts of valour by soldiers.

Britizen 3.question: What is the 'Divine Right of Kings?
Britizen 3.reference: Divine Right of Kings' is the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule. The king should be able to act without having to seek approval from Parliament.

answers.text: The King was Appointed by God
Reduced the power of the nobles
Reduced the power of the King

Britizen 4.question: Which of these UK landmarks is in Wales?
Britizen 4.reference: Snowdonia is a national park in North Wales. It covers an area of 838 square miles. Its most well-known landmark is Snowdon, which is the highest mountain in Wales.

Britizen 4.question: What's the age requirement to drink wine with a meal with someone over 18?
Britizen 4.reference: 16 year olds can drink wine or beer with a meal in a hotel or restaurant as long as they are with someone over 18.

Britizen 4.question: Who tried to arrest 5 parliamentary leaders from the House of Commons?
Britizen 4.reference: Charles I entered the House of Commons and tried to arrest five parliamentary leaders, but they had been warned and were not there. This led to the English Civil war in 1642.

Britizen 4.question: When did Protestant ideas begin to spread?
Britizen 4.reference: Protestant ideas gradually gained strength in England, Wales and Scotland during the 16th century.

Britizen 4.question: What yearly event happens between Oxford and Cambridge Universities?
Britizen 4.reference: Rowing is also popular, both as a leisure activity and as a competitive sport. There is a popular yearly race on the Thames between Oxford and Cambridge Universities.

Britizen 4.question: As per 2021 census, what percentage of the population in England and Wales doesn't follow a religion?
Britizen 4.reference: The 2021 Census results released so far show 37% of people in England and Wales (from a total sampled 60 million) stated not to follow any religion. 46% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), while 0.5% of people followed another religion.

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: People living in the UK must pay tax on which of the following TWO?
Britizen 4.reference: People in the UK have to pay tax on their income, which includes: wages from paid employment; profits from self-employment, taxable benefits; pensions; income from property, savings and dividends.

answers.text: Income from property, savings and dividends

Britizen 4.question: Where were the first farmers that came to Britain from?
Britizen 4.reference: The first farmers arrived in Britain about 6,000 years ago. The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe.

Britizen 4.question: D-Day is the day when the British evacuated the French from Normandy
Britizen 4.reference: On 6 June 1944, allied forces landed in Normandy (this event is often referred to as 'D-Day').

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: Which TWO are Police duties?
Britizen 4.reference: The job of the police in the UK is to: protect life and property; prevent disturbances (also known as keeping the peace); prevent and detect crime.

Britizen 4.question: Where was Isaac Newton born?
Britizen 4.reference: Born in Lincolnshire, eastern England, Isaac Newton first became interested in science when he studied at Cambridge University.

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: How many countries make up the EU?
Britizen 4.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973. After the Brexit vote to leave in 2020, there are now 27 EU member states

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: Children's Hearings system is used in which country?
Britizen 4.reference: In Scotland a system called the Children's Hearings System is used to deal with children and young people who have committed an offence. Northern Ireland has a system of youth conferencing to consider how a child should be dealt with when they have committed an offence.

Britizen 4.question: During the Bronze age people lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs called round barrows
Britizen 4.reference: The statement is true

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: What party won the General Election in 2010?
Britizen 4.reference: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister.

answers.text: None, Liberal Democrats and Conservative party formed a Coalition

Britizen 4.question: Which TWO did the Romans establish in Britain?
Britizen 4.reference: The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years. They built roads and public buildings, created a structure of law, and introduced new plants and animals

Britizen 4.question: Robert the Bruce is associated with which country?
Britizen 4.reference: In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn, and Scotland remained unconquered by the English.

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: What system can be used to complete a tax return if you are self-employed?
Britizen 4.reference: For most people, the right amount of income tax is automatically taken from their income from employment by their employer and paid directly to HM Revenue Customs (HMRC), the government department that collects taxes. This system is called 'Pay As You Earn' (PAYE). If you are self-employed, you need to pay your own tax through a system called 'self-assessment', which includes completing a tax return.

Britizen 4.question: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of which river?
Britizen 4.reference: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of the River Thames and is a Ferris wheel that is 443 feet (135 metres) tall. It was originally built as part of the UK's celebration of the new millennium and continues to be an important part of New Year celebrations.

Britizen 4.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 4.question: NSPCC is a charity for which of the following?
Britizen 4.reference: Children - the National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC); The homeless - Crisis and Shelter; Medical research charities - Cancer Research UK; Environmental charities- the National Trust and Friends of the Earth; Animals - People's Dispensary for Sick Animals (PDSA).

Britizen 4.question: After the Black Death, a new social class appeared, - owners of large areas of land. What were they called?
Britizen 4.reference: New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land called 'the gentry', and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

Britizen 4.question: The Chartists are associated with the Civil War which took place in the 17th century
Britizen 4.reference: In the 1830s and 1840s, a group called the Chartists campaigned for reform. They wanted six changes: for every man to have the vote; elections every year; for all regions to be equal in the electoral system; secret ballots; for any man to be able to stand as an MP; for MPs to be paid.

Britizen 4.question: Mothering Sunday is the third Sunday in June
Britizen 4.reference: Mothering Sunday (or Mother's Day) is the Sunday three weeks before Easter. Children send cards or buy gifts for their mothers.

Britizen 4.question: In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. What was it called?
Britizen 4.reference: In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. This was known as the Black Death. One third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales.

Britizen 5.question: Hadrian's Wall is a popular UNESCO World Heritage Site
Britizen 5.reference: Parts of Hadrian's Wall, including the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda, can still be seen. It is a popular area for walkers and is a UNESCO (United Nations Education, Scientific and Cultural Organization) World Heritage Site.

Britizen 5.question: When did English become the official language for documents?
Britizen 5.reference: By 1400, in England, official documents were being written in English, and English had become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament.

Britizen 5.question: What percentage of the total UK population lives in Wales?
Britizen 5.reference: UK population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. Wales makes around 5%.

Britizen 5.question: Who developed a radar?
Britizen 5.reference: Radar was developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt (1892-1973), who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.

Britizen 5.question: What is the capital of Northern Ireland?
Britizen 5.reference: Belfast is the capital city of Northern Ireland.

Britizen 5.question: As per 2021 survey, what percentage of Wales and England identified as Muslim?
Britizen 5.reference: Islam is the second largest religion in the United Kingdom. The 2021 Census results released so far show a population of 3,868,133 (6.5%) in England and Wales (from a total sampled 60 million). 46% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), 0.5% of people followed another religion and 37% stated not to follow any religion.

Britizen 5.question: The Civil War in 1642 split the country into which two groups?
Britizen 5.reference: Civil war between the king and Parliament could not now be avoided and began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

answers.text: House of Commons and House of Lords
House of York and House of Lancaster

Britizen 5.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 5.question: The small claims procedure in Northern Ireland is used for claims of less than what amount?
Britizen 5.reference: The small claims procedure is an informal way of helping people to settle minor disputes without spending a lot of time and money using a lawyer. This procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales; £5,000 in Scotland and £5,000 in Northern Ireland (Changed from £3000).

Britizen 5.question: Where was Emmeline Pankhurst born?
Britizen 5.reference: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the Women's Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women.

Britizen 5.question: Which of these was said by Winston Churchill?
Britizen 5.reference: All of these are examples of his speeches.

answers.text: I have nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears and sweat' only
Never in the field of human conflict was so much owed by so many to so few' only

Britizen 5.question: A 60 second silence is observed on the Remembrance Day
Britizen 5.reference: Remembrance Day, 11 November, commemorates those who died fighting for the UK and its allies. Originally it commemorated the dead of the First World War, which ended on 11 November 1918. People wear poppies (the red flower found on the battlefields of the First World War). At 11.00 am there is a two-minute silence and wreaths are laid at the Cenotaph in Whitehall, London.

Britizen 5.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 5.question: The European Convention on Human Rights is incorporated into UK law
Britizen 5.reference: The Human Rights Act 1998 incorporated the European Convention on Human Rights into UK law. The government, public bodies and the courts must follow the principles of the Convention.

Britizen 5.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 5.question: What should all new citizens do as part of the citizenship ceremony?
Britizen 5.reference: New citizens swear or affirm loyalty to the Queen as part of the citizenship ceremony.

answers.text: Swear or affirm loyalty to the King (or Queen)
Prepare and make an individual speech during the ceremony
Sign passports and official citizenship certificates

Britizen 5.question: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni. What is it known as now?
Britizen 5.reference: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England. She is still remembered today and there is a statue of her on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses of Parliament.

Britizen 5.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 5.question: Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18-75 can be asked to serve on a jury
Britizen 5.reference: As well as getting the right to vote, people on the electoral register are randomly selected to serve on a jury. Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18 to 75 can be asked to do this. The age limit has been raised from 70 to 75 in Wales and England in 2016.

Britizen 5.question: The Channel Islands are self-governing
Britizen 5.reference: There are also several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called 'Crown dependencies'.

Britizen 5.question: It is illegal for any person under the age of 18 to buy a Lottery ticket
Britizen 5.reference: There is a National Lottery for which draws are made every week. You can enter by buying a ticket or a scratch card. People under 18 (Changed in 2021 from 16 to 18) are not allowed to participate in the National Lottery.

Britizen 5.question: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint
Britizen 5.reference: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint. Each saint has a special day: 1 March: St David's Day, Wales;17 March: St Patrick's Day, Northern Ireland; 23 April: St George's Day, England; 30 November: St Andrew's Day, Scotland.

Britizen 5.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 5.question: The Commonwealth can suspend any of its members
Britizen 5.reference: Membership is voluntary. The Commonwealth has no power over its members, although it can suspend membership.

Britizen 5.question: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of what?
Britizen 5.reference: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral (or solo instrumental) classical music that takes place in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London. It has been organised by the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) since 1927. The Last Night of the Proms is the most well-known concert and (along with others in the series) is broadcast on television.

Britizen 6.question: Who was Queen Elizabeth II married to?
Britizen 6.reference: Queen Elizabeth II reigned since her father's death in 1952, (she celebrated her Diamond Jubilee i.e. 60 years as queen) until she passed away in 2022. She was married to Prince Philip, the Duke of Edinburgh. Her eldest son, King Charles III (previously known as the Prince of Wales) became the sovereign monarch in 2022. His eldest son, heir to the throne, Prince William is the new Prince of Wales.

Britizen 6.question: Who is Sir Chris Hoy?
Britizen 6.reference: Sir Chris Hoy (1976-) is a Scottish cyclist who has won six gold and one silver Olympic medals. He has also won 11 world championship titles.

Britizen 6.question: Who wrote the Canterbury Tales?
Britizen 6.reference: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. The people decided to tell each other stories on the journey, and the poems describe the travellers and some of the stories they told called The Canterbury Tales.

Britizen 6.question: Who won gold medals for ice dancing at the Olympic Games in 1984?
Britizen 6.reference: Jayne Torvill (1957-) and Christopher Dean (1958-) won gold medals for ice dancing at the Olympic Games in 1984 and in four consecutive world championships.

answers.text: Bobby Moore and Dame Kelly Holmes

Britizen 6.question: The Battle of Somme resulted in how many British casualties on the first day?
Britizen 6.reference: One battle, the British attack on the Somme in July 1916, resulted in about 60,000 British casualties on the first day alone.

Britizen 6.question: For how many years did Charles I raise money without Parliament's approval?
Britizen 6.reference: For 11 years, Charles I found ways in which to raise money without Parliament's approval but eventually trouble in Scotland meant that he had to recall Parliament.

Britizen 6.question: The burial place of one of the kings was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk
Britizen 6.reference: The burial place of one of the Anglo-Saxon kings was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk. This king was buried with treasure and armour, all placed in a ship which was then covered by a mound of earth.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: What is the National Citizen Service?
Britizen 6.reference: There are many opportunities for young people to volunteer and receive accreditation which will help them to develop their skills. These include the National Citizen Service programme, which gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project.

answers.text: It provides free, independent, impartial advice on your rights and responsibilities
It provides free, confidential and impartial advice on consumer issues
It is a volunteering programme for young people who want to do voluntary development work abroad
It helps you build your skills for work and life

Britizen 6.question: Protestant Christian groups include Baptists, Methodists, Presbyterians and Quakers
Britizen 6.reference: Protestant Christian groups in the UK are Baptists, Methodists, Presbyterians and Quakers. There are also other denominations of Christianity, the biggest of which is Roman Catholic.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: Which of the options given below is not necessary for a new car?
Britizen 6.reference: If your vehicle is over three years old, you must take it for a Ministry of Transport (MOT) test every year. It is an offence not to have an MOT certificate if your vehicle is more than three years old.

Britizen 6.question: What is the BBC funded by?
Britizen 6.reference: Money from TV licences goes to the BBC.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: How many senior MPs get appointed by the Prime Minister to become ministers in charge of departments?
Britizen 6.reference: The Prime Minister appoints about 20 senior MPs to become ministers in charge of departments. These include: Chancellor of the Exchequer: responsible for the economy; Home Secretary:responsible for crime, policing and immigration; Foreign Secretary: responsible for managing relationships with foreign countries; other ministers (called 'Secretaries of State') are responsible for subjects such as education, health and defence.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: When was the Scottish Parliament formed?
Britizen 6.reference: Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them. There has been a Welsh Assembly and a Scottish Parliament since 1999.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: It is illegal to sell tobacco products such as cigarettes, cigars, roll-up tobacco to anyone under what age?
Britizen 6.reference: It is illegal to sell tobacco products (for example, cigarettes, cigars, roll-up tobacco) to anyone under the age of 18.

Britizen 6.question: People living and working in towns after Black Death led to the development of what?
Britizen 6.reference: Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: £10 £20 £50 £100 are the only banknotes currently allowed in the UK
Britizen 6.reference: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £). There are 100 pence in a pound. The denominations (values) of currency are - coins: 1p, 2p, 5p, 10p, 20p, 50p, £1 and £2; notes: £5, £10, £20, £50.

Britizen 6.question: When did the American Colonies declare their independence?
Britizen 6.reference: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments. The colonists eventually defeated the British army and Britain recognised the colonies' independence in 1783.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: Which of the following statements does NOT apply to the United Nations?
Britizen 6.reference: The UK is part of the United Nations (UN), an international organisation with more than 190 countries as members. The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security. There are 15 members on the UN Security Council, which recommends action when there are international crises and threats to peace. The UK is one of five permanent members of the Security Council.

answers.text: The UN has five members in total
It was set up after the Second World War
The UK is one of five permanent members of the UN Security Council

Britizen 6.question: The Archbishop of Canterbury is the spiritual leader of the Church of England
Britizen 6.reference: The spiritual leader of the Church of England is the Archbishop of Canterbury. The monarch has the right to select the Archbishop and other senior church officials, but usually the choice is made by the Prime Minister and a committee appointed by the Church. Several Church of England bishops sit in the House of Lords. The monarch is the head of state of the church.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: The Speaker is elected by fellow MPs using the open ballot system
Britizen 6.reference: The Speaker is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot.

Britizen 6.question: What was the name of the movement which protested against the Roman Catholic church during Henry VIII's time?
Britizen 6.reference: During Henry III's reign, the Reformation was happening across Europe. This was a movement against the authority of the Pope and the ideas and practices of the Roman Catholic Church.

Britizen 6.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 6.question: What is the money limit for the small claims procedure in England and Wales?
Britizen 6.reference: The small claims procedure is an informal way of helping people to settle minor disputes without spending a lot of time and money using a lawyer. This procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales; £5,000 in Scotland and £5,000 in Northern Ireland (Changed from £3000).

Britizen 6.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 6.question: What is the responsibility that you have as a UK citizen?
Britizen 6.reference: Flowing from the fundamental principles are responsibilities and freedoms which are shared by all those living in the UK and which we expect all residents to respect. If you wish to be a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you should: respect and obey the law; respect the rights of others, including their right to their own opinions; treat others with fairness; look after yourself and your family; look after the area in which you live and the environment.

answers.text: Look after yourself and your family

Britizen 7.question: Which flower is associated with England?
Britizen 7.reference: A rose is the flower associated with England.

Britizen 7.question: Who was the first Briton to win the Olympic gold medal in the 10,000 meters?
Britizen 7.reference: Mo Farah was the first British runner to win the gold medal in the 10 km.

Britizen 7.question: How many American colonies declared their independence in 1776?
Britizen 7.reference: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments. The colonists eventually defeated the British army and Britain recognised the colonies' independence in 1783.

Britizen 7.question: When is St Patrick's day?
Britizen 7.reference: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint. Each saint has a special day: 1 March: St David's Day, Wales;17 March: St Patrick's Day, Northern Ireland; 23 April: St George's Day, England; 30 November: St Andrew's Day, Scotland.

Britizen 7.question: What university did Isaac Newton attend?
Britizen 7.reference: Born in Lincolnshire, eastern England, Isaac Newton first became interested in science when he studied at Cambridge University. He became an important figure in the field. His most famous published work was Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica (Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy), which showed how gravity applied to the whole universe.

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: National Insurance is needed if you wish to work in the UK
Britizen 7.reference: Almost everybody in the UK who is in paid work, including self-employed people, must pay National Insurance Contributions. The money raised from National Insurance Contributions is used to pay for state benefits and services such as the state retirement pension and the National Health Service (NHS).

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: What can be done to protect a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage?
Britizen 7.reference: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007. Court orders can be obtained to protect a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage. Similar Protection Orders were introduced in Scotland in November 2011.

answers.text: Court orders can be obtained by a potential victim or someone acting for them
Ask the priest to verify the identities
Check the age of both people to make sure they are over 18
Check the age of both people to make sure they are over 25

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: What can the Scottish Parliament do?
Britizen 7.reference: The Scottish Parliament can pass laws for Scotland on all matters which are not specifically reserved to the UK Parliament. The matters on which the Scottish Parliament can legislate include: civil and criminal law, health, education, planning and additional tax-raising powers.

answers.text: May pass laws on anything in Scotland
May pass laws on general economic policy
May pass laws on foreign and defence affairs
May pass legislation on anything not specifically reserved to the UK Parliament

Britizen 7.question: Who designed the new St Paul's?
Britizen 7.reference: In 1666, a great fire destroyed much of the city, including many churches and St Paul's Cathedral. London was rebuilt with a new St Paul's, which was designed by a famous architect, Sir Christopher Wren.

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: Where are the more serious civil cases dealt with in Scotland?
Britizen 7.reference: More serious civil cases - for example, when a large amount of compensation is being claimed - are dealt with in the High Court in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. In Scotland, they are dealt with in the Court of Session in Edinburgh.

Britizen 7.question: St George is the national saint of Northern Ireland
Britizen 7.reference: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint. Each saint has a special day: 1 March: St David's Day, Wales;17 March: St Patrick's Day, Northern Ireland; 23 April: St George's Day, England; 30 November: St Andrew's Day, Scotland.

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: What is the name of the building where the Northern Irish Assembly meets?
Britizen 7.reference: In Northern Ireland elected members, known as MLAs, meet in the Northern Ireland Assembly at Stormont, in Belfast.

Britizen 7.question: When was the Emancipation Act signed? It abolished slavery throughout the British Empire
Britizen 7.reference: Along with other abolitionists (people who supported the abolition of slavery), William Wilberforce succeeded in turning public opinion against the slave trade. In 1807, it became illegal to trade slaves in British ships or from British ports, and in 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

Britizen 7.question: Magna Carta is Latin for what?
Britizen 7.reference: In Latin Magna Carta means the Great Charter

Britizen 7.question: What were the Irish people who favoured complete independence from the UK known as?
Britizen 7.reference: The Irish Nationalist movement had grown strongly through the 19th century. Some, such as the Fenians, favoured complete independence.

Britizen 7.question: Which religion did the Huguenots follow?
Britizen 7.reference: Between 1680 and 1720 many refugees called Huguenots came from France. They were Protestants and had been persecuted for their religion. Many were educated and skilled and worked as scientists, in banking, or in weaving or other crafts.

Britizen 7.question: Robert Browning, William Blake and Lord Byron were all famous poets
Britizen 7.reference: The statement is true

Britizen 7.question: Which event is celebrated on the 5th of November each year?
Britizen 7.reference: Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays. The origin of this celebration was an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

answers.text: A plot to blow up the Houses of Parliament in 1605
The end of the Second World War
The end of the First World War
The defeat of the Spanish Armada

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: The jury decides on the penalty if the defendant is found guilty
Britizen 7.reference: The jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of 'guilty' or 'not guilty' based on what they have heard. In Scotland, a third verdict of 'not proven' is also possible. If the jury finds a defendant guilty, the judge decides on the penalty.

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: It is illegal for shops and businesses to not accept the banknotes of Northern Ireland and Scotland
Britizen 7.reference: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shops and businesses do not have to accept them.

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: The role and membership of the House of Lords has changed over the last 50 years
Britizen 7.reference: The role and membership of the House of Lords has changed over the last 50 years.

Britizen 7.question: Which TWO religions celebrate Diwali?
Britizen 7.reference: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is often called the Festival of Lights. It is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs. It celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge. There are different stories about how the festival came about. There is a famous celebration of Diwali in Leicester.

Britizen 7.question: What is the capital of Wales?
Britizen 7.reference: Cardiff is the capital of Wales.

Britizen 7.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 7.question: Who is the heir to the throne?
Britizen 7.reference: The King's eldest son, Prince William (the Prince of Wales), is the heir to the throne.

Britizen 8.question: Which Two British film actors have recently won Oscars?
Britizen 8.reference: Recent British actors to have won Oscars include Colin Firth, Sir Anthony Hopkins, Dame Judi Dench, Kate Winslet and Tilda Swinton.

Britizen 8.question: What created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland?
Britizen 8.reference: The Act of Union of 1800 created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

Britizen 8.question: When did the 'Battle of Agincourt' take place?
Britizen 8.reference: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V's vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

Britizen 8.question: What is the Church of England usually known as in other countries?
Britizen 8.reference: The official Church of the state is the Church of England, called the Anglican Church in other countries and the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States.

Britizen 8.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 8.question: When was the Scottish Parliament established?
Britizen 8.reference: There has been a Welsh Assembly and a Scottish Parliament since 1999.

Britizen 8.question: During Slave trade, where did slaves primarily come from?
