Britizen 8.reference: Slaves came primarily from West Africa. Travelling on British ships in horrible conditions, they were taken to America and the Caribbean, where they were made to work on tobacco and sugar plantations.

Britizen 8.question: Who is the Patron St of Wales?
Britizen 8.reference: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint. Each saint has a special day: 1 March: St David's Day, Wales;17 March: St Patrick's Day, Northern Ireland; 23 April: St George's Day, England; 30 November: St Andrew's Day, Scotland.

Britizen 8.question: What is the only major golf tournament held outside of the US?
Britizen 8.reference: The Open Championship is the only 'Major' golf tournament held outside the United States. It is hosted by a different golf course every year.

Britizen 8.question: What is Richard Arkwright remembered for?
Britizen 8.reference: Born in 1732, Arkwright originally trained and worked as a barber. He was able to dye hair and make wigs. When wigs became less popular, he started to work in textiles. He improved the original carding machine. Arkwright is particularly remembered for the efficient and profitable way that he ran his factories.

Britizen 8.question: What is meant by 'common law'?
Britizen 8.reference: In England, judges developed 'common law' by a process of precedence (that is, following previous decisions) and tradition. In Scotland, the legal system developed slightly differently and laws were 'codified' (that is, written down).

answers.text: Sentence is decided by the jury

Britizen 8.question: To be or not to be' is a famous Shakespeare line from which play or poem?
Britizen 8.reference: Lines from Shakespeare's plays and poems which are often still quoted include: Once more unto the breach (Henry V); To be or not to be (Hamlet); A rose by any other name (Romeo and Juliet); All the world's a stage (As You Like It); The darling buds of May (Sonnet 18 'Shall I Compare Thee To a Summer's Day).

Britizen 8.question: What county does Stonehenge stand in?
Britizen 8.reference: Stonehenge still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire. Stonehenge was probably a special gathering place for seasonal ceremonies.

Britizen 8.question: Where does the Grand National takes place?
Britizen 8.reference: There are racecourses all over the UK. Famous horse-racing events include: Royal Ascot, a five-day race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family; the Grand National at Aintree near Liverpool; and the Scottish Grand National at Ayr. There is a National Horseracing Museum in Newmarket, Suffolk.

Britizen 8.question: When is Halloween (an ancient festival) celebrated?
Britizen 8.reference: Halloween, 31 October, is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter. Young people will often dress up in frightening costumes to play 'trick or treat'. People give them treats to stop them playing tricks on them. A lot of people carve lanterns out of pumpkins and put a candle inside.

Britizen 8.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 8.question: What does the money raised from National Insurance Contributions pay for? (Choose TWO)
Britizen 8.reference: The money raised from National Insurance Contributions is used to pay for state benefits and services such as the state retirement pension and the National Health Service (NHS).

Britizen 8.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 8.question: Who is considered ineligible to do jury service?
Britizen 8.reference: Everyone who is summoned to do jury service must do it unless they are not eligible (for example, because they have a criminal conviction) or they provide a good reason to be excused, such as ill health.

Britizen 8.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 8.question: What are TWO core values of a civil servant?
Britizen 8.reference: Civil servants are expected to carry out their role with dedication and a commitment to the civil service and its core values. These are: integrity, honesty, objectivity and impartiality

answers.text: Must belong to a political party

Britizen 8.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 8.question: Members of the public are allowed to attend Youth Courts hearings
Britizen 8.reference: Members of the public are not allowed in Youth Courts, and the name or photographs of the accused young person cannot be published in newspapers or used by the media.

Britizen 8.question: Who had a great influence on the English language?
Britizen 8.reference: Shakespeare had a great influence on the English language and invented many words that are still common today.

Britizen 8.question: Great Britain' and 'the United Kingdom' have the same meaning
Britizen 8.reference: The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland. 'Great Britain' refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

Britizen 8.question: The Welsh Dragon doesn't appear on the Union Flag because Wales was already united with England in 1606
Britizen 8.reference: The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already united with England.

Britizen 8.question: Which Armed Force was used in the Battle of Britain?
Britizen 8.reference: The Germans waged an air campaign against Britain, but the British resisted with their fighter planes and eventually won the crucial aerial battle against the Germans, called 'the Battle of Britain', in the summer of 1940. The most important planes used by the Royal Air Force in the Battle of Britain were the Spitfire and the Hurricane - which were designed and built in Britain.

Britizen 8.question: Which is a famous area in London for theatres?
Britizen 8.reference: There are theaters in most towns and cities throughout the UK, ranging from the large to the small. They are an important part of local communities and often show both professional and amateur productions. London's West End, also known as 'Theatreland', is particularly well known. The Mousetrap, a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie, has been running in the West End since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: Where does the Prime Minister reside?
Britizen 9.reference: The official home of the Prime Minister is 10 Downing Street, in central London, near the Houses of Parliament.

Britizen 9.question: What is the 'King James' Bible?
Britizen 9.reference: One achievement of King James' reign was a new translation of the Bible into English. This translation is known as the 'King James Version' or the 'Authorised Version'. It was not the first English Bible but is a version which continues to be used in many Protestant churches today.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: How many member states does the Commonwealth have?
Britizen 9.reference: The Queen/King is the ceremonial head of the Commonwealth, which currently has 56 member states. Membership is voluntary.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: What are the MPs who don't represent a political party called?
Britizen 9.reference: There are a few MPs who do not represent any of the main political parties. They are called 'independents' and usually represent an issue important to their constituency.

Britizen 9.question: Beefeaters, who tell visitors about the building's history are associated with which of these?
Britizen 9.reference: The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror after he became king in 1066. Tours are given by the Yeoman Warders, also known as Beefeaters, who tell visitors about the building's history. People can also see the Crown Jewels there.

Britizen 9.question: Who in 2002 was voted the greatest Briton of all time?
Britizen 9.reference: In 2002, Winston Churchill was voted the greatest Briton of all time by the public.

Britizen 9.question: What were the people who supported the King known as in 1642?
Britizen 9.reference: During the English Civil War (1642) the country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

Britizen 9.question: Protestant ideas gradually gained strength in England, Wales and Scotland during what century?
Britizen 9.reference: Protestant ideas gradually gained strength in England, Wales and Scotland during the 16th century.

Britizen 9.question: How long is the longest straight distance on the mainland?
Britizen 9.reference: The longest distance on the mainland is from John O'Groats on the north coast of Scotland to Land's End in the south-west corner of England. It is about 870 miles (approximately 1,400 kilometres).

Britizen 9.question: What were James II's supporters known as?
Britizen 9.reference: Some continued to believe that James was the rightful king, particularly in Scotland. Some joined him in exile in France; others were secret supporters. James' supporters became known as Jacobites.

Britizen 9.question: In Northern Ireland some people speak Irish Gaelic
Britizen 9.reference: In Wales, many people speak Welsh - a completely different language from English - and it is taught in schools and universities. In Scotland, Gaelic (again, a different language) is spoken in some parts of the Highlands and Islands, and in Northern Ireland some people speak Irish Gaelic.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: What should you do when you move into a new house or apartment?
Britizen 9.reference: When you move into a new house or apartment, introduce yourself to the people who live near you. Getting to know your neighbours can help you to become part of the community and make friends.

answers.text: Tell the people who live near you not to make noise
Introduce yourself to the people who live near you so they can help you
Warn the people who live near you not to talk to you

Britizen 9.question: What percentage of the population has a parent or a grandparent born outside of the UK due to post-war immigration?
Britizen 9.reference: Post-war immigration means that nearly 10% of the population has a parent or grandparent born outside the UK.

Britizen 9.question: Which of these TWO is correct about Halloween?
Britizen 9.reference: Halloween, 31 October, is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter. Young people will often dress up in frightening costumes to play 'trick or treat'. People give them treats to stop them playing tricks on them. A lot of people carve lanterns out of pumpkins and put a candle inside.

answers.text: It is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter
It is when lovers exchange cards and gifts
It is celebrated on the 31 October
It is not celebrated in Scotland

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: The devolved administrations each have their own civil service.
Britizen 9.reference: The devolved administrations each have their own civil service.

Britizen 9.question: Which patron Saint has a white cross on a blue ground?
Britizen 9.reference: The Union Flag consists of three crosses: The cross of St George, patron saint of England, is a red cross on a white ground; The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland, is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground; The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland, is a diagonal red cross on a white ground.

Britizen 9.question: Who was the first Danish king in England?
Britizen 9.reference: Anglo-Saxon kings continued to rule what is now England, except for a short period when there were Danish kings. The first of these was Cnut, also called Canute.

Britizen 9.question: What is the name of the song sung by people in the UK and other countries when celebrating the New Year?
Britizen 9.reference: One of the songs sung by people in the UK at New Year is Auld Lang Syne.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: Who can stand for office as a Local Councillor?
Britizen 9.reference: British citizens can stand for Office as a local councillor, a member of Parliament (or the devolved equivalents). This is an opportunity to become even more involved in the political life of the UK. You may also be able to stand for Office if you are an Irish citizen, or an eligible Commonwealth citizen.

answers.text: British, Irish and Eligible Commonwealth citizens
British citizens of the UK only

Britizen 9.question: Which famous poet's work was inspired by Nature?
Britizen 9.reference: Other poets, including William Wordsworth, were inspired by nature. Sir Walter Scott wrote poems inspired by Scotland and the traditional stories and songs from the area on the borders of Scotland and England. He also wrote novels, many of which were set in Scotland.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: How often do PM's Questions take place in the parliament?
Britizen 9.reference: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government's failures and weaknesses. One important opportunity to do this is at Prime Minister's Questions, which takes place every week while Parliament is sitting.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: Which one of these countries in the UK requires you to complete your own registration form to get entitled to vote?
Britizen 9.reference: In Northern Ireland a different system operates. This is called 'individual registration' and all those entitled to vote must complete their own registration form. Once registered, people stay on the register provided their personal details do not change.

Britizen 9.question: Where is the UK geographically located with respect to Europe?
Britizen 9.reference: The UK is located in the North-West of Europe.

Britizen 9.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 9.question: Towns, cities and rural areas in the UK are governed by civil servants who are appointed by the Government?
Britizen 9.reference: Towns, cities and rural areas in the UK are governed by democratically elected councils, often called 'local authorities'. Some areas have both district and county councils, which have different functions. Most large towns and cities have a single local authority.

Britizen 10.question: What does the TV licence money pay for?
Britizen 10.reference: The money from TV licences is used to pay for the British Broadcasting Corporation also known as the BBC.

Britizen 10.question: Who developed important Human Nature ideas?
Britizen 10.reference: David Hume's ideas about human nature continue to influence philosophers.

Britizen 10.question: What did the Romans introduce to Britain?
Britizen 10.reference: The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years. They built roads and public buildings, created a structure of law, and introduced new plants and animals.

Britizen 10.question: What is an example of the Middle Ages architecture?
Britizen 10.reference: In the Middle Ages, great cathedrals and churches were built, many of which still stand today. Examples are the cathedrals in Durham, Lincoln, Canterbury and Salisbury. The White Tower in the Tower of London is an example of a Norman castle keep, built on the orders of William the Conqueror.

answers.text: The White Tower in the Tower of London

Britizen 10.question: When was the Giant's Causeway formed?
Britizen 10.reference: Located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland, the Giant's Causeway is a land formation of columns made from volcanic lava. It was formed about 50 million years ago. There are many legends about the Causeway and how it was formed.

Britizen 10.question: Who invented the Internet as we know it today? (World Wide Web)
Britizen 10.reference: The inventor of the World Wide Web, Sir Tim Berners-Lee (1955-), is British. Information was successfully transferred via the web for the first time on 25 December 1990.

Britizen 10.question: Who directed the two British films 'Brief Encounter' and 'Lawrence of Arabia'?
Britizen 10.reference: Brief Encounter (1945), Lawrence of Arabia (1962) were directed by David Lean

Britizen 10.question: Who captained the English football team in 1966?
Britizen 10.reference: Bobby Moore (1941-93) captained the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966.

Britizen 10.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 10.question: Which of the following is a fundamental principle of British Life?
Britizen 10.reference: The fundamental principles of British life include: Democracy; The rule of law; Individual liberty; Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs; Participation in community life.

Britizen 10.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 10.question: Only members of the public who have their name on the electoral register can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster in the House of Lords
Britizen 10.reference: The public can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster from public galleries in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords.

Britizen 10.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 10.question: What are TWO of the activities that you might be involved in when canvassing?
Britizen 10.reference: Political parties are particularly busy at election times. Members work hard to persuade people to vote for their candidates - for instance, by handing out leaflets in the street or by knocking on people's doors and asking for their support. This is called 'canvassing'.

answers.text: Handing out leaflets in the street
Knocking on people's doors and asking for their support
Getting paid for each vote obtained in commission
Getting paid for the time spent advertising

Britizen 10.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 10.question: Which TWO things are you required to have to apply for ILR or British Citizenship?
Britizen 10.reference: In order to apply for ILR or Naturalization as a British Citizen you must have a valid life in the UK pass certificate and good knowledge of English language

answers.text: A relative in the UK with a British Passport
A Life in the UK pass certificate

Britizen 10.question: Which process for the mass production of steel led to the development of the shipbuilding industry and the railways?
Britizen 10.reference: The development of the Bessemer process for the mass production of steel led to the development of the shipbuilding industry and the railways. Manufacturing jobs became the main source of employment in Britain.

Britizen 10.question: The invasion of which country by Germany provoked UK to declare war on Germany in 1939?
Britizen 10.reference: When Hitler invaded Poland in 1939, Britain and France declared war in order to stop his aggression.

Britizen 10.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 10.question: Where are minor criminal cases in England, Wales and Northern Ireland heard?
Britizen 10.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates' Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court.

Britizen 10.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 10.question: The Opposition is the second largest party in the House of Commons
Britizen 10.reference: The second-largest party in the House of Commons is called the Opposition. The leader of the Opposition usually becomes Prime Minister if his or her party wins the next General Election.

Britizen 10.question: Which prehistoric monument still stands in the English county of Wiltshire?
Britizen 10.reference: Stonehenge, still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire. Stonehenge was probably a special gathering place for seasonal ceremonies.

Britizen 10.question: Robert Stephenson and George were famous pioneers of which industry?
Britizen 10.reference: Just before Victoria came to the throne, the father and son George and Robert Stephenson pioneered the railway engine and a major expansion of the railways took place in the Victorian period. Railways were built throughout the Empire.

Britizen 10.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 10.question: Conservative Party is a political party in the UK
Britizen 10.reference: The main political parties include: Conservative Party, the Labour Party, the Liberal Democrats, or one of the parties representing Scottish, Welsh or Northern Irish interests.

Britizen 10.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 10.question: The name or photograph of cases heard in the Youth Court can be published in the newspapers once the young person has been found guilty
Britizen 10.reference: Members of the public are not allowed in Youth Courts, and the name or photographs of the accused young person cannot be published in newspapers or used by the media.

Britizen 10.question: What are traditional pub games? (Select TWO)
Britizen 10.reference: Most communities will have a 'local' pub that is a natural focal point for social activities. Pub quizzes are popular. Pool and darts are traditional pub games.

Britizen 10.question: Where was Rudyard Kipling born?
Britizen 10.reference: Rudyard Kipling was born in India in 1865 and later lived in India, the UK and the USA. He wrote books and poems set in both India and the UK. His poems and novels reflected the idea that the British Empire was a force for good. Kipling was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1907.

Britizen 10.question: What was Isaac Newton famous for?
Britizen 10.reference: His most famous published work was Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica (Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy), which showed how gravity applied to the whole universe. Newton also discovered that white light is made up of the colours of the rainbow. Many of his discoveries are still important for modern science.

Britizen 11.question: Where in Scotland is known as the home of golf?
Britizen 11.reference: St Andrews in Scotland is known as the home of golf.

Britizen 11.question: Where does the UK government sit?
Britizen 11.reference: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Westminster.

Britizen 11.question: Who was the Prime Minister during 1945-1951?
Britizen 11.reference: Clement Attlee was born in London in 1883. He was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951 and led the Labour Party for 20 years.

Britizen 11.question: Who wrote 'The Lord of the Rings'?
Britizen 11.reference: The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country's best-loved novel in 2003.

Britizen 11.question: Which organization looks after the Edinburgh Castle?
Britizen 11.reference: The Edinburgh Castle has a long history, dating back to the early Middle Ages. It is looked after by Historic Scotland, a Scottish government agency.

Britizen 11.question: What does an aged population have an impact on?
Britizen 11.reference: People in the UK are living longer than ever before. This is due to improved living standards and better health care. There are now a record number of people aged 85 and over. This has an impact on the cost of pensions and health care.

Britizen 11.question: What party won the election in 1945 (After WWII)?
Britizen 11.reference: In 1945 the British people elected a Labour government. The new Prime Minister was Clement Attlee, who promised to introduce the welfare state outlined in the Beveridge Report. In 1948, Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS), which guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: When did Queen Elizabeth II celebrate her Diamond Jubilee?
Britizen 11.reference: Big Ben tower is named 'Elizabeth Tower' in honour of Queen Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee in 2012.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: What do you have to pay tax on in the UK?
Britizen 11.reference: People in the UK have to pay tax on their income, which includes: wages from paid employment; profits from self-employment, taxable benefits; pensions; income from property, savings and dividends.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: Which of these is NOT part of the Commonwealth?
Britizen 11.reference: The US is not part of the Commonwealth.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: You can find copies of Hansard report in large libraries and on the Internet
Britizen 11.reference: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard. Written reports can be found in large libraries and online.

Britizen 11.question: Queen Mary was a Protestant and persecuted Catholics (for this reason, she became known as 'Bloody Mary')
Britizen 11.reference: Mary was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants (for this reason, she became known as 'Bloody Mary'). Mary also died after a short reign and the next monarch was her half-sister, Elizabeth, the daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn.

Britizen 11.question: Where is Sutton Hoo?
Britizen 11.reference: The burial place of one of the kings was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk. This king was buried with treasure and armour, all placed in a ship which was then covered by a mound of earth.

Britizen 11.question: Wales wasn't under Anglo-Saxon rule
Britizen 11.reference: Parts of the west of Britain, including much of what is now Wales, and Scotland, remained free of Anglo-Saxon rule.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have devolved administrations which give them full control over all policies and laws
Britizen 11.reference: Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control. However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

Britizen 11.question: Who captained the English cricket team and holds a number of records, both for batting and bowling?
Britizen 11.reference: Sir Ian Botham (1955-) captained the English cricket team and holds a number of English Test cricket records, both for batting and for bowling.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: The Prime Minister cannot be changed until the next General election
Britizen 11.reference: The Prime Minister can be changed if the MPs in the governing party decide to do so, or if he or she wishes to resign.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: Which TWO of the following are protected by law from discrimination?
Britizen 11.reference: UK laws ensure that people are not treated unfairly in any area of life or work because of their age, disability, sex, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sexuality or marital status.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland
Britizen 11.reference: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007.

Britizen 11.question: What is the name given to rented additional land which people use to grow fruit and vegetable?
Britizen 11.reference: A lot of people have gardens at home and will spend their free time looking after them. Some people rent additional land called 'an allotment', where they grow fruit and vegetables. Gardening and flower shows range from major national exhibitions to small local events.

Britizen 11.question: Many Scottish landlords destroyed individual small farms (known as 'crofts') to make space for large flocks of sheep and cattle. What was this known as?
Britizen 11.reference: A process began which became known as the 'Highland Clearances'. Many Scottish landlords destroyed individual small farms (known as 'crofts') to make space for large flocks of sheep and cattle. Evictions became very common in the early 19th century. Many Scottish people left for North America at this time.

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: Which of the statements is true?
Britizen 11.reference: Solicitors' charges are usually based on how much time they spend on a case. It is very important to find out at the start how much a case is likely to cost.

answers.text: Solicitors' charges are based on how serious the case is
Solicitors' charges are based on how much time they spend on a case

Britizen 11.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 11.question: What area is the Home Secretary responsible for?
Britizen 11.reference: The Prime Minister appoints about 20 senior MPs to become ministers in charge of departments. These include: Chancellor of the Exchequer: responsible for the economy; Home Secretary:responsible for crime, policing and immigration; Foreign Secretary: responsible for managing relationships with foreign countries; other ministers (called 'Secretaries of State') are responsible for subjects such as education, health and defence.

Britizen 11.question: Which battle did William the Conqueror fight in?
Britizen 11.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror. The battle is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

Britizen 12.topic: A modern, thriving society
Britizen 12.question: What is the Turner Prize given for?
Britizen 12.reference: The Turner Prize is recognised as one of the most prestigious visual art awards in Europe. Previous winners include Damien Hirst and Richard Wright.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: What is the main purpose of the Opposition?
Britizen 12.reference: The opposition form the shadow cabinet and their role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies.

answers.text: To represent the people of the UK

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: What is the 'Divine Right of Kings'?
Britizen 12.reference: Divine Right of Kings' is the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule. The king should be able to act without having to seek approval from Parliament.

answers.text: Reduced the power of the nobles
Reduced the power of the King
The King was Appointed by God

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: It is a civil offence to carry a weapon
Britizen 12.reference: It is a criminal offence to carry a weapon.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: How many members does a jury in England, Wales and Northern Ireland have?
Britizen 12.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland a jury has 12 members, and in Scotland a jury has 15 members.

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: Constituencies which had hardly any voters were called 'Pocket Boroughs'
Britizen 12.reference: Some constituencies were controlled by a single wealthy family. These were called 'pocket boroughs'. Other constituencies had hardly any voters and were called 'rotten boroughs'.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Can the devolved administrations control sectors such as immigration, foreign affairs and taxation?
Britizen 12.reference: Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them. Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control. However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

answers.text: Only if the local people support them

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Adult citizens of the UK, and citizens of the Commonwealth and the Irish Republic who are resident in the UK, can vote in all public elections
Britizen 12.reference: Adult citizens of the UK, and citizens of the Commonwealth and the Irish Republic who are resident in the UK, can vote in all public elections.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Forced marriages are an offence in the UK
Britizen 12.reference: Forced marriage is where one or both parties do not or cannot give their consent to enter into the partnership. Forcing another person to marry is a criminal offence.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Which of these statements is correct?
Britizen 12.reference: Since 1958, the Prime Minister has had the power to nominate peers just for their own lifetime. These are called life peers. They have usually had an important career in politics, business, law or another profession. Life peers are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister.

answers.text: Life peers are appointed by the Prime Minister on the advice of the monarchy
Life peers are appointed by the monarchy on the advice of the Prime Minister

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: How can you visit the Welsh Assembly?
Britizen 12.reference: The Senedd is an open building. You can book guided tours or seats in the public galleries for the Welsh Assembly. To make a booking, contact the Assembly Booking Service

answers.text: By queuing at the public entrance all day

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Which political party is still known as the Tories?
Britizen 12.reference: The modern Conservative Party is still sometimes referred to as the Tories

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: Which TWO of these were introduced before the First World War in 1914?
Britizen 12.reference: Financial help for the unemployed, old-age pensions and free school meals were just a few of the important measures introduced.

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: There is no upper age limit for doing jury service
Britizen 12.reference: As well as getting the right to vote, people on the electoral register are randomly selected to serve on a jury. Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18 to 70 can be asked to do this.

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: Which of these is correct in the chronological order?
Britizen 12.reference: Magna Carta(1215), The Black Death (1348), The Canterbury Tales(1400)

answers.text: The Black Death, Canterbury Tales, Magna Carta
Canterbury Tales, the Black Death, Magna Carta
Magna Carta, the Black Death, Canterbury Tales,
The Black Death, Magna Carta, Canterbury Tales

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Which of these TWO countries are Commonwealth Members?
Britizen 12.reference: Cameroon and Namibia are members of the Commonwealth

Britizen 12.topic: A modern, thriving society
Britizen 12.question: What does Eid al-Fitr celebrate?
Britizen 12.reference: Eid al-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month. They thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast. The date when it takes place changes every year. Muslims attend special services and meals.

answers.text: That the prophet Ibrahim was willing to sacrifice his son when God ordered him to
The founding of the Muslim community

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: In which year did the UK join the European Union (EU)?
Britizen 12.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973. After UK's Brexit vote, there are now 27 EU member states.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: What is the minimum age for serving on a jury?
Britizen 12.reference: As well as getting the right to vote, people on the electoral register are randomly selected to serve on a jury. Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18 to 70 can be asked to do this.

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: William defeated James II at the Battle of the Boyne in Ireland in 1690. This event is celebrated in which country?
Britizen 12.reference: William defeated James II at the Battle of the Boyne in Ireland in 1690, an event which is still celebrated by some in Northern Ireland today. William re-conquered Ireland and James fled back to France.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: Which one is not a valid UK coin?
Britizen 12.reference: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £). There are 100 pence in a pound. The denominations (values) of currency are - coins: 1p, 2p, 5p, 10p, 20p, 50p, £1 and £2; notes: £5, £10, £20, £50.

Britizen 12.topic: A long and illustrious history
Britizen 12.question: Who was the Prime minister and inspiration leader during the World War Two?
Britizen 12.reference: Churchill was the son of a politician and, before becoming a Conservative MP in 1900, was a soldier and journalist. In May 1940 he became Prime Minister. He refused to surrender to the Nazis and was an inspirational leader to the British people in a time of great hardship. He lost the General Election in 1945 but returned as Prime Minister in 1951.

Britizen 12.topic: A modern, thriving society
Britizen 12.question: What is the Grand National?
Britizen 12.reference: Famous horse-racing events include: Royal Ascot, a five-day race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family; the Grand National at Aintree near Liverpool; and the Scottish Grand National at Ayr.

Britizen 12.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 12.question: You can contact MPs only at their office in the House of Commons
Britizen 12.reference: You can contact MPs by letter or telephone at their constituency Office, or at their Office in the House of Commons.

Britizen 13.question: Who ordered to write the Book of Common Prayer?
Britizen 13.reference: Henry VIII was succeeded by his son Edward VI, who was strongly Protestant. During his reign, the Book of Common Prayer was written to be used in the Church of England.

Britizen 13.question: What was the main crop in Ireland in the 19th century?
Britizen 13.reference: In the 19th century, many people in Ireland depended on potatoes as a large part of their diet. In the middle of the century the potato crop failed, and Ireland suffered a famine. A million people died from disease and starvation.

Britizen 13.question: What was the most important principle of the Enlightenment?
Britizen 13.reference: One of the most important principles of the Enlightenment was that everyone should have the right to their own political and religious beliefs and that the state should not try to dictate to them.

answers.text: Freedom of religion and political views

Britizen 13.question: Which of the events is the landing of allied forces in Normandy?
Britizen 13.reference: On 6 June 1944, allied forces landed in Normandy (this event is often referred to as 'D-Day').

Britizen 13.question: What did R. A. Butler introduce?
Britizen 13.reference: R.A. Butler oversaw the introduction of the Education Act 1944 (often called 'The Butler Act'), which introduced free secondary education in England and Wales.

answers.text: Free university education in the UK

Britizen 13.question: In which year was the first Union Flag created?
Britizen 13.reference: The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already united with England.

Britizen 13.question: What is the correct order of the Patron Saints' Days? (Starting from January)
Britizen 13.reference: Only Scotland and Northern Ireland have their patron saint's day as an official holiday. Events are held across Scotland, Northern Ireland and the rest of the country, especially where there are a lot of people of Scottish, Northern Irish and Irish heritage.

answers.text: St David's Day, St Patrick's Day, St Andrew's Day, St George's Day
St George's Day, St David's Day, St Patrick's Day, St Andrew's Day
St David's Day, St Patrick's Day, St George's Day, St Andrew's Day
St Patrick's Day, St David's Day, St Andrew's Day, St George's Day

Britizen 13.question: In the 20th century, what did Sir Edwin Lutyens design to be the seat of government in India?
Britizen 13.reference: In the 20th century, Sir Edwin Lutyens had an influence throughout the British Empire. He designed New Delhi to be the seat of government in India. After the First World War, he was responsible for many war memorials throughout the world, including the Cenotaph in Whitehall.

Britizen 13.question: Select TWO early members of the Royal Society
Britizen 13.reference: Charles II was interested in science. During his reign, the Royal Society was formed to promote 'natural knowledge'. This is the oldest surviving scientific society in the world. Among its early members were Sir Edmund Halley, who successfully predicted the return of the comet now called Halley's Comet, and Sir Isaac Newton.

Britizen 13.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 13.question: Who resides in 10 Downing Street?
Britizen 13.reference: The official home of the Prime Minister is 10 Downing Street, in central London, near the Houses of Parliament. He or she also has a country house outside London called Chequers.

Britizen 13.question: What is Boxing Day?(Choose TWO)
Britizen 13.reference: Boxing Day is the day after Christmas Day and is a public holiday.

Britizen 13.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 13.question: The Commonwealth is an association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development
Britizen 13.reference: The Commonwealth is an association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development. Most member states were once part of the British Empire, although a few countries which were not have also joined.

Britizen 13.question: Where were the MacDonalds of Glencoe originally from?
Britizen 13.reference: All Scottish clans were required formally to accept William as king by taking an oath. The MacDonalds of Glencoe were late in taking the oath and were all killed. The memory of this massacre meant some Scots distrusted the new government.

Britizen 13.question: You should respect someone's right in court. In return, you are offered the right to a fair trial.
Britizen 13.reference: You should always respect everyone rights in court. Vise versa everyone should respect your rights.

Britizen 13.question: Which of these countries did NOT fight on the side of the Allied Powers during the First World War?
Britizen 13.reference: The Allies fought against the Central Powers - mainly Germany, the Austro-Hungarian Empire, the Ottoman Empire and later Bulgaria.

Britizen 13.question: In the Church of Scotland, who is the Moderator?
Britizen 13.reference: In Scotland, the national Church is the Church of Scotland, which is a Presbyterian Church. It is governed by ministers and elders. The chairperson of the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland is the Moderator, who is appointed for one year only and often speaks on behalf of that Church.

answers.text: The chairperson of the General Assembly, who is appointed for one year only and often speaks on behalf of the Church
A key figure who is responsible for all relations with the Church of England
A financial overseer who has overall responsibility for the accounts of the Church
The spiritual leader of the Church, who is appointed for life

Britizen 13.question: What is the name of the first major railway built in Britain?
Britizen 13.reference: The Great Western Railway is the name of the first major railway built in Britain.

Britizen 13.question: The UK has a declining elderly population
Britizen 13.reference: People in the UK are living longer than ever before. This is due to improved living standards and better health care. There are now a record number of people aged 85 and over. This has an impact on the cost of pensions and health care.

Britizen 13.question: The Man Booker Prize is awarded in which area?
Britizen 13.reference: The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe. It has been awarded since 1968. Past winners include Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes.

Britizen 13.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 13.question: Which two things should you do if you wish to be a permanent resident or citizen of the UK?
Britizen 13.reference: If you wish to be a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you should: respect and obey the law; respect the rights of others, including their right to their own opinions; treat others with fairness; look after yourself and your family; look after the area in which you live and the environment.

answers.text: Look after the area in which you live and the environment
Look after yourself and your family

Britizen 13.question: Christmas Day, which celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ, is on which day?
Britizen 13.reference: Christmas Day, 25 December, celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ. It is a public holiday. Many Christians go to church on Christmas Eve (24 December) or on Christmas Day itself.

Britizen 13.question: What term arose after the evacuation of 300,000 people from the beaches?
Britizen 13.reference: Many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats from Britain helped the Navy to rescue more than 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase 'the Dunkirk spirit'.

Britizen 14.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 14.question: What is the fundamental principle of British life?
Britizen 14.reference: The rule of law is one of the fundamental principles of British Life.

Britizen 14.question: Which word comes from the Viking language?
Britizen 14.reference: Grimsby and Scunthorpe are the examples of words that come from the Viking languages.

Britizen 14.question: Who designed the Clifton Suspension Bridge?
Britizen 14.reference: Brunel was originally from Portsmouth, England. He was an engineer who built tunnels, bridges, railway lines and ships. He was responsible for constructing the Great Western Railway and the Clifton Suspension Bridge.

Britizen 14.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 14.question: What happens if an MP dies or resigns?
Britizen 14.reference: If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election, called a by-election, in his or her constituency.

answers.text: The closest MP will have to cover
A new General Election is held

Britizen 14.question: What does the 'R' Plate stand for?
Britizen 14.reference: In Northern Ireland, a newly qualified driver must display an 'R' plate (for restricted driver) for one year after passing the test.

Britizen 14.question: When did the Vikings first attack Britain?
Britizen 14.reference: The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway. They first visited Britain in AD 789 to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves.

Britizen 14.question: When was the Emancipation Act signed?
Britizen 14.reference: In 1807, it became illegal to trade slaves in British ships or from British ports, and in 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire. The Royal Navy stopped slave ships from other countries, freed the slaves and punished the slave traders.

Britizen 14.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 14.question: How many members does the UN Security Council have?
Britizen 14.reference: The UK is part of the United Nations (UN), an international organisation with more than 190 countries as members. The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security. There are 15 members on the UN Security Council, which recommends action when there are international crises and threats to peace. The UK is one of five permanent members of the Security Council.

Britizen 14.question: Who had the title 'Lord Protector'?
Britizen 14.reference: After his campaign in Ireland and victory over Charles II at Worcester, Cromwell was recognised as the leader of the new republic. He was given the title of Lord Protector and ruled until his death in 1658.

Britizen 14.question: When did William of Orange invade England?
Britizen 14.reference: In 1688, important Protestants in England asked William to invade England and proclaim himself king.

Britizen 14.question: The process 'Highland Clearances' happened due to rebellion of slaves
Britizen 14.reference: A process began which became known as the 'Highland Clearances'. Many Scottish landlords destroyed individual small farms (known as 'crofts') to make space for large flocks of sheep and cattle. Evictions became very common in the early 19th century. Many Scottish people left for North America at this time.

Britizen 14.question: What prehistoric village (off the north coast of Scotland) has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age?
Britizen 14.reference: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

Britizen 14.question: In what county can you see a hill fort at Maiden Castle?
Britizen 14.reference: A very impressive hill fort can still be seen today at Maiden Castle, in the English county of Dorset.

Britizen 14.question: What is the 'Grand National'?
Britizen 14.reference: The Grand National at Aintree near Liverpool and the Scottish Grand National at Ayr are well known horse racing events.

Britizen 14.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 14.question: On which TWO occasions will there be a fresh election, called a by-election, in MP's constituency?
Britizen 14.reference: MPs are elected at a General Election, which is held at least every five years. If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election, called a by-election, in his or her constituency. MPs are elected through a system called 'first past the post'.

Britizen 14.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 14.question: How is the Speaker elected?
Britizen 14.reference: Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker. This person is the chief Officer of the House of Commons. The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with constituents' problems like any other MP. The Speaker is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot.

answers.text: The Archbishop of Canterbury chooses the Speaker

Britizen 14.question: Why was the Habeas Corpus Act (1679) so important?
Britizen 14.reference: The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679. This was a very important piece of legislation which remains relevant today. Habeas corpus is Latin for 'you must present the person in court'. The Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully. Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

answers.text: It gave women their property rights
It gave women the right to vote
It guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully
It limited the power of the monarch

Britizen 14.question: At what age did Queen Victoria become Queen of the UK?
Britizen 14.reference: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18. She reigned until 1901, almost 64 years. At the date of writing (2013) this is the longest reign of any British monarch. Her reign is known as the Victorian Age.

Britizen 14.question: During the Victorian period, transport link improved, enabling goods and people to move easily around the country
Britizen 14.reference: The statement is true

Britizen 14.question: What was the more probable use for the Stonehenge?
Britizen 14.reference: Stonehenge, still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire. Stonehenge was probably a special gathering place for seasonal ceremonies

answers.text: A special gathering place for seasonal ceremonies

Britizen 14.question: John Constable (1776-1837) founded the modern Police force in England
Britizen 14.reference: John Constable (1776-1837) was a landscape painter most famous for his works of Dedham Vale on the Suffolk-Essex border in the east of England.

Britizen 14.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 14.question: When was the Parliament of Northern Ireland first established?
Britizen 14.reference: A Northern Ireland Parliament was established in 1922, when Ireland was divided, but it was abolished in 1972, shortly after the Troubles broke out in 1969. The Northern Ireland Assembly was established soon after the Belfast Agreement (or Good Friday Agreement) in 1998.

Britizen 14.question: How old do you have to be to buy a lottery ticket or a scratch card?
Britizen 14.reference: People under 18 (Changed in 2021) are not allowed to participate in the National Lottery. This includes buying Scratch Cards.

Britizen 15.question: When is St David's day?
Britizen 15.reference: St David's day is on the 1st of March. He is a Patron St of Wales.

Britizen 15.question: What did the Chartists campaign for?
Britizen 15.reference: The Chartists demanded the vote for the working classes and other people without property.

answers.text: The right to vote for the 21 year olds
The right to vote for the working class
The right to vote for the women
The right to vote for the 18 year olds

Britizen 15.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 15.question: What do you need to apply for UK citizenship?
Britizen 15.reference: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to: speak and read English; have a good understanding of life in the UK.

answers.text: Be able to use computers in English

Britizen 15.question: Who won gold medals in rowing in five consecutive Olympic Games?
Britizen 15.reference: Sir Steve Redgrave won gold medals in rowing in five consecutive Olympic Games and is one of Britain's greatest Olympians.

Britizen 15.question: When did the WWI begin?
Britizen 15.reference: The first World War began in 1914 and ended in 1918.

Britizen 15.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 15.question: Where did the supporters of Charles Edward Stuart come from?
Britizen 15.reference: Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie), the grandson of James II, landed in Scotland. He was supported by clansmen from the Scottish highlands and raised an army.

Britizen 15.question: What is the official name of the country?
Britizen 15.reference: The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland. 'Great Britain' refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

answers.text: The United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: What is the British Constitution often described as?
Britizen 15.reference: The British constitution is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as 'unwritten'. This is mainly because the UK, unlike America or France, has never had a revolution which led permanently to a totally new system of government.

Britizen 15.question: What was the population of the UK in 1998?
Britizen 15.reference: Population growth in the UK (Year, Population): (1600, Just over 4 million), (1700, 5 million), (1801, 8 million), (1851, 20 million), (1901, 40 million), (1951, 50 million), (1998, 57 million), (2005, Just under 60 million), (2010, Just over 62 million).

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: Which TWO words appear in the first two lines of the national anthem 'God save the Queen/King'?
Britizen 15.reference: The first verse is: 'God save our gracious King! Long live our noble King! God save the King! Send him victorious, Happy and glorious, Long to reign over us, God save the King!'.

Britizen 15.question: What % of the UK population does Wales account for?
Britizen 15.reference: As per 2021 census, the UK population at mid-year was estimated to be 67.0 million, an increase of 3.7 million (5.9%) on the population in mid-2011. Wales accounted for 3,064,000 people - just above 4.5% of the UK population.

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: Members in the Welsh Assembly can speak English and Welsh, and all publications of the Assembly are in both languages
Britizen 15.reference: The statement is true

Britizen 15.question: How old was the Queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart when her father died and she became Queen?
Britizen 15.reference: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called 'Mary, Queen of Scots') was a Catholic. She was only a week old when her father died and she became queen.

Britizen 15.question: When was the first public film shown in the UK?
Britizen 15.reference: Films were first shown publicly in the UK in 1896 and film screenings very quickly became popular. From the beginning, film makers became famous for clever special effects and this continues to be an area of British expertise.

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: What is the name given to the PM's country house?
Britizen 15.reference: The official home of the Prime Minister is 10 Downing Street, in central London, near the Houses of Parliament. He or she also has a country house outside London called Chequers.

Britizen 15.question: How long did the Hundred Years War last for?
Britizen 15.reference: English kings also fought a long war with France, called the Hundred Years War (even though it actually lasted 116 years). One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V's vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: All Acts of Parliament are made in who's name?
Britizen 15.reference: The Monarch (King Charles III) has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year. On this occasion the King makes a speech which summarises the government’s policies for the year ahead. All Acts of Parliament are made in his name.

Britizen 15.question: In which year was there a major outbreak of plague in London?
Britizen 15.reference: During Charles II's reign, in 1665, there was a major outbreak of plague in London. Thousands of people died, especially in poorer areas. The following year, a great fire destroyed much of the city, including many churches and St Paul's Cathedral.

Britizen 15.question: Which TWO are facts about King William II of Scotland?
Britizen 15.reference: James II's elder daughter, Mary, was married to her cousin William of Orange, the Protestant ruler of the Netherlands. In 1688, important Protestants in England asked William to invade England and proclaim himself king. When William reached England, there was no resistance. James fled to France and William took over the throne, becoming William III in England, Wales and Ireland, and William II of Scotland.

answers.text: He was married to the young daughter of King James II of England, Wales and Ireland
He was defeated by King James II of England when the latter tried to take back the crown
He was William of Orange before he became King William III of England. Wales and Ireland
He was the Protestant rule of the Netherlands before he became King William II of Scotland

Britizen 15.question: Eid al-Fitr and Eid ul Adha are festivals celebrated by which religion?
Britizen 15.reference: Eid al-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month. They thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast. The date when it takes place changes every year. Muslims attend special services and meals. Eid ul Adha remembers that the prophet Ibrahim was willing to sacrifice his son when God ordered him to. It reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God. Many Muslims sacrifice an animal to eat during this festival. In Britain this has to be done in a slaughterhouse.

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: It is compulsory to join National Citizen Service programme for 16 and 17 year olds
Britizen 15.reference: There are many opportunities for young people to volunteer and receive accreditation which will help them to develop their skills. These include the National Citizen Service programme, which gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project.

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: Where are all minor criminal cases heard in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
Britizen 15.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates' Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court.

Britizen 15.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 15.question: What's the objective of the Commonwealth?
Britizen 15.reference: The Commonwealth is an association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development. Most member states were once part of the British Empire, although a few countries which were not have also joined.

answers.text: Achieve goals in democracy and development
Allow visa free migration across the member states

Britizen 15.question: Which one of the following is the most famous play written by Shakespeare?
Britizen 15.reference: Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England. He was a playwright and actor and wrote many poems and plays. His most famous plays include A Midsummer Night's Dream, Hamlet, Macbeth and Romeo and Juliet.

Britizen 16.question: What marked the beginning of 'constitutional monarchy'?
Britizen 16.reference: The laws passed after the Glorious Revolution are the beginning of what is called 'constitutional monarchy'.

Britizen 16.question: Who sat in the House of Lords in the middle ages?
Britizen 16.reference: The nobility, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords.

Britizen 16.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 16.question: Which of these charities works with children?
Britizen 16.reference: The National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC) works with children.

Britizen 16.question: Who won the Wars of the Roses?
Britizen 16.reference: This war was called the Wars of the Roses, because the symbol of Lancaster was a red rose and the symbol of York was a white rose. The war ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485. King Richard III of the House of York was killed in the battle and Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became King Henry VII.

Britizen 16.question: Which country(s) did Britain fight against during the Battle of Trafalgar?
Britizen 16.reference: Britain's navy fought against combined French and Spanish fleets, winning the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805.

Britizen 16.question: What is an important part of the UK character?
Britizen 16.reference: The traditions of comedy and satire, and the ability to laugh at ourselves, are an important part of the UK character.

answers.text: The ability to laugh at ourselves
To have fish and chips every lunch
Go to the pub every Friday

Britizen 16.question: When is the Bonfire Night celebrated?
Britizen 16.reference: Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays. The origin of this celebration was an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

Britizen 16.question: Where did many of the Viking settle after they got defeated?
Britizen 16.reference: The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England united under King Alfred the Great, who defeated the Vikings. Many of the Viking invaders stayed in Britain - especially in the east and north of England, in an area known as the Danelaw (many places names there, such as Grimsby and Scunthorpe, come from the Viking languages).

Britizen 16.question: Which flower is associated with Northern Ireland?
Britizen 16.reference: Shamrock is associated with Northern Ireland

Britizen 16.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 16.question: Civil servants are expected to be politically neutral, what core values should they have?
Britizen 16.reference: Civil servants are expected to carry out their role with dedication and a commitment to the civil service and its core values. These are: integrity, honesty, objectivity and impartiality (including being politically neutral).

Britizen 16.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 16.question: Who chairs the debates in the House of Commons?
Britizen 16.reference: Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker. This person is the chief Officer of the House of Commons. The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with constituents' problems like any other MP. The Speaker is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot.

Britizen 16.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 16.question: How many members does the Welsh Assembly have?
Britizen 16.reference: The Welsh Assembly has 60 members, with forty members chosen to represent individual Senedd constituencies, and twenty to represent the five electoral regions of the Senedd in Wales.

Britizen 16.question: On what day does 'Lent' begin?
Britizen 16.reference: The 40 days before Easter are known as Lent. It is a time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter. Traditionally, people would fast during this period and today many people will give something up, like a favourite food. The day before Lent starts is called Shrove Tuesday, or Pancake Day. Lent begins on Ash Wednesday.

Britizen 16.question: Select TWO famous Athletes
Britizen 16.reference: Sir Roger Bannister (1929-) was the first man in the world to run a mile in under four minutes, in 1954. Mo Farah (1983-) is a British distance runner, born in Somalia. He won gold medals in the 2012 Olympics for the 5,000 and 10,000 metres and is the first Briton to win the Olympic gold medal in the 10,000 metres.

Britizen 16.question: Sir Kingsley Amis was a famous English novelist and poet who wrote more than 20 novels, including 'Lucky Jim'
Britizen 16.reference: Sir Kingsley Amis (1922-95) was an English novelist and poet. He wrote more than 20 novels. The most well-known is Lucky Jim.

Britizen 16.question: What is the Church of England also known as in Scotland and the US?
Britizen 16.reference: In England, there is a constitutional link between Church and state. The official Church of the state is the Church of England (called the Anglican Church in other countries and the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States). It is a Protestant Church and has existed since the Reformation in the 1530s

Britizen 16.question: The Patron Saints' days are NOT public holidays in which TWO countries?
Britizen 16.reference: Only Scotland and Northern Ireland have their Patron Saint's day as an official holiday. While the Patron Saints' days are no longer public holidays in England and Wales, they are still celebrated. Parades and small festivals are held all over the two countries.

Britizen 16.question: What percentage of the workforce are women?
Britizen 16.reference: Women in Britain today make up about half of the workforce. On average, girls leave school with better qualifications than boys. More women than men study at university.

Britizen 16.question: What is a traditional pub game?
Britizen 16.reference: Pub quizzes are popular. Pool and darts are traditional pub games.

Britizen 16.question: When did a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes fail in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament?
Britizen 16.reference: Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays. The origin of this celebration was an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

Britizen 16.question: What tells us about how people lived just after the Norman Conquest?
Britizen 16.reference: William sent people all over England to draw up lists of all the towns and villages. The people who lived there, who owned the land and what animals they owned were also listed. This was called the Domesday Book. It still exists today and gives a picture of society in England just after the Norman Conquest.

Britizen 16.question: In 1913, the British government promised 'Home Rule' for Ireland, why was this postponed?
Britizen 16.reference: In 1913, the British government promised 'Home Rule' for Ireland. It was opposed by the Protestants in the north of Ireland, who threatened to resist Home Rule by force. The outbreak of the First World War led the British government to postpone any changes in Ireland. Irish Nationalists were not willing to wait and in 1916 there was an uprising (the Easter Rising) against the British in Dublin. A guerilla war against the British army and the police in Ireland followed. In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries.

answers.text: The Scottish people also wanted to self-govern
The outbreak of World War I

Britizen 16.question: The British constitution is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as 'unwritten'.
Britizen 16.reference: A constitution is a set of principles by which a country is governed. It includes all of the institutions that are responsible for running the country and how their power is kept in check. The constitution also includes laws and conventions. The British constitution is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as 'unwritten'.

Britizen 16.question: What did the Chartists campaign for?
Britizen 16.reference: In the 1830s and 1840s, a group called the Chartists campaigned for reform. They wanted six changes: for every man to have the vote; elections every year; for all regions to be equal in the electoral system; secret ballots; for any man to be able to stand as an MP; for MPs to be paid.

answers.text: Anyone to be able to stand as an MP
Every man to have a vote

Britizen 17.question: Which of the following is part of the UK?
Britizen 17.reference: The United Kingdom consists of Wales,England,Scotland and Northern Ireland.

Britizen 17.question: Who became one of the most popular monarchs in the 16th century?
Britizen 17.reference: Queen Elizabeth I became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada.

Britizen 17.question: When did the BBC start the first radio broadcast?
Britizen 17.reference: The BBC started radio broadcasts in 1922 and began the world's first regular television service in 1936.

Britizen 17.question: Where do the Laurence Olivier Awards take place?
Britizen 17.reference: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London. There are a variety of categories, including best director, best actor and best actress. The awards are named after the British actor Sir Laurence Olivier, later Lord Olivier, who was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays.

Britizen 17.question: Who were the first people to start living in Britain?
Britizen 17.reference: The first people to live in Britain were hunter-gatherers, in what we call the Stone Age.

Britizen 17.question: Where can you find Boudicca, the Queen of the Iceni statue?
Britizen 17.reference: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England. She is still remembered today and there is a statue of her on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses of Parliament.

Britizen 17.question: When did the 'Battle of the Boyne' happen?
Britizen 17.reference: James II wanted to regain the throne and invaded Ireland with the help of a French army. William defeated James II at the Battle of the Boyne in Ireland in 1690, an event which is still celebrated by some in Northern Ireland today. William re-conquered Ireland and James fled back to France.

Britizen 17.question: When did Queen Victoria become queen?
Britizen 17.reference: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18. She reigned until 1901, almost 64 years.

Britizen 17.question: Which British TV shows are popular in the UK now?
Britizen 17.reference: Popular programmes include regular soap operas such as Coronation Street and EastEnders.

Britizen 17.question: What was build as part of the UK's celebration of the new millennium?
Britizen 17.reference: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of the River Thames and is a Ferris wheel that is 443 feet (135 metres) tall. It was originally built as part of the UK's celebration of the new millennium and continues to be an important part of New Year celebrations.

Britizen 17.question: What became the symbol of the House of Tudor?
Britizen 17.reference: This war was called the Wars of the Roses, because the symbol of Lancaster was a red rose and the symbol of York was a white rose. The war ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485 the House of Lancaster won. The symbol of the House of Tudor was a red rose with a white rose inside it as a sign that the Houses of York and Lancaster were now allies.

Britizen 17.question: Which Patron Saint day is on the 17th of March?
Britizen 17.reference: England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland each have a national saint, called a patron saint. Each saint has a special day: 1 March: St David's Day, Wales;17 March: St Patrick's Day, Northern Ireland; 23 April: St George's Day, England; 30 November: St Andrew's Day, Scotland.

Britizen 17.question: After the Emancipation Act, how many Indian and Chinese workers were employed to replace the slaves?
Britizen 17.reference: The Emancipation Act of 1833 abolished slavery throughout British Empire. William Wilberforce was leading abolitionist and Quakers set up first anti-slavery groups. More than 2 million migrants came from India and China to replace labour force.

Britizen 17.question: Which TWO are NOT part of the UK?
Britizen 17.reference: The UK is made up of England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

Britizen 17.question: What do many theatres produce at Christmas time?
Britizen 17.reference: One British tradition is the pantomime. Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time. They are based on fairy stories and are light-hearted plays with music and comedy, enjoyed by family audiences. One of the traditional characters is the Dame, a woman played by a man. There is often also a pantomime horse or cow played by two actors in the same costume.

Britizen 17.question: Winston Churchill lost the General Election in 1945
Britizen 17.reference: Churchill was the son of a politician and, before becoming a Conservative MP in 1900, was a soldier and journalist. In May 1940 he became Prime Minister. He refused to surrender to the Nazis and was an inspirational leader to the British people in a time of great hardship. He lost the General Election in 1945 but returned as Prime Minister in 1951.

Britizen 17.question: Who was the monarch when the Book of Common Prayer was written to be used in the Church of England?
Britizen 17.reference: Henry VIII was succeeded by his son Edward VI, who was strongly Protestant. During his reign, the Book of Common Prayer was written to be used in the Church of England. A version of this book is still used in some churches today.

Britizen 17.question: Britain became fully democratic in the 19th century
Britizen 17.reference: The UK has had a fully democratic voting system since 1928 (20th century). The present voting age of 18 was set in 1969 and (with a few exceptions) all UK-born and naturalised adult citizens have the right to vote.

Britizen 17.question: When did motor-car racing start in the UK?
Britizen 17.reference: Motor-car racing in the UK started in 1902. The UK continues to be a world leader in the development and manufacture of motor-sport technology. A Formula 1 Grand Prix event is held in the UK each year and a number of British Grand Prix drivers have won the Formula 1 World Championship. Recent British winners include Damon Hill, Lewis Hamilton and Jenson Button.

Britizen 17.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 17.question: Which of these drugs can you buy or sell legally in UK?
Britizen 17.reference: Selling or buying drugs such as heroin, cocaine, ecstasy and cannabis is illegal in the UK.

Britizen 17.question: Which sport often refers to the phrase 'rain stopped play'?
Britizen 17.reference: You may come across expressions such as 'rain stopped play', 'batting on a sticky wicket', 'playing a straight bat', 'bowled a googly' or 'it's just not cricket', which have passed into everyday usage. The most famous cricket competition is the Ashes, which is a series of Test matches played between England and Australia.

Britizen 17.question: Who was Thomas Gainsborough?
Britizen 17.reference: Thomas Gainsborough (1727-88) was a portrait painter who often painted people in country or garden scenery.

answers.text: Was a landscape painter in modern style
Painted pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours
Was a portrait painter who often painted people in country or garden scenery
Is best known for his large bronze abstract sculptures

Britizen 17.question: What is the most famous competition which is a series of Test matches played between England and Australia?
Britizen 17.reference: Cricket originated in England and is now played in many countries. Games can last up to five days but still result in a draw! The idiosyncratic nature of the game and its complex laws are said to reflect the best of the British character and sense of fair play.
