You may come across expressions such as 'rain stopped play', 'batting on a sticky wicket', 'playing a straight bat', 'bowled a googly' or 'it's just not cricket', which have passed into everyday usage. The most famous competition is the Ashes, which is a series of Test matches played between England and Australia.

Britizen 18.question: Where did the people of the Bronze Age bury their dead?
Britizen 18.reference: People lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs called round barrows.

Britizen 18.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 18.question: What does the UK offer to its residents?
Britizen 18.reference: In return, the UK offers: freedom of belief and religion, freedom of speech, freedom from unfair discrimination, a right to a fair trial, a right to join in the election of a government.

Britizen 18.question: When were the women given the right to vote at the same age as men?
Britizen 18.reference: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the Women's Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. Shortly before Emmeline's death in 1928, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

Britizen 18.question: What is known as the Bessemer process?
Britizen 18.reference: The development of the Bessemer process for the mass production of steel led to the development of the shipbuilding industry and the railways.

Britizen 18.question: Which party formed the government in 2010?
Britizen 18.reference: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister.

Britizen 18.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 18.question: What organisations try to influence government policy?
Britizen 18.reference: Pressure and lobby groups are organisations which try to influence government policy. They play an important role in politics.

Britizen 18.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 18.question: Where are the youth cases heard in Scotland?
Britizen 18.reference: In Scotland a system called the Children's Hearings System is used to deal with children and young people who have committed an offence.

answers.text: There is a Children's Hearings System

Britizen 18.question: When did Scotland abolish the authority of the Pope?
Britizen 18.reference: Scotland had also been strongly influenced by Protestant ideas. In 1560, the predominantly Protestant Scottish Parliament abolished the authority of the Pope in Scotland and Roman Catholic religious services became illegal.

Britizen 18.question: Which play was written by Shakespeare?
Britizen 18.reference: Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England. He was a playwright and actor and wrote many poems and plays. His most famous plays include A Midsummer Night's Dream, Hamlet, Macbeth and Romeo and Juliet. Lines from his plays and poems which are often still quoted include: Once more unto the breach (Henry V); To be or not to be (Hamlet); A rose by any other name (Romeo and Juliet); All the world's a stage (As You Like It); The darling buds of May (Sonnet 18, Shall I Compare Thee To a Summer's Day).

Britizen 18.question: Which TWO scientists led a team which was the first to clone an animal?
Britizen 18.reference: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep. This has led to further research into the possible use of cloning to preserve endangered species and for medical purposes.

Britizen 18.question: When did the American colonies declare independence?
Britizen 18.reference: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments. The colonists eventually defeated the British army and Britain recognised the colonies' independence in 1783.

Britizen 18.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 18.question: The Monarch is responsible for the opening of the new parliamentary session each year
Britizen 18.reference: The Monarch has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year. On this occasion the Monarch makes a speech which summarises the government’s policies for the year ahead. All Acts of Parliament are made in her/his name.

Britizen 18.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 18.question: Who is the next heir to the throne?
Britizen 18.reference: Queen Elizabeth II reigned since her father's death in 1952 (in 2012 she celebrated her Diamond Jubilee - 60 years as queen) until she passed away in 2022. Her eldest son, King Charles III (previously the Prince of Wales) became the new monarch. His elder son, William, Prince of Wales is next in line.

Britizen 18.question: Henry VIII's fourth wife, Anne of Cleves was a Spanish princess
Britizen 18.reference: Anne of Cleves was a German princess. Henry married her for political reasons but divorced her soon after.

Britizen 18.question: In Elizabeth I's time, English settlers began to colonise which part of the world?
Britizen 18.reference: In Elizabeth I's time, English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America. The Elizabethan period is also remembered for the richness of its poetry and drama, especially the plays and poems of William Shakespeare.

Britizen 18.question: In which years did British Film Studios flourish?
Britizen 18.reference: British studios flourished in the 1930s. The 1950s and 1960s were a high point for British comedies, including Passport to Pimlico, The Ladykillers and, later, the Carry On films.

Britizen 18.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 18.question: The jury decides whether a person is 'guilty' or 'not guilty' based on what they have heard
Britizen 18.reference: The jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of 'guilty' or 'not guilty' based on what they have heard. In Scotland, a third verdict of 'not proven' is also possible. If the jury finds a defendant guilty, the judge decides on the penalty. The judge is only responsible for interpreting the law.

Britizen 18.question: How many Olympic gold medals has the Scottish cyclist, Sir Chris Hoy, won?
Britizen 18.reference: Sir Chris Hoy (1976-) is a Scottish cyclist who has won six gold and one silver Olympic medals. He has also won 11 world championship titles.

Britizen 18.question: During the WWII, the evacuation of more than 300,000 British and French soldiers from France gave rise to which phrase?
Britizen 18.reference: Many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats from Britain helped the Navy to rescue more than 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. Although many lives and a lot of equipment were lost, the evacuation was a success and meant that Britain was better able to continue the fight against the Germans. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase 'the Dunkirk spirit'.

Britizen 18.question: Which film or film franchise produced in UK has been the biggest gross earner?
Britizen 18.reference: Many of the films now produced in the UK are made by foreign companies, using British expertise. Some of the most commercially successful films of all time, including the two highest-grossing film franchises (Harry Potter and James Bond), have been produced in the UK.

Britizen 18.question: What is the reason for more ageing population? Choose Two Answers
Britizen 18.reference: People in the UK are living longer than ever before. This is due to improved living standards and better health care. There are now a record number of people aged 85 and over. This has an impact on the cost of pensions and health care.

Britizen 18.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 18.question: What is the National Anthem of the UK?
Britizen 18.reference: The National Anthem of the UK is 'God Save the King/Queen'. It is played at important national occasions and at events attended by the King/Queen or the Royal Family. The first verse is: 'God save our gracious King! Long live our noble King! God save the King! Send him victorious, Happy and glorious, Long to reign over us, God save the King!'.

Britizen 18.question: What did the 'Bill of Rights' confirm?
Britizen 18.reference: The Bill of Rights, 1689, confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king's power.

Britizen 19.question: When did people learn how to make bronze?
Britizen 19.reference: Around 4,000 years ago, people learned to make bronze

Britizen 19.question: When is Valentine's Day?
Britizen 19.reference: Valentine's Day, 14 February, is when lovers exchange cards and gifts. Sometimes people send anonymous cards to someone they secretly admire.

Britizen 19.question: Which Scottish poet wrote 'The Bruce'?
Britizen 19.reference: In Scotland, many people continued to speak Gaelic and the Scots language also developed. A number of poets began to write in the Scots language. One example is John Barbour, who wrote The Bruce about the Battle of Bannockburn.

Britizen 19.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 19.question: How often do you have to take the MOT test for an old vehicle?
Britizen 19.reference: If your vehicle is over three years old, you must take it for a Ministry of Transport (MOT) test every year. It is an offence not to have an MOT certificate if your vehicle is more than three years old.

Britizen 19.question: When did Britain become separated from the continent?
Britizen 19.reference: Britain only became permanently separated from the continent by the Channel about 10,000 years ago.

Britizen 19.question: When did many Scottish people leave for North America?
Britizen 19.reference: A process began which became known as the 'Highland Clearances'. Many Scottish landlords destroyed individual small farms (known as 'crofts') to make space for large flocks of sheep and cattle. Evictions became very common in the early 19th century. Many Scottish people left for North America at this time.

Britizen 19.question: What is the most famous rugby tournament?
Britizen 19.reference: The most famous rugby union competition is the Six Nations Championship between England, Ireland, Scotland, Wales, France and Italy. The Super League is the most well-known rugby league (club) competition.

Britizen 19.question: Where can you see the 'Bayeux Tapestry' today?
Britizen 19.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror. The battle is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

Britizen 19.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 19.question: What is an example of a Criminal Offence?
Britizen 19.reference: Examples of criminal laws are: Carrying a weapon, selling or buying drugs, racial crime, selling tobacco to under 18s, smoking/drinking in public in inappropriate places, buying alcohol for under 18s.

Britizen 19.question: Who was Isambard Kingdom Brunel?
Britizen 19.reference: Brunel was originally from Portsmouth, England. He was an engineer who built tunnels, bridges, railway lines and ships.

Britizen 19.question: The Union Flag is made up of Welsh, Scottish, Irish and English crosses
Britizen 19.reference: The Union Flag consists of three crosses: The cross of St George, patron saint of England, is a red cross on a white ground; The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland, is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground; The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland, is a diagonal red cross on a white ground.

Britizen 19.question: Scotland and Wales remained free of Anglo-Saxon rule
Britizen 19.reference: Parts of the west of Britain, including much of what is now Wales, and Scotland, remained free of Anglo-Saxon rule.

Britizen 19.question: Oliver Cromwell took the title 'King of Ireland'
Britizen 19.reference: Cromwell was given the title of Lord Protector and ruled until his death in 1658. Henry VIII took the title 'King of Ireland'

Britizen 19.question: On Valentine's Day people exchange gifts and cards and send anonymous cards to people they secretly admire
Britizen 19.reference: Valentine's Day, 14 February, is when lovers exchange cards and gifts. Sometimes people send anonymous cards to someone they secretly admire.

Britizen 19.question: Who wrote music for George VI and Queen Elizabeth II?
Britizen 19.reference: Sir William Walton (1902-83) wrote a wide range of music, from film scores to opera. He wrote marches for the coronations of King George VI and Queen Elizabeth II but his best-known works are probably Facade, which became a ballet, and Balthazar's Feast, which is intended to be sung by a large choir.

Britizen 19.question: In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country's best loved novel
Britizen 19.reference: In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country's best loved novel

Britizen 19.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 19.question: When did hereditary peers lose the automatic right to attend the House of Lords?
Britizen 19.reference: Since 1999, hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords. They now elect a few of their number to represent them in the House of Lords.

Britizen 19.question: Which official holiday is celebrated on the 30th November in Scotland?
Britizen 19.reference: Only Scotland and Northern Ireland have their patron saint's day as an official holiday. Events are held across Scotland, Northern Ireland and the rest of the country, especially where there are a lot of people of Scottish, Northern Irish and Irish heritage.

Britizen 19.question: Who led the invasion of England in 1066?
Britizen 19.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror. The battle is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

Britizen 19.question: Who was Catherine of Aragon (the first of Henry VIII's six wives)?
Britizen 19.reference: Catherine was a Spanish princess. She and Henry had a number of children but only one, Mary, survived. When Catherine was too old to give him another child, Henry decided to divorce her, hoping that another wife would give him a son to be his heir.

Britizen 19.question: What is Sir Isaac Newton's famous published work?
Britizen 19.reference: His most famous published work was Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica ('Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy'), which showed how gravity applied to the whole universe. Newton also discovered that white light is made up of the colours of the rainbow

Britizen 19.question: Who is Sir Edward Elgar(1857-1934)?
Britizen 19.reference: Sir Edward Elgar (1857-1934) was born in Worcester, England. His best-known work is probably the Pomp and Circumstance Marches. March No 1 (Land of Hope and Glory) is usually played at the Last Night of the Proms at the Royal Albert Hall.

Britizen 19.question: Nick Park created the 'Wallace and Gromit' film. What kind of film was it?
Britizen 19.reference: Britain continues to be particularly strong in special effects and animation. One example is the work of Nick Park, who has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.

Britizen 20.question: What Palace was a cast-iron and plate-glass building originally erected in Hyde Park, London, England, to house the Great Exhibition of 1851?
Britizen 20.reference: British industry led the world in the 19th century. The UK produced more than half of the world's iron, coal and cotton cloth. The UK also became a centre for financial services, including insurance and banking. In 1851, the Great Exhibition opened in Hyde Park in the Crystal Palace, a huge building made of steel and glass. Exhibits ranged from huge machines to handmade goods. Countries from all over the world showed their goods but most of the objects were made in Britain.

Britizen 20.question: What percentage of the population has a grandparent born outside of the UK?
Britizen 20.reference: Post-war immigration means that nearly 10% of the population has a parent or grandparent born outside the UK.

Britizen 20.question: Where can you find the ship HMS Victory now?
Britizen 20.reference: HMS Victory, can be visited in Portsmouth. It was Admiral Nelson's ship.

Britizen 20.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 20.question: How long can you use an EU driving licence for?
Britizen 20.reference: If your driving licence is from a country in the European Union (EU), Iceland, Liechtenstein or Norway, you can drive in the UK for as long as your licence is valid, subject to UK licence renewal requirements (at the age of 70, or three years after the driver becomes resident). If you have a licence from any other country, you may use it in the UK for up to 12 months.

Britizen 20.question: What does the Turner Prize celebrate?
Britizen 20.reference: The Turner Prize was established in 1984 and celebrates contemporary art.

Britizen 20.question: Who discovered Insulin?
Britizen 20.reference: The Scottish physician and researcher John MacLeod (1876-1935) was the co-discoverer of insulin, used to treat diabetes.

Britizen 20.question: Which of these is an Anglo-Saxon poem?
Britizen 20.reference: The Anglo-Saxon poem 'Beowulf' tells of its hero's battles against monsters and is still translated into modern English.

Britizen 20.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 20.question: Which court deals with the most serious cases in Scotland?
Britizen 20.reference: The most serious cases in Scotland, such as murder, are heard at a High Court with a judge and jury.

Britizen 20.question: What was the 'Black Death'?
Britizen 20.reference: In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. This was known as the Black Death. One third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales.

Britizen 20.question: The Bill of Rights confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the King's power
Britizen 20.reference: The Bill of Rights, 1689, confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king's power.

Britizen 20.question: When William of Orange re-conquered Ireland (in 1690) he took the title 'William the Conqueror'
Britizen 20.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror.

Britizen 20.question: Why is 1928 an important date for women's rights?
Britizen 20.reference: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the Women's Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. In 1918, women over the age of 30 were given voting rights. Shortly before Emmeline's death in 1928, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

answers.text: Women could vote at 21, the same age as men
Women could vote at 18, the same age as men
Women could vote if they were over 30 years old

Britizen 20.question: English laws and the English language were introduced in Wales by the middle of which century?
Britizen 20.reference: By the middle of the 15th century the last Welsh rebellions had been defeated. English laws and the English language were introduced.

Britizen 20.question: The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished glassworkers
Britizen 20.reference: The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished metalworkers

Britizen 20.question: Whose powers were increased by the Bill of Rights of 1689?
Britizen 20.reference: The Bill of Rights, 1689, confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king's power. Parliament took control of who could be monarch and declared that the king or queen must be a Protestant. A new Parliament had to be elected at least every three years.

Britizen 20.question: What is Diwali?
Britizen 20.reference: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is often called the Festival of Lights. It is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs. It celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge. There are different stories about how the festival came about. There is a famous celebration of Diwali in Leicester.

Britizen 20.question: Charles Dickens, Thomas Hardy and Jane Austen are famous writers
Britizen 20.reference: They are all famous authors and writers.

Britizen 20.question: Where is the Royal Crescent is located?
Britizen 20.reference: In the 18th century, simpler designs became popular. The Scottish architect Robert Adam influenced the development of architecture in the UK, Europe and America. He designed the inside decoration as well as the building itself in great houses such as Dumfries House in Scotland. His ideas influenced architects in cities such as Bath, where the Royal Crescent was built.

Britizen 20.question: St Augustine led missionaries from Rome. Where did he spread Christianity?
Britizen 20.reference: The Anglo-Saxons were not Christians when they first came to Britain but, during this period, missionaries came to Britain to preach about Christianity. Missionaries from Ireland spread the religion in the north. The most famous of these were St Patrick, who would become the patron saint of Ireland and St Columba, who founded a monastery on the island of Iona, off the coast of what is now Scotland. St Augustine led missionaries from Rome, who spread Christianity in the south. St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

Britizen 20.question: Which pair won gold medals for Ice dancing at the Olympic Games in 1984 and in four consecutive world championships?
Britizen 20.reference: Jayne Torvill (1957-) and Christopher Dean (1958-) won gold medals for ice dancing at the Olympic Games in 1984 and in four consecutive world championships.

Britizen 20.question: Which TWO are associated with rugby?
Britizen 20.reference: The most famous rugby union competition is the Six Nations Championship between England, Ireland, Scotland, Wales, France and Italy. The Super League is the most well-known rugby league (club) competition.

Britizen 20.question: The band called Beatles was popular and some alteration to social laws occurred during which period?
Britizen 20.reference: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as 'the Swinging Sixties'. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music. Two well-known pop music groups at the time were The Beatles and The Rolling Stones. It was also a time when social laws were liberalised, for example in relation to divorce and to abortion in England, Wales and Scotland. The position of women in the workplace also improved.

Britizen 21.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 21.question: What is the official report that contains everything said in Parliament called?
Britizen 21.reference: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard.

Britizen 21.question: What did the Statute of Rhuddlan do?
Britizen 21.reference: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

Britizen 21.question: Which of these was a British plane in WWII?
Britizen 21.reference: The most important planes used by the Royal Air Force in the Battle of Britain were the Spitfire and the Hurricane - which were designed and built in Britain.

Britizen 21.question: Which is not a Protestant Christian group?
Britizen 21.reference: There is no established Church in Wales or Northern Ireland. Other Protestant Christian groups in the UK are Baptists, Methodists, Presbyterians and Quakers. There are also other denominations of Christianity, the biggest of which is Roman Catholic.

Britizen 21.question: What is the capital of Scotland?
Britizen 21.reference: Edinburgh is the capital city of Scotland.

Britizen 21.question: When did the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms establish in Britain?
Britizen 21.reference: Britain was invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons. The languages they spoke are the basis of modern-day English. Battles were fought against these invaders but, by about AD 600, Anglo-Saxon kingdoms were established in Britain.

Britizen 21.question: Where can you visit Admiral Nelson's ship , HMS Victory?
Britizen 21.reference: Admiral Nelson's ship, HMS Victory, can be visited in Portsmouth

Britizen 21.question: What time do Public Houses open at?
Britizen 21.reference: Public houses (pubs) are an important part of the UK social culture. Pubs are usually open during the day from 11.00 am (12 noon on Sundays).

Britizen 21.question: A very impressive hill fort can still be seen today at Maiden Castle, what English county does it stand in?
Britizen 21.reference: A very impressive hill fort can still be seen today at Maiden Castle, in the English county of Dorset.

Britizen 21.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 21.question: On election day, you will get your ballot paper, which you take to a polling booth to fill in front of the polling officer
Britizen 21.reference: When you arrive at the polling station, the staff will ask for your name and address. In Northern Ireland you will also have to show photographic identification. You will then get your ballot paper, which you take to a polling booth to fill in privately

Britizen 21.question: Which popular yearly sporting event takes place between Oxford and Cambridge universities?
Britizen 21.reference: Rowing is also popular, both as a leisure activity and as a competitive sport. There is a popular yearly race on the Thames between Oxford and Cambridge Universities.

Britizen 21.question: Dame Ellen MacArthur is famous for her achievements in which sport?
Britizen 21.reference: Dame Ellen MacArthur (1976-) is a yachtswoman and in 2004 became the fastest person to sail around the world single handed.

Britizen 21.question: The UK has hosted the Olympic Games three times. When did the UK host the games in the 20th century? (Choose TWO)
Britizen 21.reference: The UK has hosted the Olympic Games on three occasions: 1908, 1948 and 2012. The main Olympic site for the 2012 Games was in Stratford, East London. The British team was very successful, across a wide range of Olympic sports, finishing third in the medal table.

Britizen 21.question: When did the first farmers start settling in Britain?
Britizen 21.reference: The first farmers arrived in Britain about 6,000 years ago. The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe. These people built houses, tombs and monuments on the land.

Britizen 21.question: What followed the Bronze Age?
Britizen 21.reference: The Bronze Age was followed by the Iron Age, when people learned how to make weapons and tools out of iron.

Britizen 21.question: James VI of Scotland was not related to Queen Elizabeth I of England
Britizen 21.reference: Mary was suspected of murdering her husband and fled to England. She gave her throne to her Protestant son, James VI of Scotland. Mary was Elizabeth I's cousin.

Britizen 21.question: Which of these flowers is closely associated with Remembrance Day?
Britizen 21.reference: The poppy has become the symbol of Remembrance worn during the weeks leading to Remembrance Sunday and Armistice Day.

Britizen 21.question: Who, in 1810 opened the first curry house in Britain called 'The Hindoostane Coffee House' in George Street, London?
Britizen 21.reference: In 1810 Sake Dean Mahomet opened the Hindoostane Coffee House in George Street, London. It was the first curry house to open in Britain. Mahomet and his wife also introduced 'shampooing', the Indian art of head massage, to Britain.

Britizen 21.question: Which TWO are correct about King Henry's wives?
Britizen 21.reference: 3 of the wives were called Catherine and 2 were called Anne.

answers.text: Two of the wives were sisters

Britizen 21.question: Ralph Vaughan Williams was strongly influenced by traditional English folk music
Britizen 21.reference: Ralph Vaughan Williams (1872-1958) wrote music for orchestras and choirs. He was strongly influenced by traditional English folk music.

Britizen 21.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 21.question: What's the Monarch's yearly parliamentary speech for?
Britizen 21.reference: The King has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year. On this occasion the Monarch makes a speech which summarises the government's policies for the year ahead. All Acts of Parliament are made in his name.

answers.text: Set out the government's legislative plans for the next year
Give advice to the general public on upcoming legislations
Summarise all of the last year's policies

Britizen 21.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 21.question: How can you arrange to get a National Insurance number?
Britizen 21.reference: If you have permission to work in the UK, you will need to telephone the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to arrange to get a National Insurance number.

answers.text: Contact the Department for Work and Pensions

Britizen 21.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 21.question: How can you contact your MP?
Britizen 21.reference: You can contact MPs by letter or telephone at their constituency Office, or at their Office in the House of Commons

Britizen 22.question: Where is the Cenotaph located?
Britizen 22.reference: The Cenotaph can be visited in London in Whitehall.

Britizen 22.question: What proportion of the population died because of the Black Death?
Britizen 22.reference: One third of the population of England and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales died because of the Black Death.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: How long does it take to give blood?
Britizen 22.reference: Donated blood is used by hospitals to help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses, it takes about an hour to do so.

Britizen 22.question: What country is Swansea in?
Britizen 22.reference: Swansea is a Welsh city.

Britizen 22.question: How is the New Year often called in Scotland?
Britizen 22.reference: Known in Scotland as 'The Bard', Robert Burns was a Scottish poet. His best-known work is probably the song Auld Lang Syne, which is sung by people in the UK and other countries when they are celebrating the New Year (or Hogmanay as it is called in Scotland).

Britizen 22.question: What did the Magna Carta do?
Britizen 22.reference: The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law. It protected the rights of the nobility and restricted the king's power to collect taxes or to make or change laws.

Britizen 22.question: Which of these is known as West End in London?
Britizen 22.reference: There are theatres in most towns and cities throughout the UK, ranging from the large to the small. London's West End, also known as 'Theatreland', is particularly well known.

Britizen 22.question: The King James' Bible is a new translation of the Bible into English
Britizen 22.reference: One achievement of King James' reign was a new translation of the Bible into English. This translation is known as the 'King James Version' or the 'Authorised Version'. It was not the first English Bible but is a version which continues to be used in many Protestant churches today.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: For which TWO subjects are the Secretaries of State responsible?
Britizen 22.reference: Secretaries of State are responsible for subjects such as education, health and defence.

Britizen 22.question: Which TWO kings believed in the 'Divine Right of Kings'?
Britizen 22.reference: James I and his son Charles I were less skilled politically. Both believed in the 'Divine Right of Kings': the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule. They thought that the king should be able to act without having to seek approval from Parliament.

Britizen 22.question: Which TWO of these aircrafts were used by Britain in WWII?
Britizen 22.reference: The most important planes used by the Royal Air Force in the Battle of Britain(1940) were the Spitfire and the Hurricane - which were designed and built in Britain.

Britizen 22.question: During Queen Elizabeth I's reign the English (1588) defeated a large fleet of ships. Where did the ships come from?
Britizen 22.reference: Elizabeth became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada (a large fleet of ships), which had been sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

Britizen 22.question: People don't celebrate Christmas as much as they used to before
Britizen 22.reference: Christmas remains very popular in the UK. People usually spend the day at home and eat a special meal, which often includes roast turkey, Christmas pudding and mince pies.

Britizen 22.question: Which TWO British Actors have recently won Oscars?
Britizen 22.reference: British actors continue to be popular and continue to win awards throughout the world. Recent British actors to have won Oscars include Colin Firth, Sir Anthony Hopkins, Dame Judi Dench, Kate Winslet and Tilda Swinton.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: Which of these statements is correct?
Britizen 22.reference: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish Republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for Public Office. There are some exceptions, including: members of the armed forces; civil servants; people found guilty of certain criminal offences.

answers.text: People found guilty of all criminal offences cannot stand for Public Office
People found guilty of certain criminal offences cannot stand for Public Office

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: In Northern Ireland, Deputy District Judges hear minor criminal cases
Britizen 22.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates' Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court. In Northern Ireland, cases are heard by a District Judge or Deputy District Judge, who is legally qualified and paid.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: Big Ben was named 'Elizabeth Tower' in honour of Queen Elizabeth I
Britizen 22.reference: Big Ben is the nickname for the great bell of the clock at the Houses of Parliament in London. Many people call the clock Big Ben as well. The clock is over 150 years old and is a popular tourist attraction. The clock tower is named 'Elizabeth Tower' in honour of Queen Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee in 2012.

Britizen 22.question: The Lake District is the largest national park in Scotland
Britizen 22.reference: The Lake District is England's largest national park. It covers 885 square miles (2,292 square kilometres). It is famous for its lakes and mountains and is very popular with climbers, walkers and sailors. The biggest stretch of water is Windermere. In 2007, television viewers voted Wastwater as Britain's favourite view.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: What is the minimum age for going into betting shops or gambling clubs?
Britizen 22.reference: You have to be 18 to go into betting shops or gambling clubs.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: What is the name of the charity that works to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK?
Britizen 22.reference: Many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open by the National Trust in England, Wales and Northern Ireland and the National Trust for Scotland. Both are charities that work to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK. The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers. There are now more than 61,000 volunteers helping to keep the organisation running.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: Every MP in the UK has his/her own constituency
Britizen 22.reference: Voters in each constituency elect their member of Parliament (MP) in a General Election. All of the elected MPs from the House of Commons. Most MPs belong to a political party, and the party with the majority of MPs forms the government.

Britizen 22.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 22.question: In the UK, under the Individual Electoral Registration (IER) system, every eligible person is individually responsible for their own registration.
Britizen 22.reference: The Individual Electoral Registration system replaced the old "head of household" method where one person could register everyone in the home. Now, each eligible person is legally responsible for their own registration.

Britizen 22.question: Who helped found the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU) in 1903?
Britizen 22.reference: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the Women's Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. In 1903 she helped found the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU).

Britizen 22.question: The Union Jack is comprised of four countries' crosses. They are: England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland.
Britizen 22.reference: One symbol of the union between England, Scotland, Wales and Ireland was a new version of the official flag, the Union Flag. This is often called the Union Jack. The flag combined crosses associated with England, Scotland and Ireland. It is still used today as the official flag of the UK.

Britizen 23.question: Who was one of the founders of England's naval tradition?
Britizen 23.reference: Sir Francis Drake, one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada, was one of the founders of England's naval tradition. His ship (The Golden Hind) was one of the first to sail around the world.

Britizen 23.question: How many Houses did the Scottish Parliament have in the middle ages?
Britizen 23.reference: In the middle ages the Scottish Parliament had three Houses, called Estates: the lords, the commons and the clergy.

Britizen 23.question: Which clan was killed because of not taking an oath?
Britizen 23.reference: All Scottish clans were required formally to accept William as king by taking an oath. The MacDonalds of Glencoe were late in taking the oath and were all killed.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: What information about you is asked at the polling station?
Britizen 23.reference: When you arrive at the polling station, the staff will ask for your name and address. In Northern Ireland you will also have to show photographic identification.

answers.text: Your name, contact details and age

Britizen 23.question: What treaty was signed in 1707?
Britizen 23.reference: The Act of Union, known as the Treaty of Union in Scotland, was therefore agreed in 1707, creating the Kingdom of Great Britain.

answers.text: The Act for Government of Wales

Britizen 23.question: Which was the last successful invasion of Britain?
Britizen 23.reference: The Norman Conquest was the last successful foreign invasion of England and led to many changes in government and social structures in England.

Britizen 23.question: When was the time of growing patriotism?
Britizen 23.reference: The Elizabethan period in England was a time of growing patriotism: a feeling of pride in being English. English explorers sought new trade routes and tried to expand British trade into the Spanish colonies in the Americas.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: Which TWO changes did the Chartists campaign for?
Britizen 23.reference: In the 1830s and 1840s, a group called the Chartists campaigned for reform. They wanted six changes: for every man to have the vote; elections every year; for all regions to be equal in the electoral system; secret ballots; for any man to be able to stand as an MP; for MPs to be paid.

answers.text: For every woman to have the vote

Britizen 23.question: The Corn Laws were repealed in 1846. These had prevented the import of cheap grain
Britizen 23.reference: The statement is true.

Britizen 23.question: What are Halloween lanterns traditionally made out of?
Britizen 23.reference: Halloween, 31 October, is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter. Young people will often dress up in frightening costumes to play 'trick or treat'. People give them treats to stop them playing tricks on them. A lot of people carve lanterns out of pumpkins and put a candle inside.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: The Speaker also represents Parliament on ceremonial occasions
Britizen 23.reference: The Speaker keeps order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed. This includes making sure the opposition (see The government) has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses. The Speaker also represents Parliament on ceremonial occasions.

Britizen 23.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 23.question: You must treat everyone equally, regardless of sex, race, age, religion, disability, class or sexual orientation
Britizen 23.reference: The statement is true.

Britizen 23.question: In the 2021 England and Wales census, what percentage of people identified themselves as Christian?
Britizen 23.reference: The 2021 Census results released so far show that 46% of people in England and Wales (from a total sampled 60 million) identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), 37% stated not to follow any religion and 0.5% of people followed another religion

Britizen 23.question: At the beginning of the Middle Ages, Ireland was an independent country
Britizen 23.reference: At the beginning of the Middle Ages, Ireland was an independent country. The English first went to Ireland as troops to help the Irish king and remained to build their own settlements. By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin. Some of the important lords in other parts of Ireland accepted the authority of the English king.

Britizen 23.question: Who led the Scottish, in 1314, to defeat the English at the Battle of Bannockburn?
Britizen 23.reference: In Scotland, the English kings were less successful. In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn, and Scotland remained unconquered by the English.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: The Reform Act of 1832 took away the automatic right of hereditary peers to attend the House of Lords
Britizen 23.reference: Since 1999, hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords. The Reform Act of 1832 had greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote. The Act also abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs and more parliamentary seats were given to the towns and cities.

Britizen 23.question: The chairperson of the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland is the Moderator, who is appointed for the whole lifetime to speak on behalf of that Church.
Britizen 23.reference: In Scotland, the national Church is the Church of Scotland, which is a Presbyterian Church. It is governed by ministers and elders. The chairperson of the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland is the Moderator, who is appointed for one year only and often speaks on behalf of that Church.

Britizen 23.question: The Protestants formed their own churches during the Reformation
Britizen 23.reference: At the same time the Reformation was happening across Europe. This was a movement against the authority of the Pope and the ideas and practices of the Roman Catholic Church. The Protestants formed their own churches. They read the Bible in their own languages instead of in Latin; they did not pray to saints or at shrines; and they believed that a person's own relationship with God was more important than submitting to the authority of the Church. Protestant ideas gradually gained strength in England, Wales and Scotland during the 16th century.

Britizen 23.question: If you wish to be a permanent resident of the UK, you should look after yourself. In return you are guaranteed a job
Britizen 23.reference: You are not guaranteed a job if you settle in the UK.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: What did the Beveridge Report of 1942, by William Beveridge, provide the basis for?
Britizen 23.reference: William Beveridge (later Lord Beveridge) was a British economist and social reformer. He served briefly as a Liberal MP and was subsequently the leader of the Liberals in the House of Lords but is best known for the 1942 report Social Insurance and Allied Services (known as the Beveridge Report). The report was commissioned by the wartime government in 1941. It recommended that the government should find ways of fighting the five 'Giant Evils' of Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness and provided the basis of the modern welfare state.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: Which TWO are safe places to go and stay in for anyone facing domestic violence?
Britizen 23.reference: It is important for anyone facing domestic violence to get help as soon as possible. A solicitor or the Citizens Advice Bureau can explain the available options. In some areas there are safe places to go and stay in, called refuges or shelters.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: How are members of Parliament (MPs) elected?
Britizen 23.reference: MPs are elected through a system called 'first past the post'. In each constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected. The government is usually formed by the party that wins the majority of constituencies. If no party wins a majority, two parties may join together to form a coalition.

Britizen 23.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 23.question: The European Union was originally called the EEC
Britizen 23.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973. After UK's Brexit vote, there are now 27 EU member states.

Britizen 23.question: Which Admiral's Column is in Trafalgar Square, London?
Britizen 23.reference: Britain's navy fought against combined French and Spanish fleets, winning the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805. Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet at Trafalgar and was killed in the battle. Nelson's Column in Trafalgar Square, London, is a monument to him. His ship, HMS Victory, can be visited in Portsmouth.

Britizen 24.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 24.question: Which TWO are British Overseas territories?
Britizen 24.reference: There are also several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called 'Crown dependencies'. There are also several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands. They are also linked to the UK but are not a part of it.

Britizen 24.question: Which flag has a white cross on a blue background?
Britizen 24.reference: Scottish flag has a white cross on a blue background because St Andrew was the Patron Saint of Scotland.

Britizen 24.question: Where does the Fringe take place?
Britizen 24.reference: The Edinburgh Festival takes place in Edinburgh, Scotland, every summer. The most well-known is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe, also known as the Fringe.

Britizen 24.question: When did the Roman army leave Britain?
Britizen 24.reference: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned.

Britizen 24.question: What helped the progress of the industrial revolution?
Britizen 24.reference: Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt's work on steam power, helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

Britizen 24.question: What was the estimated population of the British Empire?
Britizen 24.reference: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

Britizen 24.question: How many times did Sir Jackie Stewart win the Formula 1 championship?
Britizen 24.reference: Sir Jackie Stewart (1939-) is a Scottish former racing driver who won the Formula 1 world championship three times.

Britizen 24.question: Who was R. A. Butler?
Britizen 24.reference: Richard Austen Butler (later Lord Butler) was born in 1902. He became a Conservative MP in 1923 and held several positions before becoming responsible for education in 1941.

Britizen 24.question: What county is 'Maiden Castle' in?
Britizen 24.reference: A very impressive hill fort can still be seen today at Maiden Castle, in the English county of Dorset.

Britizen 24.question: What country did Britain fight against during the Crimean War?
Britizen 24.reference: From 1853 to 1856, Britain fought with Turkey and France against Russia in the Crimean War. It was the first war to be extensively covered by the media through news stories and photographs. Many soldiers died from illnesses they caught in the hospitals, rather than from war wounds. Queen Victoria introduced the Victoria Cross medal during this war.

Britizen 24.question: The Medieval Period is referred to the period after the Norman Conquest up until what year?
Britizen 24.reference: The period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485 is called the Middle Ages (or the medieval period). It was a time of almost constant war.

Britizen 24.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 24.question: In Northern Ireland, minor cases are heard by a District Judge or Deputy District Judge, who is legally qualified and paid
Britizen 24.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates' Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court. In Northern Ireland, cases are heard by a District Judge or Deputy District Judge, who is legally qualified and paid.

Britizen 24.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 24.question: How long does it take to give blood?
Britizen 24.reference: Donated blood is used by hospitals to help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses. Giving blood only takes about an hour to do.

Britizen 24.question: Falkland Islands are a Crown Dependency
Britizen 24.reference: There are also several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called 'Crown dependencies'. There are also several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands. They are also linked to the UK but are not a part of it.

Britizen 24.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 24.question: Which of these statements is incorrect?
Britizen 24.reference: Owing money is a civil offence

answers.text: Selling or buying drugs such as heroin is illegal in the UK
It's a criminal offence to offend one's religion or ethnic origin
It's a criminal offence to carry a weapon of any kind
It's a criminal offence to owe someone money

Britizen 24.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 24.question: Which TWO countries took part in the development of Concorde?
Britizen 24.reference: The 1960s was also a time of technological progress. Britain and France developed the world's only supersonic commercial airliner, Concorde. New styles of architecture, including high-rise buildings and the use of concrete and steel, became common.

Britizen 24.question: Which of the following statements is correct?
Britizen 24.reference: The king's army was defeated at the Battles of Marston Moor and Naseby. By 1646, it was clear that Parliament had won the war. Charles was held prisoner by the parliamentary army. He was still unwilling to reach any agreement with Parliament and in 1649 he was executed.

answers.text: Charles I's army was defeated at the Battle of Marston Moor but won the Battle of Naseby
Parliament won the English Civil War

Britizen 24.question: What is the highest denomination of coins one can find in the UK?
Britizen 24.reference: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £). There are 100 pence in a pound. The denominations (values) of currency are - coins: 1p, 2p, 5p, 10p, 20p, 50p, £1 and £2; notes: £5, £10, £20, £50.

Britizen 24.question: Sir Ian Botham is famous for his achievements in which sport?
Britizen 24.reference: Sir Ian Botham (1955-) captained the English cricket team and holds a number of English Test cricket records, both for batting and for bowling.

Britizen 24.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 24.question: National Trust is a charity that works to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside
Britizen 24.reference: Many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open by the National Trust in England, Wales and Northern Ireland and the National Trust for Scotland. Both are charities that work to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK. The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers. There are now more than 61,000 volunteers helping to keep the organisation running.

Britizen 24.question: What character did Charlie Chaplin play?
Britizen 24.reference: Sir Charles (Charlie) Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character and was one of many British actors to make a career in Hollywood.

Britizen 24.question: Who ran 1 mile in 4 minutes in 1954?
Britizen 24.reference: Sir Roger Bannister (1929-) was the first man in the world to run a mile in under four minutes, in 1954.

Britizen 24.question: Queen Elizabeth I was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants
Britizen 24.reference: Mary was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants (for this reason, she became known as 'Bloody Mary'). Mary also died after a short reign and the next monarch was her half-sister, Elizabeth, the daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn.

Britizen 24.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 24.question: When did the UK join the EU?
Britizen 24.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973. After UK's Brexit vote, there are now 27 EU member states.

Britizen 25.question: Who defeated Napoleon at the Battle of Waterloo?
Britizen 25.reference: In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo.

Britizen 25.question: Who was the first Danish King?
Britizen 25.reference: The first Danish King was Cnut, also called Canute.

Britizen 25.question: How long is Hanukkah celebrated for?
Britizen 25.reference: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days. It is to remember the Jews' struggle for religious freedom. On each day of the festival a candle is lit on a stand of eight candles (called a menorah) to remember the story of the festival, where oil that should have lasted only a day did so for eight.

Britizen 25.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 25.question: How many 'local authorities' does the city of London have?
Britizen 25.reference: London has 33 local authorities, with the Greater London Authority and the Mayor of London coordinating policies across the capital.

Britizen 25.question: Who developed important Economics ideas?
Britizen 25.reference: Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish. Adam Smith developed ideas about economics which are still referred to today.

Britizen 25.question: What happens at 11:00 on the Remembrance Day?
Britizen 25.reference: Remembrance Day, 11 November, commemorates those who died fighting for the UK and its allies. Originally it commemorated the dead of the First World War, which ended on 11 November 1918. People wear poppies (the red flower found on the battlefields of the First World War). At 11.00 am there is a two-minute silence and wreaths are laid at the Cenotaph in Whitehall, London.

Britizen 25.question: What was the purpose of the Emancipation Act?
Britizen 25.reference: In 1807, it became illegal to trade slaves in British ships or from British ports, and in 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

answers.text: Abolish slavery in the British Empire
No one could be held prisoner unlawfully

Britizen 25.question: Who was the first 'Scottish King'?
Britizen 25.reference: In the north, the threat of attack by Vikings had encouraged the people to unite under one king, Kenneth MacAlpin. The term Scotland began to be used to describe that country.

Britizen 25.question: When was the famous satirical magazine 'Punch' published?
Britizen 25.reference: In the 19th century, satirical magazines began to be published. The most famous was 'Punch', which was published for the first time in the 1840s. Today, political cartoons continue to be published in newspapers, and magazines such as Private Eye continue the tradition of satire.

Britizen 25.question: Which TWO are used when making Haggis?
Britizen 25.reference: Scottish Traditional Food: Haggis, a sheep's stomach stuffed with offal, suet, onions and oatmeal.

Britizen 25.question: What are the 40 days before Easter known as?
Britizen 25.reference: The 40 days before Easter are known as Lent. It is a time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter. Traditionally, people would fast during this period and today many people will give something up, like a favourite food. The day before Lent starts is called Shrove Tuesday, or Pancake Day. Lent begins on Ash Wednesday.

Britizen 25.question: As per 2021 England and Wales census, what's the percetage of Jewish or Buddhist people in the UK?
Britizen 25.reference: In the 2021 England and Wales census, around 525,000 people from the sampled population of 60 million identified as Sikh. 46% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (6.5%), Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), 0.5% of people followed another religion and 37% stated not to follow any religion.

Britizen 25.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 25.question: To be able to vote in a parliamentary, local or European election, you must have your name on the electoral register
Britizen 25.reference: To be able to vote in a parliamentary, local or European election, you must have your name on the electoral register. If you are eligible to vote, you can register by contacting your local council electoral registration Office.

Britizen 25.question: In what year did the Troubles break out?
Britizen 25.reference: Some 3,000 people lost their lives in the decades after 1969 in the violence in Northern Ireland. The conflict between those wishing for full Irish independence and those wishing to remain loyal to the British government is often referred to as 'the Troubles'.

Britizen 25.question: The Welsh language, spoken in Wales and is taught in schools and universities, is a completely different language from English
Britizen 25.reference: The English language has many accents and dialects. In Wales, many people speak Welsh - a completely different language from English - and it is taught in schools and universities. In Scotland, Gaelic (again, a different language) is spoken in some parts of the Highlands and Islands, and in Northern Ireland some people speak Irish Gaelic.

Britizen 25.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 25.question: In which TWO countries were PCCs elected in?
Britizen 25.reference: In November 2012, the public elected Police and Crime Commissioners (PCCs) in England and Wales. These are directly elected individuals who are responsible for the delivery of an efficient and effective police force that reflects the needs of their local communities. PCCs set local police priorities and the local policing budget. They also appoint the Chief Constable.

Britizen 25.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 25.question: When did newspapers begin to operate without a government licence?
Britizen 25.reference: This was also an important time for the development of a free press (newspapers and other publications which are not controlled by the government). From 1695, newspapers were allowed to operate without a government licence. Increasing numbers of newspapers began to be published.

Britizen 25.question: In which country was Charles II residing before he was invited to England by the Parliament?
Britizen 25.reference: In May 1660, Parliament invited Charles II to come back from exile in the Netherlands. He was crowned King Charles II of England, Wales, Scotland and Ireland.

Britizen 25.question: During Queen Elizabeth's reign the British Empire became the largest empire the world had ever seen
Britizen 25.reference: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

Britizen 25.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 25.question: You need to be a member of a political party to be able to stand for election as an MP
Britizen 25.reference: Anyone aged 18 or over can stand for election as an MP but they are unlikely to win unless they have been nominated to represent one of the major political parties.

Britizen 25.question: You are not responsible for cleaning up after your pet in public places
Britizen 25.reference: The owner is responsible for keeping the dog under control and for cleaning up after the animal in a public place.

Britizen 26.question: Who was Henry Purcell?
Britizen 26.reference: Henry Purcell (1659-95) was the organist at Westminster Abbey. He wrote church music and operas.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: How many members does a jury have in Scotland?
Britizen 26.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland a jury has 12 members, and in Scotland a jury has 15 members.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: How many member states does the UN have?
Britizen 26.reference: The UK is part of the United Nations (UN), an international organisation with more than 190 countries as members.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: Which is an example of a Civil Offence?
Britizen 26.reference: Examples of civil laws are - Housing law: this includes disputes between landlords and tenants over issues such as repairs and eviction; Consumer rights: an example of this is a dispute about faulty goods or services; Employment law: these cases include disputes over wages and cases of unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace; Debt: people might be taken to court if they owe money to someone.

Britizen 26.question: Who built the Hadrian's Wall to keep out the Picts?
Britizen 26.reference: Areas of what is now Scotland were never conquered by the Romans, and the Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people).

Britizen 26.question: What is Diwali often called?
Britizen 26.reference: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is often called the Festival of Lights. It is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs. It celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge. There are different stories about how the festival came about. There is a famous celebration of Diwali in Leicester.

Britizen 26.question: What is the maximum fine if you watch TV without a valid TV licence?
Britizen 26.reference: You will receive a fine of up to £1,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.

Britizen 26.question: What does the York Minster have?
Britizen 26.reference: York Minster had windows of stained glass, telling stories about the Bible and Christian saints.

Britizen 26.question: Which was a crucial aerial battle against the Germans?
Britizen 26.reference: The Germans waged an air campaign against Britain, but the British resisted with their fighter planes and eventually won the crucial aerial battle against the Germans, called 'the Battle of Britain'.

Britizen 26.question: By which year did the English rule an area around Dublin called the Pale?
Britizen 26.reference: At the beginning of the Middle Ages, Ireland was an independent country. The English first went to Ireland as troops to help the Irish king and remained to build their own settlements. By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin. Some of the important lords in other parts of Ireland accepted the authority of the English king.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: Who appoints the members of the Cabinet?
Britizen 26.reference: The Prime Minister appoints about 20 senior MPs to become ministers in charge of departments which together form the cabinet.

Britizen 26.question: When were women given the right to vote at the same age as men?
Britizen 26.reference: In 1918, women over the age of 30 were given voting rights and the right to stand for Parliament, partly in recognition of the contribution women made to the war effort during the First World War. Shortly before Emmeline's death in 1928, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

Britizen 26.question: Which house won the War of the Roses?
Britizen 26.reference: The war ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485. King Richard III of the House of York was killed in the battle and Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became King Henry VII.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: How often are Elections for the European parliament held?
Britizen 26.reference: Elections for the European Parliament are also held every five years. Elected members are called members of the European Parliament (MEPs).

Britizen 26.question: Which TWO materials did the UK produce more than half of the world in the 19th century?
Britizen 26.reference: British industry led the world in the 19th century. The UK produced more than half of the world's iron, coal and cotton cloth.

Britizen 26.question: When was a Turing machine invented?
Britizen 26.reference: A Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54), a British mathematician, in the 1930s. The theory was influential in the development of computer science and the modern-day computer.

Britizen 26.question: Queen Elizabeth II was married to Prince Phillip
Britizen 26.reference: The statement is true

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: The leader of the Opposition usually becomes Prime Minister if his or her party wins the next General Election
Britizen 26.reference: The second-largest party in the House of Commons is called the opposition. The leader of the opposition usually becomes Prime Minister if his or her party wins the next General Election.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: The devolved governments do not have the power to legislate on any issues but can advise and encourage
Britizen 26.reference: Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control. However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

Britizen 26.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 26.question: The anthem 'God save the King/Queen' can only be played in presence of the Monarch
Britizen 26.reference: You can play the Anthem in anyone's presence

Britizen 26.question: What is the Cenotaph associated with?
Britizen 26.reference: After the First World War, Sir Edwin Lutyens was responsible for many war memorials throughout the world, including the Cenotaph in Whitehall. The Cenotaph is the site of the annual Remembrance Day service attended by the Queen/King, politicians and foreign ambassadors

Britizen 27.question: Which of these venues is located in Scotland?
Britizen 27.reference: The Scottish Exhibition and Conference Centre (SECC) is located in Glasgow, Scotland.

Britizen 27.question: Which of these are 'Crown Dependencies'?
Britizen 27.reference: The Channel Islands and the Isle of Man are closely related to the UK but are not part of it. They have their own governments and are called 'Crown dependencies'.

answers.text: The Channel Islands and the Isle of Man

Britizen 27.question: Who was the first woman Prime Minister?
Britizen 27.reference: In 1979, Margaret Thatcher became the first woman Prime Minister of the UK. She was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century, remaining in Office until 1990.

Britizen 27.question: When did the Romans successfully invade Britain?
Britizen 27.reference: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC. This was unsuccessful and for nearly 100 years Britain remained separate from the Roman Empire. In AD 43 the Emperor Claudius led the Roman army in a new invasion. This time, there was resistance from some of the British tribes but the Romans were successful in occupying almost all of Britain.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: What is the country house owned by the Prime Minister called?
Britizen 27.reference: The official home of the Prime Minister is 10 Downing Street, in central London, near the Houses of Parliament. He or she also has a country house outside London called Chequers.

Britizen 27.question: Who led the team of scientists to 'split the atom' for the first time?
Britizen 27.reference: Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford, working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to 'split the atom' and took part in the Manhattan Project in the United States, which developed the atomic bomb.

Britizen 27.question: When is Vaisakhi celebrated?
Britizen 27.reference: Vaisakhi (also spelled Baisakhi) is a Sikh festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa. It is celebrated on 14 April each year with parades, dancing and singing.

Britizen 27.question: What were the refugees that came from France between 1680 and 1720 called?
Britizen 27.reference: Between 1680 and 1720 many refugees called Huguenots came from France. They were Protestants and had been persecuted for their religion.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: Where can you get help from if you are facing domestic violence?
Britizen 27.reference: It is important for anyone facing domestic violence to get help as soon as possible. A solicitor or the Citizens Advice Bureau can explain the available options.

Britizen 27.question: Mary Stuart (also known as 'Mary, Queen of Scots') fled to England. She was suspected of trying to take over the throne and was kept a prisoner for 20 years
Britizen 27.reference: The statement is true.

Britizen 27.question: Areas of what is now Scotland were never conquered by the Romans
Britizen 27.reference: Areas of what is now Scotland were never conquered by the Romans, and the Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people).

Britizen 27.question: When did the First World War end?
Britizen 27.reference: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

Britizen 27.question: Which TWO of these are cycling athletes?
Britizen 27.reference: Sir Chris Hoy (1976-) is a Scottish cyclist who has won six gold and one silver Olympic medals. He has also won 11 world championship titles. Bradley Wiggins (1980-) is a cyclist. In 2012, he became the first Briton to win the Tour de France. He has won seven Olympic medals, including gold medals in the 2004, 2008 and 2012 Olympic Games.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: In Scotland, judges developed 'common law' by following previous decisions. It was referred to as 'unwritten'
Britizen 27.reference: In England, judges developed 'common law' by a process of precedence (that is, following previous decisions) and tradition. In Scotland, the legal system developed slightly differently and laws were 'codified' (that is, written down).

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: You need to buy a TV Licence if there are no TVs in your house but you watch TV programs on your mobile phone
Britizen 27.reference: You must pay for a licence to watch TV through any medium.

Britizen 27.question: Which TWO of these are English civil war battles?
Britizen 27.reference: During the civil war, the king's army was defeated at the Battles of Marston Moor and Naseby. By 1646, it was clear that Parliament had won the war. Charles was held prisoner by the parliamentary army. He was still unwilling to reach any agreement with Parliament and in 1649 he was executed.

Britizen 27.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 27.question: Which TWO of these are core values and responsibilities of the British society?
Britizen 27.reference: Although Britain is one of the world's most diverse societies, there is a set of shared values and responsibilities that everyone can agree with. These values and responsibilities include: to obey and respect the law; to be aware of the rights of others and respect those rights; to treat others with fairness; to behave responsibly; to help and protect your family; to respect and preserve the environment; to treat everyone equally, regardless of sex, race, religion, age, disability, class or sexual orientation; to work to provide for yourself and your family; to help others; to vote in local and national government elections.

answers.text: Always share the same views as your partner
To vote in local and national government elections
To work to provide for yourself and your family

Britizen 27.question: There is a dragon on the official flag of Wales
Britizen 27.reference: here is also an official Welsh flag, which shows a Welsh dragon. The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already united with England.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: Which TWO of these roles, in the UK, are unpaid?
Britizen 27.reference: You can volunteer with the police, and become a special constable or a lay (non-police) representative. You can also apply to become a Magistrate.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: You have to be 21 years old to stand as an MP
Britizen 27.reference: Anyone aged 18 or over can stand for election as an MP but they are unlikely to win unless they have been nominated to represent one of the major political parties. These are the Conservative Party, the Labour Party, the Liberal Democrats, or one of the parties representing Scottish, Welsh or Northern Irish interests.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: Where are the minor criminal cases dealt with in England, Wales and N. Ireland?
Britizen 27.reference: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates' Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court.

Britizen 27.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 27.question: The National Citizen Service programme gives 16 and 17 year olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills
Britizen 27.reference: There are many opportunities for young people to volunteer and receive accreditation which will help them to develop their skills. These include the National Citizen Service programme, which gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project

Britizen 28.question: What was the population of the UK in 1901?
Britizen 28.reference: The population of the UK was 40 million in 1901.

Britizen 28.question: When was the first coin in Britain made?
Britizen 28.reference: The Iron Age people made the first coins to be minted in Britain, some inscribed with the names of Iron Age kings.

Britizen 28.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 28.question: What programme helps teenagers develop their skills?
Britizen 28.reference: There are many opportunities for young people to volunteer and receive accreditation which will help them to develop their skills. These include the National Citizen Service programme, which gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project.

Britizen 28.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 28.question: When were women given the right to vote at the age of 18?
Britizen 28.reference: By 1918, the voting franchise was extended to women over 30, and then in 1928 to men and women over 21. In 1969, the voting age was reduced to 18 for men and women.

Britizen 28.question: Which of these words is based on Norman words?
Britizen 28.reference: Some words in modern English - for example, 'park' and 'beauty' - are based on Norman French words. Others - for example, 'apple', 'cow' and 'summer' - are based on Anglo-Saxon words.

Britizen 28.question: Where is the Hadrian Wall?
