Britizen 28.reference: Areas of what is now Scotland were never conquered by the Romans, and the Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people).

Britizen 28.question: The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries
Britizen 28.reference: The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries.

Britizen 28.question: Puritans are a group of Catholics who advocate complex religious doctrine and worship
Britizen 28.reference: Puritans, a group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship.

Britizen 28.question: Easter eggs are often given as presents at Easter as a symbol of new life
Britizen 28.reference: Easter is also celebrated by people who are not religious. 'Easter eggs' are chocolate eggs often given as presents at Easter as a symbol of new life.

Britizen 28.question: How many counties in Ireland remained in the UK under Northern Ireland?
Britizen 28.reference: In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries. The six counties in the north which were mainly Protestant remained part of the UK under the name Northern Ireland. The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State. It had its own government and became a republic in 1949.

Britizen 28.question: Why is 1969 a very important year in British history?
Britizen 28.reference: The voting franchise was also extended to women over 30, and then in 1928 to men and women over 21. In 1969, the voting age was reduced to 18 for men and women.

answers.text: Women were given the right to vote at the age of 30
Women were given the right to vote at the age of 18
Women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, same as men
Women were given the right to vote

Britizen 28.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 28.question: Blood and organ donations are compulsory in the UK
Britizen 28.reference: it's a donation, thus its not compulsory. Donated blood is used by hospitals to help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses. Giving blood only takes about an hour to do.

Britizen 28.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 28.question: How can you help looking after the environment?
Britizen 28.reference: It is important to recycle as much of your waste as you can. Using recycled materials to make new products uses less energy and means that we do not need to extract more raw materials from the earth. It also means that less rubbish is created, so the amount being put into landfill is reduced.

Britizen 28.question: Which TWO industries developed during the Great Depression?
Britizen 28.reference: During the Great Depression (1929),he traditional heavy industries such as shipbuilding were badly affected but new industries - including the automobile and aviation industries - developed.

Britizen 28.question: What is the currency used in the UK?
Britizen 28.reference: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £). There are 100 pence in a pound.

Britizen 28.question: Which TWO are associated with the 'Dunkirk Spirit'?
Britizen 28.reference: Many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats from Britain helped the Navy to rescue more than 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. Although many lives and a lot of equipment were lost, the evacuation was a success and meant that Britain was better able to continue the fight against the Germans. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase 'the Dunkirk spirit'.

answers.text: Small boats coming to the rescue

Britizen 28.question: George Frederick wrote which oratorio, which is sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter time?
Britizen 28.reference: The German-born composer George Frideric Handel (1695-1759) spent many years in the UK and became a British citizen in 1727. He wrote the Water Music for King George I and Music for the Royal Fireworks for his son, George II. Both these pieces continue to be very popular. Handel also wrote an oratorio, Messiah, which is sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter time.

Britizen 28.question: How many ski centres are there in Scotland?
Britizen 28.reference: There are five ski centres in Scotland, as well as Europe's longest dry ski slope near Edinburgh.

Britizen 28.question: What type of literature are 'The Canterbury Tales' written by Geoffrey Chaucer?
Britizen 28.reference: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. The people decided to tell each other stories on the journey, and the poems describe the travellers and some of the stories they told. This collection of poems is called The Canterbury Tales. It was one of the first books to be printed by William Caxton, the first person in England to print books using a printing press. Many of the stories are still popular. Some have been made into plays and television programmes.

Britizen 28.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 28.question: The Scottish Parliament has 33 members
Britizen 28.reference: There are 129 members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs), elected by a form of proportional representation. The Scottish Parliament can pass laws for Scotland on all matters which are not specifically reserved to the UK Parliament.

Britizen 28.question: What did the Queen Elizabeth II celebrate in 2012?
Britizen 28.reference: Queen Elizabeth II celebrated her Diamond Jubilee in 2012. The clock tower 'Big Ben' was named 'Elizabeth Tower' in honour of this event.

Britizen 29.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 29.question: Who is currently the Head of State of the UK?
Britizen 29.reference: King Charles III is the head of state of the UK. He is also the monarch or head of state for many countries in the Commonwealth.

Britizen 29.question: Why was Mary known as 'Bloody Mary'?
Britizen 29.reference: Mary was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants.

answers.text: She put her sister in prison

Britizen 29.question: What was encouraged to develop the UK economy in 1950?
Britizen 29.reference: During the 1950s, there was still a shortage of labour in the UK. Further immigration was therefore encouraged for economic reasons, and many industries advertised for workers from overseas.

Britizen 29.question: Which of these countries was NOT UK's ally in the WWII?
Britizen 29.reference: The war was initially fought between the Axis powers (fascist Germany and Italy and the Empire of Japan) and the Allies

Britizen 29.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 29.question: The members of the Armed Forces can stand for Public Office
Britizen 29.reference: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish Republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for Public Office. There are some exceptions, including: members of the armed forces; civil servants; people found guilty of certain criminal offences.

Britizen 29.question: Where is the SECC located?
Britizen 29.reference: There are many large venues that host music events throughout the year, such as: Wembley Stadium; The O2 in Greenwich, south-east London; and the Scottish Exhibition and Conference Centre (SECC) in Glasgow.

Britizen 29.question: People of the Iron Age sometimes defended sites. What were these sites?
Britizen 29.reference: People still lived in roundhouses, grouped together into larger settlements, and sometimes defended sites called hill forts. A very impressive hill fort can still be seen today at Maiden Castle, in the English county of Dorset.

Britizen 29.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 29.question: How often are the European Parliament elections held?
Britizen 29.reference: Elections for the European Parliament are also held every five years. Elected members are called members of the European Parliament (MEPs). Elections to the European Parliament use a system of proportional representation, where seats are allocated to each party in proportion to the total number of votes it has won.

Britizen 29.question: How is the Church of England often called?
Britizen 29.reference: In England, there is a constitutional link between Church and state. The official Church of the state is the Church of England called the Anglican Church in other countries and the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States.

Britizen 29.question: What do the Brit Awards celebrate?
Britizen 29.reference: The Brit Awards is an annual event that gives awards in a range of categories, such as best British group and best British solo artist.

Britizen 29.question: Which TWO houses formed the House of Tudor?
Britizen 29.reference: King Richard III of the House of York was killed in the battle and Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became King Henry VII. Henry then married King Richard's niece, Elizabeth of York, and united the two families. Henry was the first king of the House of Tudor. The symbol of the House of Tudor was a red rose with a white rose inside it as a sign that the Houses of York and Lancaster were now allies.

Britizen 29.question: Which TWO are famous UK sports stars?
Britizen 29.reference: Sir Jackie Stewart (1939-) is a Scottish former racing driver who won the Formula 1 world championship three times. Sir Chris Hoy (1976-) is a Scottish cyclist who has won six gold and one silver Olympic medals. He has also won 11 world championship titles.

Britizen 29.question: Which Monarch entered the House of Commons to arrest 5 parliamentary leaders?
Britizen 29.reference: Charles I entered the House of Commons and tried to arrest five parliamentary leaders, but they had been warned and were not there. (No monarch has set foot in the Commons since.) Civil war between the king and Parliament could not now be avoided and began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

Britizen 29.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 29.question: Who appoints life peers to the House of Lords?
Britizen 29.reference: Life peers are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister.

Britizen 29.question: Which government was elected in 1945?
Britizen 29.reference: Clement Attlee was Winston Churchill's Deputy Prime Minister in the wartime coalition government and became Prime Minister after the Labour Party won the 1945 election. He was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951 and led the Labour Party for 20 years. Attlee's government undertook the nationalisation of major industries (like coal and steel), created the National Health Service and implemented many of Beveridge's plans for a stronger welfare state.

Britizen 29.question: Which TWO are famous British sailors?
Britizen 29.reference: A British sailor, Sir Francis Chichester, was the first person to sail single-handed around the world, in 1966/67. Two years later, Sir Robin Knox-Johnston became the first person to do this without stopping.

Britizen 29.question: Which TWO of the following are major horse-racing events in the UK?
Britizen 29.reference: Famous horse-racing events include: Royal Ascot, a five-day race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family; the Grand National at Aintree near Liverpool; and the Scottish Grand National at Ayr. There is a National Horseracing Museum in Newmarket, Suffolk.

Britizen 29.question: Which TWO are associated with a Bank Holiday?
Britizen 29.reference: Bank holidays are public holidays, when banks and many other businesses are closed for the day.

answers.text: A day when most people have an official day off
A holiday which is always on a Monday
A day off for all the banks in the country

Britizen 29.question: Which of the following is a William Wordsworth's poem?
Britizen 29.reference: All 4 options are poems, but all written by different poets.(Robert Browning, 1812-89 'Home Thoughts from Abroad'), (Lord Byron, 1788-1824 'She Walks in Beauty'), (William Blake, 1757-1827 'The Tyger'), (William Wordsworth, 1770-1850 'The Daffodils' - 'I wander'd lonely as a cloud, That floats on high o'er vales and hills, When all at once I saw a crowd, A host of golden daffodils').

Britizen 29.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 29.question: Pakistan is part of the Commonwealth
Britizen 29.reference: Pakistan is currently part of the Commonwealth

Britizen 29.question: Which of the following traditional heavy industries were badly affected during the great depression?
Britizen 29.reference: The effects of the depression of the 1930s were felt differently in different parts of the UK. The traditional heavy industries such as shipbuilding were badly affected but new industries - including the automobile and aviation industries - developed.

Britizen 30.question: Who designed the new St Paul Cathedral?
Britizen 30.reference: Christopher Wren designed a new St Paul Cathedral after the previous one was destroyed by a great fire in 1666.

Britizen 30.question: Who won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games?
Britizen 30.reference: Dame Kelly Holmes (1970-) won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games.

Britizen 30.question: What was the purpose of the Corn Laws in 1846?
Britizen 30.reference: Corn Laws in 1846 had prevented the import of cheap grain.

answers.text: To prevent the import of cheap grain

Britizen 30.question: Where did the Vikings come from?
Britizen 30.reference: The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway. They first visited Britain in AD 789 to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves.

Britizen 30.question: Which movement campaigned for the women's rights?
Britizen 30.reference: In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, an increasing number of women campaigned and demonstrated for greater rights and, in particular, the right to vote. They formed the women's suffrage movement and became known as 'suffragettes'.

Britizen 30.question: Who became famous in silent movies for his tramp character?
Britizen 30.reference: Sir Charles (Charlie) Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character and was one of many British actors to make a career in Hollywood.

Britizen 30.question: What do many theatres in the UK produce at Christmas time?
Britizen 30.reference: One British tradition is the pantomime. Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time. They are based on fairy stories and are light-hearted plays with music and comedy, enjoyed by family audiences. One of the traditional characters is the Dame, a woman played by a man.

Britizen 30.question: What does St Patrick's cross look like?
Britizen 30.reference: The Union Flag consists of three crosses: The cross of St George, patron saint of England, is a red cross on a white ground; The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland, is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground; The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland, is a diagonal red cross on a white ground.

answers.text: A red cross on a white ground
A diagonal red cross on a white ground
A diagonal white cross on a blue ground

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: The House of Lords is more independent of the government than the House of Commons
Britizen 30.reference: The House of Lords is normally more independent of the government than the House of Commons. It can suggest amendments or propose new laws, which are then discussed by MPs. The House of Lords checks laws that have been passed by the House of Commons to ensure they are fit for purpose.

Britizen 30.question: Who should you contact to get your National Insurance number?
Britizen 30.reference: You will need to telephone the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to arrange to get a National Insurance number.

Britizen 30.question: What is the name given to the conflict between those wishing for full Irish independence and those wishing to remain loyal to the UK government?
Britizen 30.reference: The conflict between those wishing for full Irish independence and those wishing to remain loyal to the British government is often referred to as 'the Troubles'.

Britizen 30.question: During the Middle Ages, which TWO wars were fought abroad?
Britizen 30.reference: During the Middle Ages, the English kings also fought a number of wars abroad. Many knights took part in the Crusades, in which European Christians fought for control of the Holy Land. English kings also fought a long war with France, called the Hundred Years War (even though it actually lasted 116 years).

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: Which party forms the government?
Britizen 30.reference: Most MPs belong to a political party, and the party with the majority of MPs forms the government. If one party does not get a majority, two parties can join together to form a coalition.

answers.text: The party with the majority of the members of Parliament (MPs)
The party appointed by the Monarch
The party with the most votes
The party which is the most popular

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: Who appoints the government, which most people have chosen in a democratic election?
Britizen 30.reference: The monarch does not rule the country but appoints the government, which the people have chosen in a democratic election. The monarch invites the leader of the party with the largest number of MPs, or the leader of a coalition between more than one party, to become the Prime Minister.

Britizen 30.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 30.question: Those who don't respect the law should not expect to be allowed to become permanent residents
Britizen 30.reference: If you don't respect the law you should not expect to be allowed to settle in the UK

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: How often do the PM's questions take place?
Britizen 30.reference: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government's failures and weaknesses. One important opportunity to do this is at Prime Minister's Questions, which takes place every week while Parliament is sitting

Britizen 30.question: The Anglo-Saxons established Christianity when they invaded Britain
Britizen 30.reference: The Anglo-Saxons were not Christians when they first came to Britain but, during this period, missionaries came to Britain to preach about Christianity.

Britizen 30.question: Which of these international events was held in London in 2012?
Britizen 30.reference: The UK has hosted the Olympic Games on three occasions: 1908, 1948 and 2012. The Paralympic Games for 2012 were also hosted in London.

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: Pressure groups and lobby groups are organisations which try to influence government policy
Britizen 30.reference: Pressure and lobby groups are organisations which try to influence government policy. They play an important role in politics.

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: The National Citizen Service provides military training to young people
Britizen 30.reference: The National Citizen Service programme, which gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project.

Britizen 30.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 30.question: The Monarch appoints the members of the Cabinet
Britizen 30.reference: The Prime Minister (PM) is the leader of the political party in power. He or she appoints the members of the Cabinet and has control over many important public appointments.

Britizen 30.question: Which TWO of the athletes are paralympians?
Britizen 30.reference: Ellie Simmonds (1994-) is a Paralympian who won gold medals for swimming at the 2008 and 2012 Paralympic Games and holds a number of world records. She was the youngest member of the British team at the 2008 Games. David Weir (1979-) is a Paralympian who uses a wheelchair and has won six gold medals over two Paralympic Games. He has also won the London Marathon six times.

Britizen 30.question: After Cromwell defeated Charles II at Worcester, where did Charles II hide?
Britizen 30.reference: The Scots had not agreed to the execution of Charles I and declared his son Charles II to be king. He was crowned king of Scotland and led a Scottish army into England. Cromwell defeated this army in the Battles of Dunbar and Worcester. Charles II escaped from Worcester, famously hiding in an oak tree on one occasion, and eventually fled to Europe.

Britizen 31.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 31.question: What is not a fundamental principle of British life?
Britizen 31.reference: Driving a car is NOT one of the fundamental principles of British life

Britizen 31.question: Which style of architecture became popular in the 19th century?
Britizen 31.reference: In the 19th century, the medieval 'gothic' style became popular once again.

Britizen 31.question: Who was the first man to be called the 'Prime Minister'?
Britizen 31.reference: The first man to be called 'Prime Minister' was Sir Robert Walpole, who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

Britizen 31.question: What were the Canterbury Tales about?
Britizen 31.reference: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. The people decided to tell each other stories on the journey, and the poems describe the travellers and some of the stories they told. This collection of poems is called The Canterbury Tales.

answers.text: The stories people told each other

Britizen 31.question: Who is Andy Murray?
Britizen 31.reference: Andy Murray (1987-) is a Scottish tennis player who in 2012 won the men's singles in the US Open. He is the first British man to win a singles title in a Grand Slam tournament since 1936. In the same year, he won Olympic gold and silver medals.

Britizen 31.question: How long does Diwali normally last for?
Britizen 31.reference: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is often called the Festival of Lights. It is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs. It celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge. There are different stories about how the festival came about. There is a famous celebration of Diwali in Leicester.

Britizen 31.question: What was the Spanish Armada?
Britizen 31.reference: Elizabeth became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada (a large fleet of ships), which had been sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: Which of the devolved administrations has been suspended a few times?
Britizen 31.reference: There has been a Welsh Assembly and a Scottish Parliament since 1999. There is a Northern Ireland Assembly, although this has been suspended on a few occasions.

Britizen 31.question: Who designed New Delhi to be the seat of government in India?
Britizen 31.reference: In the 20th century, Sir Edwin Lutyens had an influence throughout the British Empire. He designed New Delhi to be the seat of government in India. He was responsible for many war memorials throughout the world, including the Cenotaph in Whitehall.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: The UK has a constitutional monarchy
Britizen 31.reference: The UK has a constitutional monarchy. This means that the king or queen does not rule the country but appoints the government, which the people have chosen in a democratic election.

Britizen 31.question: Rudyard Kipling reflected the idea that the British Empire was a force for good
Britizen 31.reference: Kipling wrote books and poems set in both India and the UK. His poems and novels reflected the idea that the British Empire was a force for good. Kipling was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1907.

Britizen 31.question: When did the British troops leave Iraq?
Britizen 31.reference: Since 2000, British armed forces have been engaged in the global fight against international terrorism and against the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction, including operations in Afghanistan and Iraq. British combat troops left Iraq in 2009.

Britizen 31.topic: The Values and principles of the UK
Britizen 31.question: There is no place in British society for extremism or intolerance
Britizen 31.reference: The statement is true

Britizen 31.question: What is the national flower of Northern Ireland?
Britizen 31.reference: A Shamrock is the national flower of Northern Ireland.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: In England and Wales, the small claims procedure is used for claims of less than what amount?
Britizen 31.reference: The small claims procedure is an informal way of helping people to settle minor disputes without spending a lot of time and money using a lawyer. This procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales; £5,000 in Scotland and £5,000 in Northern Ireland (Changed from £3000).

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: Many people volunteer simply because they want to help other people
Britizen 31.reference: Volunteering and helping your community are an important part of being a good citizen. They enable you to integrate and get to know other people. It helps to make your community a better place if residents support each other.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: In Northern Ireland, what letter does a newly qualified driver has to display for one year after passing the test?
Britizen 31.reference: In Northern Ireland, a newly qualified driver must display an 'R' place (for restricted driver) for one year after passing the test.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: EEC was set up in 1957 by six countries
Britizen 31.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973. After the Brexit vote to leave in 2020, there are now 27 EU member states

Britizen 31.question: People over 70 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 75% discount
Britizen 31.reference: People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount. You will receive a fine of up to £1,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: There are some MPs (Members of Parliament) who do not represent any of the main political parties
Britizen 31.reference: There are a few MPs who do not represent any of the main political parties. They are called 'independents' and usually represent an issue important to their constituency.

Britizen 31.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 31.question: What should you do to make a complaint about the Police?
Britizen 31.reference: Anyone can make a complaint about the police by going to a police station or writing to the Chief Constable of the police force involved. Complaints can also be made to an independent body: the Independent Police Complaints Commission in England and Wales, the Police Complaints Commissioner for Scotland or the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland.

answers.text: Write to the Chief Constable of the Police force
Write a complaint letter to the House of Commons
Complain to the Police force directly

Britizen 32.question: How long did the Romans stay in Britain?
Britizen 32.reference: The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years.

Britizen 32.question: How old is Big Ben?
Britizen 32.reference: Big Ben, also known as 'Elizabeth Tower', is over 150 years of age.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: What must the police officer tell you if you get arrested?
Britizen 32.reference: A Police officer will tell you the reason for your arrest and you will be able to seek legal advice.

answers.text: To put your arms in the air

Britizen 32.question: When did Ireland split into 2 countries?
Britizen 32.reference: Ireland became two countries in 1922.

Britizen 32.question: When did England win the World Cup?
Britizen 32.reference: Bobby Moore (1941-93) captained the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966.

Britizen 32.question: Who led the Labour Government from 2007 to 2010?
Britizen 32.reference: Gordon Brown took over as Prime Minister in 2007 and led the Labour Party.

Britizen 32.question: Which Castles are in use to this day?
Britizen 32.reference: Castles were built in many places in Britain and Ireland, partly for defence. Today many are in ruins, although some, such as Windsor and Edinburgh, are still in use.

Britizen 32.question: How many National Parks are there in the UK?
Britizen 32.reference: There are 15 national parks in England, Wales and Scotland. They are areas of protected countryside that everyone can visit, and where people live, work and look after the landscape.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: NATO aims to maintain peace between all of its members
Britizen 32.reference: NATO is a group of European and North American countries that have agreed to help each other if they come under attack. It also aims to maintain peace between all of its members.

Britizen 32.question: First professional football clubs were formed in the late 19th century
Britizen 32.reference: its True. The first professional football clubs were formed in the late 19th century.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: There are 9 National Parks in the UK
Britizen 32.reference: The UK has a large network of public footpaths in the countryside. There are also many opportunities for mountain biking, mountaineering and hill walking. There are 15 national parks in England, Wales and Scotland. They are areas of protected countryside that everyone can visit, and where people live, work and look after the landscape.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: The Prime Minister is the head of the Commonwealth
Britizen 32.reference: The Monarch (King Charles III at the time of writing) is the ceremonial head of the Commonwealth, which currently has 56 member states.

Britizen 32.question: Who was the Queen of the Iceni? She is still remembered today and there is a statue of her on Westminster Bridge in London
Britizen 32.reference: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the Queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: Which of these statements is correct?
Britizen 32.reference: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

answers.text: During Queen Victoria's reign the British Empire became the largest empire the world has ever seen
During Queen Elizabeth's reign the British Empire became the largest empire the world has ever seen

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: If you think someone is trying to persuade you to join an extremist or terrorist cause, who should you notify?
Britizen 32.reference: If you think someone is trying to persuade you to join an extremist or terrorist cause, you should notify your local police force.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: How can you look after the environment? (Choose TWO)
Britizen 32.reference: It is important to recycle as much of your waste as you can. A good way to support your local community is to shop for products locally where you can. This will help businesses and farmers in your area and in Britain. It will also reduce your carbon footprint, because the products you buy will not have had to travel as far.

answers.text: Shop for products locally as this reduces your carbon footprint
Recycle as much as you can

Britizen 32.question: The Blitz is associated which event?
Britizen 32.reference: Despite winning the Battle of Britain, the German air force was able to continue bombing London and other British cities at night-time. This was called the Blitz.

answers.text: The UK and Germany bombing French cities
The UK evacuating troops from Dunkirk

Britizen 32.question: In which year was Britain's first woman Prime Minister, Margaret Thatcher, elected to join the parliament of United Kingdom?
Britizen 32.reference: Margaret Thatcher was the daughter of a grocer from Grantham in Lincolnshire. She trained as a chemist and lawyer. She was elected as a Conservative MP in 1959 and became a cabinet minister in 1970 as the Secretary of State for Education and Science. In 1975 she was elected as Leader of the Conservative Party and so became Leader of the Opposition.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: Civil servants cannot stand for Office
Britizen 32.reference: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish Republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public Office. There are some exceptions, including: members of the armed forces; civil servants; people found guilty of certain criminal offences.

Britizen 32.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 32.question: How often is the electoral register updated?
Britizen 32.reference: The electoral register is updated every year in September or October. An electoral registration form is sent to every household and this has to be completed and returned with the names of everyone who is resident in the household and eligible to vote.

Britizen 32.question: Women over 30 were allowed to vote as a result of their contribution towards the war effort. Which war was it?
Britizen 32.reference: In 1918, women over the age of 30 were given voting rights and the right to stand for Parliament, partly in recognition of the contribution women made to the war effort during the First World War. Shortly before Emmeline's death in 1928, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

Britizen 32.question: Admiral Nelson died in which battle?
Britizen 32.reference: Britain's navy fought against combined French and Spanish fleets, winning the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805. Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet at Trafalgar and was killed in the battle.

Britizen 32.question: St Augustine led missionaries from Rome, who spread Christianity in the south. Who did he become?
Britizen 32.reference: St Augustine led missionaries from Rome, who spread Christianity in the south. St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury (see the Archbishop of Canterbury and the Church in Britain today).

Britizen 33.question: When did the first Christian communities appear in Britain?
Britizen 33.reference: It was during the 3rd and 4th centuries AD that the first Christian communities began to appear in Britain.

Britizen 33.question: When do pubs usually open?
Britizen 33.reference: Public Houses, also known as Pubs, usually open at 11:00 AM, 12:00 PM on Sundays.

Britizen 33.question: Who led the Scottish Army during the Battle of Bannockburn?
Britizen 33.reference: In Scotland, the English kings were less successful. In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn, and Scotland remained unconquered by the English.

Britizen 33.question: When did the development of a free press happen?
Britizen 33.reference: From 1695, newspapers were allowed to operate without a government licence. Increasing numbers of newspapers began to be published.

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: Which of the Houses is more important in today's Britain?
Britizen 33.reference: The House of Commons is regarded as the more important of the two chambers in Parliament because its members are democratically elected.

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: What does the UN aim to do?
Britizen 33.reference: The UK is part of the United Nations (UN), an international organisation with more than 190 countries as members. The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security.

Britizen 33.question: What was the population of the UK in 1801?
Britizen 33.reference: The population of the UK was just 8 million in 1801.

Britizen 33.question: When did the Industrial Revolution take place?
Britizen 33.reference: The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries.

Britizen 33.question: When were films first shown publicly in the UK?
Britizen 33.reference: Films were first shown publicly in the UK in 1896 and film screenings very quickly became popular

Britizen 33.question: Where did the Jutes come from?
Britizen 33.reference: Britain was invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons. The languages they spoke are the basis of modern-day English.

Britizen 33.question: Which TWO buildings were built during the Middle Ages?
Britizen 33.reference: The Middle Ages also saw a change in the type of buildings in Britain. Castles were built in many places in Britain and Ireland, partly for defence. Today many are in ruins, although some, such as Windsor and Edinburgh, are still in use. Great cathedrals - for example, Lincoln Cathedral - were also built, and many of these are still used for worship.

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: It is an offence not to have an MOT certificate if your vehicle is more than _____ years old
Britizen 33.reference: If your vehicle is over three years old, you must take it for a Ministry of Transport (MOT) test every year. It is an offence not to have an MOT certificate if your vehicle is more than three years old.

Britizen 33.question: The Mousetrap','Jesus Christ Superstar' and 'Evita' are examples of British what?
Britizen 33.reference: They are examples of theatre plays.

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: How old do you have to be to stand for election as an MP?
Britizen 33.reference: Anyone aged 18 or over can stand for election as an MP but they are unlikely to win unless they have been nominated to represent one of the major political parties

Britizen 33.question: During the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, the English government encouraged Scottish and English Protestants to settle in which Irish province?
Britizen 33.reference: During the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, many people in Ireland opposed rule by the Protestant government in England. There were a number of rebellions. The English government encouraged Scottish and English Protestants to settle in Ulster, the northern province of Ireland, taking over the land from Catholic landholders.

Britizen 33.question: The UK is a diverse and dynamic nation which welcomes those who want to make a positive contribution to the society
Britizen 33.reference: The UK is a diverse and welcoming country. It welcomes those who want to make a positive contribution to the society

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: It is a criminal offence to owe money to someone and you might be taken to court
Britizen 33.reference: Owing money is a civil offence

Britizen 33.question: The Boer War took place in which country of the British Empire?
Britizen 33.reference: The Boer War of 1899 to 1902 made the discussions about the future of the Empire more urgent. The British went to war in South Africa with settlers from the Netherlands called the Boers.

Britizen 33.question: Which Battle is commemorated in the Bayeux Tapestry?
Britizen 33.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror. The battle is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

Britizen 33.question: A treaty gave independence to the south of Ireland in 1921
Britizen 33.reference: In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries. The six counties in the north which were mainly Protestant remained part of the UK under the name Northern Ireland. The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State. It had its own government and became a republic in 1949.

Britizen 33.question: Who was the British scientist who was awarded the Nobel prize for the discovery of the structure of the DNA molecule?
Britizen 33.reference: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. This discovery contributed to many scientific advances, particularly in medicine and fighting crime. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize for this discovery, was British.

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: Members of the European Union can vote in all public UK elections
Britizen 33.reference: Prior to Brexit, adult citizens of other EU states who are resident in the UK could vote in all elections except General Elections. As of 2020, EU citizens' local voting rights have also changed and are now set on individual, per-country basis.

Britizen 33.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 33.question: The system of government in the UK is a parliamentary democracy
Britizen 33.reference: The system of government in the UK is a parliamentary democracy. The UK is divided into parliamentary constituencies. Voters in each constituency elect their member of Parliament (MP) in a General Election. All of the elected MPs from the House of Commons. Most MPs belong to a political party, and the party with the majority of MPs forms the government. If one party does not get a majority, two parties can join together to form a coalition.

Britizen 33.question: The King Charles III's eldest son, Prince William, is also known as?
Britizen 33.reference: Queen Elizabeth II reigned since her father's death in 1952, (she celebrated her Diamond Jubilee i.e. 60 years as queen) until she passed away in 2022. She was married to Prince Philip, the Duke of Edinburgh. Her eldest son, King Charles III (previously known as the Prince of Wales) became the sovereign monarch in 2022. His eldest son, heir to the throne, Prince William is the new Prince of Wales.

Britizen 34.question: As per 2021 census, what percentage of the England and Wales population identified as Sikh?
Britizen 34.reference: In the 2021 England and Wales census, around 525,000 people from the sampled population of 60 million identified as Sikh. 46% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (6.5%), Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), 0.5% of people followed another religion and 37% stated not to follow any religion.

Britizen 34.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 34.question: How often do the ministers meet to discuss important issues?
Britizen 34.reference: Ministers form the cabinet, a committee which usually meets weekly and makes important decisions about government policy. Many of these decisions have to be debated or approved by Parliament.

Britizen 34.question: Where can you get help if you can't afford to pay a vet?
Britizen 34.reference: Vaccinations and medical treatment for animals are available from veterinary surgeons (vets). There are charities which may help people who cannot afford to pay a vet.

Britizen 34.question: Which of these are British Overseas Territories?
Britizen 34.reference: There are also several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands. They are also linked to the UK but are not a part of it.

answers.text: St Helena and the Isle of Man
The Channel Islands and the Isle of Man

Britizen 34.question: Which country invented Cricket?
Britizen 34.reference: Cricket originated in England and is now played in many countries. Games can last up to five days but still result in a draw! The most famous Cricket competition is the Ashes, which is a series of Test matches played between England and Australia.

Britizen 34.question: What celebrates the end of Ramadan?
Britizen 34.reference: Eid al-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month. They thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast.

Britizen 34.question: How many Russian and Polish Jews came to Britain between 1870-1914?
Britizen 34.reference: People continued to come to Britain from other parts of the world. For example, between 1870 and 1914, around 120,000 Russian and Polish Jews came to Britain to escape persecution.

Britizen 34.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 34.question: Everything found in Hansard is available online
Britizen 34.reference: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard. Written reports can be found in large libraries and online.

Britizen 34.question: On average, girls leave school with better qualifications than boys
Britizen 34.reference: The Statement is true

Britizen 34.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 34.question: Where can you get details about the small claims procedure?
Britizen 34.reference: You can get details about the small claims procedure from your local County Court or Sheriff Court.

Britizen 34.question: Who was John Constable?
Britizen 34.reference: John Constable (1776-1837) was a landscape painter most famous for his works of Dedham Vale on the Suffolk, Essex border in the east of England.

answers.text: An important contributor to the 'pop art' movement of the 1960s
A landscape painter, most famous for his works of Dedham Vale on the Suffolk-Essex border in the East of England
A very successful Northern Irish portrait painter
A Welsh artist, best known for his engravings and stained glass

Britizen 34.question: What food is traditionally associated with Wales?
Britizen 34.reference: Welsh cakes - a traditional Welsh snack made from flour, dried fruits and spices, and served either hot or cold.

answers.text: Roast beef, which is served with potatoes, vegetables, Yorkshire puddings

Britizen 34.question: Who of the following is associated with the Bonfire Night?
Britizen 34.reference: Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays. The origin of this celebration was an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

Britizen 34.question: The Domesday Book gives a picture of society in England just after the Norman Conquest
Britizen 34.reference: William the Conqueror (the Duke of Normandy) sent people all over England to draw up lists of all the towns and villages. The people who lived there, who owned the land and what animals they owned were also listed. This was called the Domesday Book. It still exists today and gives a picture of society in England just after the Norman Conquest.

Britizen 34.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 34.question: Who invented the Jet Engine?
Britizen 34.reference: The Jet Engine was developed in Britain in the 1930s by Sir Frank Whittle (1907-96), a British Royal Air Force engineer Officer.

Britizen 34.question: What religion did Queen Elizabeth I practise?
Britizen 34.reference: Queen Elizabeth I was a Protestant. She re-established the Church of England as the official Church in England. Everyone had to attend their local church and there were laws about the type of religious services and the prayers which could be said, but Elizabeth did not ask about people's real beliefs. She succeeded in finding a balance between the views of Catholics and the more extreme Protestants.

Britizen 34.question: Who will remain the longest reigning monarch until 9 September 2015?
Britizen 34.reference: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18. She reigned until 1901, almost 64 years. Her reign is known as the Victorian Age. On September 9 2015, Queen Elizabeth II will pass the record set by her great-great-grandmother Queen Victoria.

Britizen 34.question: Which TWO developments are associated with the 'Swinging Sixties'?
Britizen 34.reference: The Swinging Sixties was also a time when social laws were liberalised, for example in relation to divorce and to abortion in England, Wales and Scotland. The position of women in the workplace also improved. It was quite common at the time for employers to ask women to leave their jobs when they got married, but Parliament passed new laws giving women the right to equal pay and made it illegal for employers to discriminate against women because of their gender.

Britizen 34.question: Which of these is a British invention/discovery?
Britizen 34.reference: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. This discovery contributed to many scientific advances, particularly in medicine and fighting crime. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize for this discovery, was British.

answers.text: The structure of the DNA molecule

Britizen 34.question: When are Pantomimes usually produced?
Britizen 34.reference: One British tradition is the pantomime. Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time. They are based on fairy stories and are light-hearted plays with music and comedy, enjoyed by family audiences. One of the traditional characters is the Dame, a woman played by a man. There is often also a pantomime horse or cow played by two actors in the same costume.

Britizen 34.question: Eid ul Adha remembers that the prophet Ibrahim was willing to sacrifice his son when God ordered him to
Britizen 34.reference: Eid ul Adha remembers that the prophet Ibrahim was willing to sacrifice his son when God ordered him to. It reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God. Many Muslims sacrifice an animal to eat during this festival. In Britain this has to be done in a slaughterhouse.

Britizen 34.question: What was the longest British War with France called?
Britizen 34.reference: English kings also fought a long war with France, called the Hundred Years War (even though it actually lasted 116 years). One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V's vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

Britizen 34.question: William Orange was asked by Protestants to invade England. This event was later known as what?
Britizen 34.reference: James II's elder daughter, Mary, was married to her cousin William of Orange, the Protestant ruler of the Netherlands. In 1688, important Protestants in England asked William to invade England and proclaim himself king. This event was later called the 'Glorious Revolution' because there was no fighting in England and because it guaranteed the power of Parliament.

Britizen 35.question: Where was Florence Nightingale born?
Britizen 35.reference: Florence Nightingale was born in Italy to English parents in 1820.

Britizen 35.question: Which of these gardens is located in Wales?
Britizen 35.reference: Bodnant Garden is in Wales.

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: Who is responsible for subjects such as education, health and defence?
Britizen 35.reference: Secretaries of State are responsible for subjects such as education, health and defence.

Britizen 35.question: What UK landmark was voted as Britain's favourite view?
Britizen 35.reference: The Lake District is England's largest national park. It covers 885 square miles (2,292 square kilometres). It is famous for its lakes and mountains and is very popular with climbers, walkers and sailors. The biggest stretch of water is Windermere. In 2007, television viewers voted Wastwater as Britain's favourite view.

Britizen 35.question: How many people lost their lives in the decades after 1969 due to violence in Northern Ireland?
Britizen 35.reference: Some 3,000 people lost their lives in the decades after 1969 in the violence in Northern Ireland.

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: What do the National Insurance Contributions fund?
Britizen 35.reference: Almost everybody in the UK who is in paid work, including self-employed people, must pay National Insurance Contributions. The money raised from National Insurance Contributions is used to pay for state benefits and services such as the state retirement pension and the National Health Service (NHS).

Britizen 35.question: How many people lost their lives in Northern Ireland in the decades after 1969?
Britizen 35.reference: The 1970s were also a time of serious unrest in Northern Ireland. In 1972, the Northern Ireland Parliament was suspended and Northern Ireland was directly ruled by the UK government. Some 3,000 people lost their lives in the decades after 1969 in the violence in Northern Ireland.

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: What method is used to elect UK MPs?
Britizen 35.reference: MPs are elected through a system called 'first past the post'. In each constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected. The government is usually formed by the party that wins the majority of constituencies. If no party wins a majority, two parties may join together to form a coalition.

Britizen 35.question: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC but this was unsuccessful. For how many years did Britain remain separate from the Roman Empire?
Britizen 35.reference: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC. This was unsuccessful and for nearly 100 years Britain remained separate from the Roman Empire. In AD 43 the Emperor Claudius led the Roman army in a new invasion. This time, there was resistance from some of the British tribes but the Romans were successful in occupying almost all of Britain.

Britizen 35.question: Hanukkah is celebrated to remember the Jews' struggle for religious freedom and it is in November or December for how many days?
Britizen 35.reference: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days. It is to remember the Jews' struggle for religious freedom. On each day of the festival a candle is lit on a stand of eight candles (called a menorah) to remember the story of the festival, where oil that should have lasted only a day did so for eight.

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: The leader of the Opposition appoints senior opposition MPs to be what?
Britizen 35.reference: The leader of the Opposition also appoints senior opposition MPs to be 'shadow ministers'. They form the shadow cabinet and their role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies.

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: Which TWO of these countries are currently in the EU?
Britizen 35.reference: Following a referendum held in the UK on 23 June 2016, the UK left the EU on 31 January 2020. France are Poland are still part of the EU.

Britizen 35.question: Christmas Day celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ
Britizen 35.reference: Christmas Day, 25 December, celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ. It is a public holiday. Many Christians go to church on Christmas Eve (24 December) or on Christmas Day itself.

Britizen 35.question: What is the second biggest country in the UK?
Britizen 35.reference: Scotland has the second highest population and area.

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: The EU was set up by 6 western countries who signed the Treaty of Rome on which date?
Britizen 35.reference: The European Union (EU), originally called the European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957. The UK originally decided not to join this group but it became a member in 1973.

Britizen 35.question: When Queen Anne died, a German, George of Hanover, became the next King of England
Britizen 35.reference: When Queen Anne died in 1714, Parliament chose a German, George I, to be the next King, because he was Anne's nearest Protestant relative. An attempt by Scottish Jacobites to put James II's son on the throne instead was quickly defeated.

Britizen 35.question: Which of these TWO lines from Shakespeare's plays and poems are often still quoted?
Britizen 35.reference: Lines from Shakespeare's plays and poems which are often still quoted include: Once more unto the breach (Henry V); To be or not to be (Hamlet); A rose by any other name (Romeo and Juliet); All the world's a stage (As You Like It); The darling buds of May (Sonnet 18 'Shall I Compare Thee To a Summer's Day).

answers.text: To be or not to be

Britizen 35.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 35.question: People who are self-employed need to pay National Insurance Contributions themselves
Britizen 35.reference: Almost everybody in the UK who is in paid work, including self-employed people, must pay National Insurance Contributions.

Britizen 35.question: The Swinging Sixties was a period of new ideas about politics, philosophy and science
Britizen 35.reference: he decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as 'the Swinging Sixties'. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music. Two well-known pop music groups at the time were The Beatles and The Rolling Stones. People started to become better off and many bought cars and other consumer goods. It was also a time when social laws were liberalised, for example in relation to divorce and to abortion in England, Wales and Scotland.

Britizen 35.question: In year 1066, William the Conqueror invaded England in which battle?
Britizen 35.reference: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle. William became king of England and is known as William the Conqueror. The battle is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

Britizen 35.question: Which of the following discoveries contributed to the progress of the Industrial Revolution?
Britizen 35.reference: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called 'the Enlightenment'. Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish. Adam Smith developed ideas about economics which are still referred to today. David Hume's ideas about human nature continue to influence philosophers. Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt's work on steam power, helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

Britizen 36.question: What is the Church of England known as in Scotland and the US?
Britizen 36.reference: The official Church of the state is the Church of England is called the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States. It is a Protestant Church.

Britizen 36.question: Which countries are in Great Britain?
Britizen 36.reference: Great Britain consists of 3 countries: England, Wales and Scotland.

Britizen 36.question: What language did the Iron Age people speak?
Britizen 36.reference: Their language was part of the Celtic language family.

Britizen 36.question: When did the UK join the EEC?
Britizen 36.reference: West Germany, France, Belgium, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands formed the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957. At first the UK did not wish to join the EEC but it eventually did so in 1973.

Britizen 36.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 36.question: Who is able to change the Prime Minister during his/her term?
Britizen 36.reference: The Prime Minister can be changed if the MPs in the governing party decide to do so, or if he or she wishes to resign. The Prime Minister usually resigns if his or her party loses a General Election.

Britizen 36.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 36.question: What can schools do to raise money?
Britizen 36.reference: Many schools organise events to raise money for extra equipment or out-of-school activities. Activities might include book sales, toy sales or bringing food to sell.

answers.text: Ask the local council for extra funding

Britizen 36.question: Which of Henry VIII's wives were executed?
Britizen 36.reference: Anne Boleyn was English. She and Henry had one daughter, Elizabeth. Anne was unpopular in the country and was accused of taking lovers. She was executed at the Tower of London. Catherine was a cousin of Anne Boleyn. She was also accused of taking lovers and executed.

answers.text: Catherine Parr and Anne of Cleves
Catherine of Aragon and Anne Boleyn
Anne of Cleves and Jane Seymour

Britizen 36.question: Who led a team to clone a mammal for the first time?
Britizen 36.reference: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep. This has led to further research into the possible use of cloning to preserve endangered species and for medical purposes.

answers.text: Sir Ian Wilmut and Keith Campbell

Britizen 36.question: Where was William Shakespeare born?
Britizen 36.reference: Shakespeare (1564-1616) was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England.

Britizen 36.question: What tools did the Bronze age people introduce?
Britizen 36.reference: The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished metalworkers who made many beautiful objects in bronze and gold, including tools, ornaments and weapons.

Britizen 36.question: How many colonies were granted independence in 1947?
Britizen 36.reference: Another aspect of change was self-government for former colonies. In 1947, independence was granted to nine countries, including India, Pakistan and Ceylon (now Sri Lanka). Other colonies in Africa, the Caribbean and the Pacific achieved independence over the next 20 years.

Britizen 36.question: The Battle of Hastings is commemorated in a piece of embroidery , known as the Bayeux Tapestry
Britizen 36.reference: The battle of Hastings is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux Tapestry, which can still be seen in France today.

Britizen 36.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 36.question: What are TWO responsibilities of members of Parliament (MPs)?
Britizen 36.reference: MPs have a number of different responsibilities. They:; represent everyone in their constituency; ; help to create new laws; ; scrutinise and comment on what the government is doing; ; debate important national issues;

answers.text: Represent only those who voted for them
They represent everyone in their constituency
Scrutinise and comment on what the government is doing

Britizen 36.question: Where was Anne Boleyn, the wife of Henry VIII who was accused of taking lovers, executed?
Britizen 36.reference: Anne Boleyn was English. She and Henry had one daughter, Elizabeth. Anne was unpopular in the country and was accused of taking lovers. She was executed at the Tower of London.

Britizen 36.question: What is the percentage of Sikh living in the UK?
Britizen 36.reference: In the 2021 England and Wales census, around 525,000 (0.9%) people from the sampled population of 60 million identified as Sikh. 46% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (6.5%), Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), 0.5% of people followed another religion and 37% stated not to follow any religion.

Britizen 36.question: Where can you find Crown Jewels?
Britizen 36.reference: The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror after he became king in 1066. Tours are given by the Yeoman Warders, also known as Beefeaters, who tell visitors about the building's history. People can also see the Crown Jewels there.

Britizen 36.question: How is the Queen Elizabeth II's husband, Prince Philip, often called?
Britizen 36.reference: Queen Elizabeth II reigned since her father's death in 1952, in 2012 she celebrated her Diamond Jubilee (60 years as queen) until she passed away in 2022. She was married to Prince Philip, the Duke of Edinburgh. Her eldest son, Prince Charles (the Prince of Wales) became the sovereign monarch in 2022.

Britizen 36.question: What name is given to the official flag that represents the union between England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland?
Britizen 36.reference: One symbol of the union between England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland was a new version of the official flag, the Union Flag. This is often called the Union Jack.

Britizen 36.question: Which TWO describe the Wimbledon Championships?
Britizen 36.reference: Modern tennis evolved in England in the late 19th century. The first tennis club was founded in Leamington Spa in 1872. The most famous tournament hosted in Britain is The Wimbledon Championships, which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club. It is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only 'Grand Slam' event played on grass.

answers.text: Only 'Grand Slam' event played on grass
Oldest tennis tournament in the world
Only 'Major' tournament held outside the US
Only tennis tournament that Andy Murray won

Britizen 36.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 36.question: What is a good way to support your local community?
Britizen 36.reference: A good way to support your local community is to shop for products locally where you can. This will help businesses and farmers in your area and in Britain. It will also reduce your carbon footprint, because the products you buy will not have had to travel as far.

answers.text: Shop for products locally where you can

Britizen 36.question: What is the geographic position of the UK in Europe?
Britizen 36.reference: The UK is located in the North West of Europe.

Britizen 37.question: Which charity works to preserve important buildings?
Britizen 37.reference: Many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open by the National Trust. It aims to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK. The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers. There are now more than 61,000 volunteers helping to keep the organisation running.

Britizen 37.question: What is the capital of England?
Britizen 37.reference: London is the capital city of England and the United Kingdom.

Britizen 37.question: Who became Prime Minister during WWII?
Britizen 37.reference: Hitler invaded Poland and took control of Belgium and the Netherlands. At this time of national crisis, Winston Churchill became Prime Minister.

Britizen 37.question: When is Boxing Day?
Britizen 37.reference: Boxing Day (26th of December) is the day after Christmas Day (25th of December) and is a public holiday.

Britizen 37.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 37.question: Where are the most serious civil cases heard in England and Wales?
Britizen 37.reference: More serious civil cases - for example, when a large amount of compensation is being claimed - are dealt with in the High Court in England, Wales and Northern Ireland.

Britizen 37.question: Where is the Millennium Stadium located?
Britizen 37.reference: Sporting events take place at major stadiums such as Wembley Stadium in London and the Millennium Stadium in Cardiff.

Britizen 37.question: What event is known as the evacuation of 300,000 people from the beaches?
Britizen 37.reference: Many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats from Britain helped the Navy to rescue more than 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase 'the Dunkirk spirit'.

Britizen 37.question: Which river banks the London eye?
Britizen 37.reference: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of the River Thames and is a Ferris wheel that is 443 feet (135 metres) tall. It was originally built as part of the UK's celebration of the new millennium and continues to be an important part of New Year celebrations.

Britizen 37.question: How many years did Charles I try to rule without Parliament?
Britizen 37.reference: For 11 years, Charles I found ways in which to raise money without Parliament's approval but eventually trouble in Scotland meant that he had to recall Parliament.

Britizen 37.question: When did the first Christian communities begin to appear in Britain?
Britizen 37.reference: It was during the 3rd and 4th centuries AD that the first Christian communities began to appear in Britain.

Britizen 37.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 37.question: Where are the most serious criminal cases in Scotland heard at?
Britizen 37.reference: The most serious cases in Scotland, such as murder, are heard at a High Court with a judge and jury.

Britizen 37.question: Clans lost a lot of their power and influence after which event?
Britizen 37.reference: The clans lost a lot of their power and influence after the Battle of Culloden (1746). Chieftains became landlords if they had the favour of the English king, and clansmen became tenants who had to pay for the land they used.

Britizen 37.question: Which of the following was written by Lord Byron?
Britizen 37.reference: She Walks in Beauty is a short poem by Lord Byron

Britizen 37.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 37.question: Who gets appointed by local authorities to be the ceremonial leader of the council?
Britizen 37.reference: Many local authorities appoint a mayor, who is the ceremonial leader of the council. In some towns, a mayor is elected to be the effective leader of the administration.

Britizen 37.question: Which country (or colony) did NOT gain its independence from the British Empire in 1947?
Britizen 37.reference: In 1947, independence was granted to nine countries, including India, Pakistan and Ceylon (now Sri Lanka). Other colonies in Africa, the Caribbean (e.g. The British West Indies) and the Pacific achieved independence over the next 20 years.

Britizen 37.question: What language did the peasants speak after the Norman conquest?
Britizen 37.reference: The Middle Ages saw the development of a national culture and identity. After the Norman Conquest, the king and his noblemen had spoken Norman French and the peasants had continued to speak Anglo-Saxon. Gradually these two languages combined to become one English language.

Britizen 37.question: EastEnders and Coronation Street are popular soap operas.
Britizen 37.reference: British television shows a wide variety of programmes. Popular programmes include regular soap operas such as Coronation Street and EastEnders.

Britizen 37.question: Which TWO are used in making an Ulster fry?
Britizen 37.reference: Ulster fry - a fried meal with bacon, eggs, sausage, black pudding, white pudding, tomatoes, mushrooms, soda bread and potato bread.

Britizen 37.question: In 1998, what was the estimated population of the UK?
Britizen 37.reference: (1951, 50 million), (1998, 57 million), (2005, Just under 60 million), (2010, Just over 62 million)

Britizen 37.question: Mary Stuart, often now called Mary Queen of Scots was a Protestant
Britizen 37.reference: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called 'Mary, Queen of Scots') was a Catholic. She was only a week old when her father died and she became queen

Britizen 37.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 37.question: Which TWO are some of the key roles of Governors and school boards?
Britizen 37.reference: Governors and school boards have an important part to play in raising school standards. They have three key roles: setting the strategic direction of the school; ensuring accountability; monitoring and evaluating school performance.

Britizen 37.question: Which of the following flowers represents England?
Britizen 37.reference: The national flower of England is the Rose.

Britizen 38.question: Which of these is a famous classical music event in the UK?
Britizen 38.reference: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of classical music (orchestral or solo instrumental and nowadays may feature non-classical music) that takes place in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London.

Britizen 38.question: When did the Enlightenment happen?
Britizen 38.reference: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called 'the Enlightenment'.

Britizen 38.question: What is the period after the Norman Conquest up until 1485 called?
Britizen 38.reference: The period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485 is called the Middle Ages or the medieval period. It was a time of almost constant war.

Britizen 38.question: Which flower is associated with Northern Ireland?
Britizen 38.reference: A shamrock is associated with Northern Ireland. A rose is associated with England, a thistle with Scotland and a daffodil with Wales.

Britizen 38.question: Where did the canal builders come from in the middle ages?
Britizen 38.reference: People came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France, engineers from Germany, glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

Britizen 38.question: What percentage of the total population lives in England?
Britizen 38.reference: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8% and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

Britizen 38.question: Who wrote music for King George I?
Britizen 38.reference: The German-born composer George Frideric Handel (1695-1759) spent many years in the UK and became a British citizen in 1727. He wrote the Water Music for King George I and Music for the Royal Fireworks for his son, George II.

Britizen 38.question: When did Britain recognise the American colonies' independence?
Britizen 38.reference: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments. The colonists eventually defeated the British army and Britain recognised the colonies' independence in 1783.

Britizen 38.question: Which TWO are located in Scotland?
Britizen 38.reference: Loch Lomond and the Edinburgh Castle are located in Scotland.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: How many volunteers is The National Trust run by?
Britizen 38.reference: The National Trust was set up by just 3 volunteers. Now over 61,000 people help to run it.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: Many schools organise events to raise money for extra equipment or out-of-school activities
Britizen 38.reference: Many schools organise events to raise money for extra equipment or out-of-school activities. Activities might include book sales, toy sales or bringing food to sell.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: Many of the painters working in Britain in the 16th and 17th centuries were from abroad
Britizen 38.reference: Many of the painters working in Britain in the 16th and 17th centuries were from abroad - for example, Hans Holbein and Sir Anthony Van Dyck.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: What will happen to any driver who has either taken more than the acceptable quantity of alcohol or refused to take the test?
Britizen 38.reference: If you are found driving while exceeding the alcohol limit you will be arrested.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: Women over 21 were allowed to vote for the first time in 1969
Britizen 38.reference: The voting franchise was also extended to women over 30, and then in 1928 to men and women over 21. In 1969, the voting age was reduced to 18 for men and women.

Britizen 38.question: When did the Wars of the Roses end with the Battle of Bosworth?
Britizen 38.reference: The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.

Britizen 38.question: What kind of battle was the Battle of Britain (1940)?
Britizen 38.reference: The Germans waged an air campaign against Britain, but the British resisted with their fighter planes and eventually won the crucial aerial battle against the Germans, called 'the Battle of Britain', in the summer of 1940. The most important planes used by the Royal Air Force in the Battle of Britain were the Spitfire and the Hurricane - which were designed and built in Britain.

Britizen 38.question: Mince pies are traditionally eaten on which day?
Britizen 38.reference: Christmas is celebrated in a traditional way. People usually spend the day at home and eat a special meal, which often includes roast turkey, Christmas pudding and mince pies.

Britizen 38.question: David Hockney was an important contributor to the 'pop art' movement of the 1960s
Britizen 38.reference: David Hockney (1937-) was an important contributor to the 'pop art' movement of the 1960s and continues to be influential today.

Britizen 38.question: Which TWO are notable British artists?
Britizen 38.reference: Joseph Turner (1775-1851) was an influential landscape painter in a modern style. He is considered the artist who raised the profile of landscape painting. John Constable (1776-1837) was a landscape painter most famous for his works of Dedham Vale on the Suffolk, Essex border in the east of England.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: What are elected members of the National Assembly of Wales known as?
Britizen 38.reference: In Wales the elected members, known as AMs, meet in the Welsh Assembly in the Senedd in Cardiff Bay

Britizen 38.question: In 1745, who was supported by clansmen from the Scottish highlands and raised an army?
Britizen 38.reference: In 1745 there was another attempt to put a Stuart king back on the throne in place of George I's son, George II. Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie), the grandson of James II, landed in Scotland. He was supported by clansmen from the Scottish highlands and raised an army.
