Charles initially had some successes but was defeated by George II's army at the Battle of Culloden in 1746. Charles escaped back to Europe.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: Northern Ireland has its own banknotes which are valid everywhere in the UK
Britizen 38.reference: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shops and businesses do not have to accept them.

Britizen 38.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 38.question: Wales and Scotland use a system called 'individual registration'.All those entitled to vote must complete their own registration forms
Britizen 38.reference: In Northern Ireland a system called 'individual registration' operates and all those entitled to vote must complete their own registration form. Once registered, people stay on the register provided their personal details do not change.

Britizen 39.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 39.question: What is the monarch's ceremonial role?
Britizen 39.reference: The Monarch has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year.

answers.text: Travelling abroad for banquets and to negotiate with foreign dignitaries

Britizen 39.question: Under which king did the people unite to defeat the Vikings?
Britizen 39.reference: The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England united under King Alfred the Great, who defeated the Vikings.

Britizen 39.question: What did the first farmers build in Britain?
Britizen 39.reference: The first farmers built houses, tombs and monuments on the land.

Britizen 39.question: Which operas were written by Gilbert and Sullivan?
Britizen 39.reference: Gilbert and Sullivan wrote comic operas, often making fun of popular culture and politics. These operas include HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado.

Britizen 39.question: What were the working conditions like during the Industrial Revolution?
Britizen 39.reference: Working conditions during the Industrial Revolution were very poor. There were no laws to protect employees, who were often forced to work long hours in dangerous situations. Children also worked and were treated in the same way as adults.

Britizen 39.question: Which of these is not a valid bank note?
Britizen 39.reference: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £). There are 100 pence in a pound. The denominations (values) of currency are - coins: 1p, 2p, 5p, 10p, 20p, 50p, £1 and £2; notes: £5, £10, £20, £50.

Britizen 39.question: When were the last Welsh rebellions defeated?
Britizen 39.reference: By the middle of the 15th century the last Welsh rebellions had been defeated. English laws and the English language were introduced.

Britizen 39.question: The Glorious Revolution was called the 'Glorious revolution' because there was no fighting in England and it guaranteed the power of Parliament
Britizen 39.reference: The Glorious Revolution was called the 'Glorious revolution' because there was no fighting in England and it guaranteed the power of Parliament

Britizen 39.question: Which TWO are compulsory in the UK if you own an old car?
Britizen 39.reference: It is an offence not to have an MOT certificate if your vehicle is more than three years old or without car insurance.

Britizen 39.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 39.question: Which of these is a charity working with animals?
Britizen 39.reference: People's Dispensary for Sick Animals (PDSA)is the charity that works with animals

Britizen 39.question: Which of these names was Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn's child's name?
Britizen 39.reference: Anne Boleyn was English. She and Henry had one daughter, Elizabeth. Anne was unpopular in the country and was accused of taking lovers. She was executed at the Tower of London.

Britizen 39.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 39.question: Who makes a speech which summarises the government's policies for the year ahead?
Britizen 39.reference: The Monarch (King Charles III at the time of writing) has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year. On this occasion the Monarch makes a speech which summarises the government's policies for the year ahead. All Acts of Parliament are made in his name.

Britizen 39.question: Until what time do people play jokes on one another on April Fool's Day?
Britizen 39.reference: April Fool's Day, 1 April, is a day when people play jokes on each other until midday. The television and newspapers often have stories that are April Fool jokes.

Britizen 39.question: In which UK country can you find the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe?
Britizen 39.reference: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

Britizen 39.question: In the middle of the 19th century the potato crop failed, and Ireland suffered a famine
Britizen 39.reference: In the middle of the 19th century the potato crop failed, and Ireland suffered a famine. A million people died from disease and starvation. Another million and a half left Ireland.

Britizen 39.question: What are the parliaments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland also known as?
Britizen 39.reference: Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them. These powers are also known as devolved administrations.

Britizen 39.question: Select TWO famous Paralympians
Britizen 39.reference: Baroness Tanni-Grey Thompson (1969-) is an athlete who uses a wheelchair and won 16 Paralympic medals, including 11 gold medals, in races over five Paralympic Games. She won the London Marathon six times and broke a total of 30 world records. Ellie Simmonds (1994-) is a Paralympian who won gold medals for swimming at the 2008 and 2012 Paralympic Games and holds a number of world records. She was the youngest member of the British team at the 2008 Games.

Britizen 39.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 39.question: What does the abbreviation PTA stand for?
Britizen 39.reference: Sometimes school events are organised by parent-teacher associations (PTAs)

Britizen 39.question: Which TWO of these are associated with Sir Francis Drake?
Britizen 39.reference: Sir Francis Drake, one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada, was one of the founders of England's naval tradition. His ship, the Golden Hind, was one of the first to sail right around ('circumnavigate') the world.

Britizen 39.question: Which TWO major welfare changes were introduced between 1945 and 1950?
Britizen 39.reference: In 1948, Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS). A national system of benefits was also introduced to provide 'social security', so that the population would be protected from the 'cradle to the grave'.

answers.text: A social security system for everyone

Britizen 39.question: Which comedy double act by Nick Park won many Oscars?
Britizen 39.reference: Nick Park, who has won four Oscars for his animated films, including three for films featuring Wallace and Gromit.

Britizen 40.question: When is Christmas Eve?
Britizen 40.reference: Christmas Day is on the 25th of December. Christmas Eve is the day before that which is the 24th of December.

Britizen 40.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 40.question: What is the system that automatically deducts tax called?
Britizen 40.reference: This system that deducts tax automatically is called 'Pay As You Earn', also known as PAYE.

Britizen 40.question: Where did the ancestors of the first farmers come from?
Britizen 40.reference: The first farmers came to Britain 6,000 years ago. The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe.

Britizen 40.question: Where is Loch Lomond located?
Britizen 40.reference: Loch Lomond is the largest expanse of freshwater in mainland Britain and probably the best-known part of the park. It is located in Scotland.

Britizen 40.question: When were films first shown in the UK?
Britizen 40.reference: Films were first shown publicly in the UK in 1896 and film screenings very quickly became popular.

Britizen 40.question: When did the Parliament begin developing into the institution it is now?
Britizen 40.reference: In the Middle Ages, Parliament began to develop into the institution it is today. Its origins can be traced to the king's council of advisers, which included important noblemen and the leaders of the Church.

Britizen 40.question: Who set up the first formal anti-slavery campaigns?
Britizen 40.reference: The first formal anti-slavery groups were set up by the Quakers in the late 1700s, and they petitioned Parliament to ban the practice.

Britizen 40.question: What type of painter was Joseph Turner?
Britizen 40.reference: Joseph Turner (1775-1851) was an influential landscape painter in a modern style. He is considered the artist who raised the profile of landscape painting.

Britizen 40.question: When was the Magna Carta established?
Britizen 40.reference: There were few formal limits to the king's power until 1215. In that year, King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to a number of demands. The result was a charter of rights called the Magna Carta (which means the Great Charter). The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law. It protected the rights of the nobility and restricted the king's power to collect taxes or to make or change laws.

Britizen 40.question: What is the Mercury Music Prize awarded for?

The best classical song in the UK and Ireland
The best song in the UK and Ireland
The best album from the UK and Ireland

Britizen 40.question: What percentage of the total population lives in Scotland?

Britizen 40.question: When was Charles I executed?

Britizen 40.question: What kind of event is The Wimbledon Championship?

The most famous tournament hosted in Britain is The Wimbledon Championships, which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club. It is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only 'Grand Slam' event played on grass.

Britizen 40.question: Who wrote the popular operas HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and the Mikado?

Britizen 40.question: The Boer War of 1899 to 1902 established the idea that the British Empire is a force for good
Britizen 40.reference: The Boer War of 1899 to 1902 made the discussions about the future of the Empire more urgent. The British went to war in South Africa with settlers from the Netherlands called the Boers. The Boers fought fiercely and the war went on for over three years. Many died in the fighting and many more from disease. There was some public sympathy for the Boers and people began to question whether the Empire could continue.

Britizen 40.question: Who is the author of 'Charlie and the Chocolate Factory'?

Britizen 40.question: Who founded a monastery on the Island of Iona?

St Augustine led missionaries from Rome, who spread Christianity in the south. St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

Britizen 40.question: Which stories are associated with Geoffrey Chaucer?
Britizen 40.reference: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. Britizen 40.question: The BBC is controlled by the UK government

Britizen 41.reference: Between 1680 and 1720 many refugees called Huguenots came from France. They were Protestants and had been persecuted for their religion.

Britizen 41.question: When did the first professional football clubs form?
Britizen 41.reference: Football is the UK's most popular sport. It has a long history in the UK and the first professional football clubs were formed in the late 19th century.

Britizen 41.question: What is 'Snowdon'?

Britizen 41.topic: The UK government, the law and your role

Britizen 41.reference: The UK is part of the United Nations (UN), an international organisation with more than 190 countries as members. The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security. There are 15 members on the UN Security Council, which recommends action when there are international crises and threats to peace. The UK is one of five permanent members of the Security Council.

Britizen 41.question: When did the First World War end?
Britizen 41.reference: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

Britizen 41.topic: The UK government, the law and your role

answers.text: Unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace

Britizen 41.question: In what year was Germany comprehensively defeated by the Allies during the WWII?
Britizen 41.reference: Following victory on the beaches of Normandy, the allied forces pressed on through France and eventually into Germany. The Allies comprehensively defeated Germany in May 1945.

Britizen 41.reference: Throughout the 1990s, Britain played a leading role in coalition forces involved in the liberation of Kuwait, following the Iraqi invasion in 1990, and the conflict in the Former Republic of Yugoslavia. Britizen 41.reference: Anyone can make a complaint about the police by going to a police station or writing to the Chief Constable of the police force involved. Britizen 42.reference: The British constitution is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as 'unwritten'. Our most important institutions have developed over hundreds of years.

Britizen 42.reference: Vaccinations and medical treatment for animals are available from veterinary surgeons (vets). There are charities which may help people who cannot afford to pay a vet.

She continues to promote sport and tourism in Northern Ireland and was made a Dame of the British Empire in 2000 in recognition of her work.

Britizen 42.reference: The devolved administrations each have their own civil service.

Britizen 42.reference: Although Britain is one of the world's most diverse societies, there is a set of shared values and responsibilities that everyone can agree with. These values and responsibilities include: to obey and respect the law; to be aware of the rights of others and respect those rights; to treat others with fairness; to behave responsibly; to help and protect your family; to respect and preserve the environment; to treat everyone equally, regardless of sex, race, religion, age, disability, class or sexual orientation; to work to provide for yourself and your family; to help others; to vote in local and national government elections.

answers.text: To vote in local and national government elections
To go to your local pub
To help and protect your family

Britizen 43.reference: The Union Flag consists of three crosses: The cross of St George, patron saint of England, is a red cross on a white ground; The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland, is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground; The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland, is a diagonal red cross on a white ground.

Britizen 43.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 43.question: Which TWO are part of the Government?
Britizen 43.reference: In the UK, there are several different parts of government. The main ones are: the monarchy, Parliament (the House of Commons and the House of Lords), the Prime Minister, the cabinet, the judiciary (courts) ,the police, the civil service, local government.

Britizen 43.question: What was the name of plague that killed one third of the population of England in 1348?

Britizen 43.reference: Sir Francis Drake, one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada, was one of the founders of England's naval tradition. His ship, the Golden Hind, was one of the first to sail right around ('circumnavigate') the world.

Britizen 43.question: Which TWO principles are included in the European Convention on Human Rights?
Britizen 43.reference: Some of the principles included in the European Convention on Human Rights are: right to life; prohibition of torture; prohibition of slavery and forced labour; right to liberty and security; right to a fair trial; freedom of thought, conscience and religion; freedom of expression (speech).

Britizen 43.question: Rudyard Kipling was born in South Africa
Britizen 43.reference: Rudyard Kipling was born in India in 1865 and later lived in India, the UK and the USA. He wrote books and poems set in both India and the UK. Britizen 43.question: When King Charles I tried to impose a revised Prayer Book on the Presbyterian Church in Scotland, which TWO events occurred?
Britizen 43.reference: Charles I wanted the worship of the Church of England to include more ceremony and introduced a revised Prayer Book. He tried to impose this Prayer Book on the Presbyterian Church in Scotland and this led to serious unrest. A Scottish army was formed and Charles could not find the money he needed for his own army without the help of Parliament. In 1640, he recalled Parliament to ask it for funds. Many in Parliament were Puritans, a group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship. They did not agree with the king's religious views and disliked his reforms of the Church of England. Parliament refused to give the king the money he asked for, even after the Scottish army invaded England.

Parliament only gave the money to King Charles when the Scottish army invaded England
King Charles I recalled Parliament to ask it for funds to form his own army

Britizen 43.question: Which TWO are correct about Mary Stuart?
Britizen 43.reference: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called 'Mary, Queen of Scots') was a Catholic. She was only a week old when her father died and she became queen. Much of her childhood was spent in France. When she returned to Scotland, she was the centre of a power struggle between different groups. When her husband was murdered, Mary was suspected of involvement and fled to England. She gave her throne to her Protestant son, James VI of Scotland. Mary was Elizabeth I's cousin and hoped that Elizabeth might help her, but Elizabeth suspected Mary of wanting to take over the English throne, and kept her a prisoner for 20 years. Mary was eventually executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I.

answers.text: She died of the Black Death
She was only a week old when she became queen
She spent much of her childhood in France

Britizen 43.question: What celebration takes place on the 25th of December each year?

Britizen 44.question: What was an important English export in the Middle Ages?
Britizen 44.reference: English wool became a very important export in the middle ages. People came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France, engineers from Germany, glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

Britizen 44.reference: In the middle ages, the nobility, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords. Knights, who were usually smaller landowners, and wealthy people from towns and cities were elected to sit in the House of Commons.

Any man who forces a woman to have sex, excluding her husband, can be charged with rape

Britizen 44.question: In 1982, which country invaded the Falklands prompting UK to send its naval force in defense?
Britizen 44.reference: In 1982, Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands, a British overseas territory in the South Atlantic. A naval task force was sent from the UK and military action led to the recovery of the islands.

Britizen 44.question: What service does the TV licence pay for?
Britizen 44.reference: The money from TV licences is used to pay for the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC).

Britizen 44.question: Who directed the British movie the Belles of St Trinian's in 1954?
Britizen 44.reference: The Belles of St Trinian's (1954) was directed by Frank Launder

Britizen 44.question: Which TWO are 20th century British inventions?
Britizen 44.reference: Britain and France developed Concorde, the world's only supersonic passenger aircraft. It first flew in 1969 and began carrying passengers in 1976. Concorde was retired from service in 2003. The television was developed by Scotsman John Logie Baird in the 1920s. In 1932 he made the first television broadcast between London and Glasgow.

Britizen 44.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 44.question: When walking your dog in a public place, what must you ensure?
Britizen 44.reference: A lot of people in the UK have pets such as cats or dogs. They might have them for company or because they enjoy looking after them. It is against the law to treat a pet cruelly or to neglect it. All dogs in public places must wear a collar showing the name and address of the owner. The owner is responsible for keeping the dog under control and for cleaning up after the animal in a public place.

answers.text: That your dog wears a special dog coat
That your dog never strays more than 3 metres away from you
That your dog does not come into contact with other dogs
That your dog wears a collar showing the name and address of the owner

Britizen 44.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 44.question: Arranged marriages are allowed in the UK
Britizen 44.reference: A marriage should be entered into with the full and free consent of both people involved. Arranged marriages, where both parties agree to the marriage, are acceptable in the UK.

Britizen 44.question: What lead the American colonies to declare their independence?
Britizen 44.reference: By the 1760s, there were substantial British colonies in North America. The colonies were wealthy and largely in control of their own affairs. Many of the colonist families had originally gone to North America in order to have religious freedom. They were well educated and interested in ideas of liberty. The British government wanted to tax the colonies.

answers.text: The UK not providing enough health care
The UK not sending enough money
The UK wanting to tax the colonies
The UK wanting to stop the slave trade

Britizen 44.question: Where is Tate Britain and Tate Modern located?
Britizen 44.reference: Works by British and international artists are displayed in galleries across the UK. Some of the most well-known galleries are The National Gallery, Tate Britain and Tate Modern in London, the National Museum in Cardiff, and the National Gallery of Scotland in Edinburgh.

Britizen 45.question: For how long did England remain a republic?
Britizen 45.reference: Britain had been a republic for 11 years.

Britizen 45.question: Where was one of the Anglo Saxon kings buried with treasure and armour?
Britizen 45.reference: The burial place of one of the kings was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk. This king was buried with treasure and armour, all placed in a ship which was then covered by a mound of earth. Scotland and Wales remained free of Anglo Saxon rule.

Britizen 45.question: When did the Glorious Revolution happen?
Britizen 45.reference: In 1688, important Protestants in England asked William to invade England and proclaim himself king. When William reached England, there was no resistance. This event was later called the 'Glorious Revolution' because there was no fighting in England and because it guaranteed the power of Parliament, ending the threat of a monarch ruling on his or her own as he or she wished.

Britizen 45.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 45.question: What service can you use to find a lawyer or a solicitor?
Britizen 45.reference: The Citizens Advice Bureau can give you names of local solicitors and which areas of law they specialise in. You can also get this information from the Law Society.

Britizen 45.question: What is the longest distance on the mainland in the UK?
Britizen 45.reference: The longest distance on the mainland is from John O'Groats on the north coast of Scotland to Land's End in the south-west corner of England. It is about 870 miles (approximately 1,400 kilometres).

Britizen 45.question: Haggis is a traditional food of which country?
Britizen 45.reference: Scotland's food is Haggis - a sheep's stomach stuffed with offal, suet, onions and oatmeal.

Britizen 45.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 45.question: Who appoints the local Chief Constable?
Britizen 45.reference: PCCs (Police and Crime Commissioners) set local police priorities and the local policing budget. They also appoint the Chief Constable.

Britizen 45.question: Who took the title 'King of Ireland'?
Britizen 45.reference: Henry VII and Henry VIII had extended English control outside the Pale. Henry VIII took the title 'King of Ireland' and English laws were introduced.

Britizen 45.question: Who was Robert Burns?
Britizen 45.reference: Known in Scotland as 'The Bard', Robert Burns was a Scottish poet. Burns' best-known work is probably the song Auld Lang Syne, which is sung by people in the UK and other countries when they are celebrating the New Year (or Hogmanay as it is called in Scotland).

Britizen 45.topic: The UK government, the law and your role
Britizen 45.question: The Council of Europe has no power to make laws
Britizen 45.reference: The Council of Europe is separate from the EU. It has 47 member countries, including the UK, and is responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in those countries. It has no power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters, the most well-known of which is the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, usually called the European Convention on Human Rights.

Britizen 45.question: Which of the following is correct?
Britizen 45.reference: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8% and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

answers.text: Northern Ireland makes up 4% of the total population
England makes up 83% of the total population
Wales makes up 6% of the total population
Scotland makes up 8% of the total population

Britizen 45.question: What percentage of the UK population lives in England?
Britizen 45.reference: England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8% and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

Britizen 45.question: The people of which age made the first coins to be minted in Britain?
Britizen 45.reference: The people of the Iron Age had a sophisticated culture and economy. They made the first coins to be minted in Britain, some inscribed with the names of Iron Age kings. This marks the beginnings of British history.

Britizen 45.question: As per 2021 census, what is the percentage of Muslims living in England and Wales?
Britizen 45.reference: Islam is the second largest religion in the United Kingdom. The 2021 Census results released so far show a population of 3,868,133 (6.5%) in England and Wales (from a total sampled 60 million). 46% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both around 0.5%), 0.5% of people followed another religion and 37% stated not to follow any religion.

Britizen 45.question: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes valid everywhere in the UK
Britizen 45.reference: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shops and businesses do not have to accept them.

Britizen 45.question: Who governs the Church of Scotland?
Britizen 45.reference: In Scotland, the national Church is the Church of Scotland, which is a Presbyterian Church. It is governed by ministers and elders. The chairperson of the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland is the Moderator, who is appointed for one year only and often speaks on behalf of that Church.

Britizen 45.question: In what year was there a revolution in France which led to a war with Britain?
Britizen 45.reference: During the 18th century, Britain fought a number of wars with France. In 1789, there was a revolution in France and the new French government soon declared war on Britain. Napoleon, who became Emperor of France, continued the war.

Britizen 45.question: Which language was spoken by people during the Iron Age?
Britizen 45.reference: Most Iron age people were farmers, craft workers or warriors. The language they spoke was part of the Celtic language family.

Britizen 45.question: When did the English settlers first begin to colonise the eastern coast of America?
Britizen 45.reference: In Elizabeth I's time, English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America. This colonisation, particularly by people who disagreed with the religious views of the next two kings, greatly increased in the next century.

Britizen 45.question: Which of these is a Crown Dependency of the UK and not a part of it?
Britizen 45.reference: There are also several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called 'Crown dependencies'. There are also several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands. They are also linked to the UK but are not a part of it.
